review:
The Source 2 transition brought reworked visuals and physics-based smokes that genuinely move the game forward; the smokes are so pretty now you almost want to get lost in them. The sub-tick system changed how shooting feels, and thanks to it I'm absolutely certain every death of mine is perfectly fair (it's not). Still, the movement and gunplay kept that classic bone structure, so veterans feel right at home immediately.
On the anti-cheat side, things are better than before now that VACnet has improved, but you'll still bump into the occasional "suspicious" people in Premier. To be honest, I also learned to check my own crosshair placement before blaming a cheater. Despite all that, the competitive depth, economy management, and team play are still the best in the genre.
That said, I genuinely miss CS:GO. There was something about its feel, its cleaner simplicity and that old-school identity, that CS2 hasn't fully recaptured for me. CS:GO was just better in a lot of ways, and a part of me wishes I could go back.
It's free, constantly updated, and has a massive community. You can spend hours trash-talking with your friends, what more could you want? It's a game every FPS fan should try.
Highly recommend, but consider this a warning so your life doesn't get derailed.

I grew up playing this franchise. It shaped my childhood, fueled my passion for gaming, and ultimately became my career. Transitioning from a kid playing for fun to competing as a professional player gives me a unique perspective on Counter-Strike 2.Valve had a massive task in replacing CS:GO, and while the transition hasn't been flawless, CS2 successfully modernizes the greatest tactical shooter of all time.The ProsStrategic Evolution: Volumetric smoke physics completely rewrite the tactical playbook. Displacing smoke with HE grenades adds incredible depth to professional play.Visual Clarity: The Source 2 engine provides vibrant lighting and vastly improved player visibility, which is crucial for high-level competitive integrity.Audio Engineering: Sound cues are sharper and more directional, allowing for precise, instinctual audio positioning.The ConsSubtick Consistency: From a professional standpoint, the subtick system still lacks the crisp, predictable responsiveness of legacy 128-tick servers.Performance Optimization: Higher hardware demands can cause occasional frame-rate drops, requiring a high-end PC to maintain stable tournament-level performance.Final VerdictCounter-Strike 2 honors the legacy of its predecessor while building the foundation for the future of esports. It preserves the unforgiving skill ceiling I fell in love with as a kid, while delivering the modern upgrades needed for the next decade of competition. Whether you are a casual player or a aspiring pro, this is still the pinnacle of tactical shooters.

The “No Anti-Cheat” Experience
Playing CS2 in the beginning was pure anarchy in the best and worst ways:

Pros: -

Cons: The games were a coin flip between “this is the best FPS ever made” and “I’m getting blatantly aimbotted every death.” Spinning bots and rage hackers with triggerbots made casual queues almost unplayable for normal players.

I've been playing Counter-Strike for well over 20,000 hours, and at this point, it's more than just a game for me. I've played through different eras of Counter-Strike and experienced both the highs and the lows. Despite all the changes, CS2 still delivers something that no other FPS has managed to replace for me.

The gunplay is incredibly satisfying, movement feels great, and the competitive nature of the game keeps me coming back day after day. There are matches that make you want to queue again immediately, and there are matches that make you question your sanity. That's Counter-Strike.

Of course, not everything is perfect. Cheaters remain one of the biggest frustrations in the game. Running into suspicious players can ruin the experience, especially in competitive matches, and I genuinely hope Valve continues to improve anti-cheat measures because the community deserves better. Nothing kills the excitement of a close match faster than feeling like you're playing against someone with an unfair advantage.

That being said, I still believe CS2 has the best core gameplay in the genre. No battle passes, no gimmicks, no hero abilities can replace the simple formula that has kept Counter-Strike alive for decades. Every round matters, every clutch feels rewarding, and every mistake teaches you something.

I've taken breaks from the game many times, tried countless other shooters, sold skins, bought skins again, and somehow I always find myself coming back. There is just something special about Counter-Strike that is difficult to explain unless you've spent thousands of hours with it.

After more than 20,000 hours, I can honestly say that I'm probably addicted at this point, but I don't regret a single minute. Even with the problems, even with the cheaters, this is still my favorite competitive game of all time.

Valve still has work to do, but the foundation is fantastic, and I believe the game will only continue to improve.

I've played PUBG since before its full release. I have over 2,000 hours in this game.

That number means something. It means I didn't just try PUBG and move on. I stuck with it. I came back to it. I played it through bad patches, bad performance, cheaters, bugs, questionable decisions, and years of frustration. A lot of those hours were with friends, especially over the last three years after taking a long break.

And honestly? That is the part that hurts.

Was PUBG good when it first came out? Not really. It looked rough, ran badly, and often felt like it was barely holding itself together. But it was fun. It had something special.

Did it get better when it went free-to-play? A little, maybe. But not enough. The bugs stayed. The cheaters multiplied. The game kept finding new ways to disappoint you. And still, I kept playing, because despite everything, I was having fun with my friends.

Over the years, I watched PUBG slowly turn more and more into a cash grab. Season passes, collaborations, skin boxes, endless cosmetics, monetization everywhere. The soul of the game started to feel buried under all of it.

But I still played.

Not because the game deserved endless forgiveness, but because the moments with friends were worth it.

Every few matches, you run into someone who is very obviously cheating. Not “maybe they’re just good” cheating. I mean the kind where everyone in the squad goes quiet because we all know exactly what just happened. Did that make me quit? No. We complained, we sighed, we queued again.

PUBG never came to Linux either. The excuse was always something about cheaters and anti-cheat. So I installed Windows literally just to play this one game with my friends, in 2026 (or back then in 2022), when so many other games run flawlessly on Linux through Proton.

And the worst part? That anti-cheat still doesn't seem to stop the most blatant cheaters. It blocks Linux users, but somehow the people ruining matches every day are still there.

I tolerated a lot.

Cheaters? Fine, queue again.

Bugs? Annoying, but whatever.

Bad performance? Been there before.

Greedy monetization? Disappointing, but not surprising anymore.

I kept making excuses for this game because I wanted to enjoy it with my friends.

But now I can't even reliably open it.

That is where it finally breaks me.

Sometimes I get lucky and reach the lobby. We queue, we play a match, maybe we win, maybe we lose. And then the real game begins: trying to get PUBG to work again. My friends sit there waiting while I restart, relaunch, crash, fail to load, get stuck, or hope that this attempt will be the one that actually lets me back in.

Thirty minutes can disappear just trying to enter the lobby of a game that is almost ten years old.

And what makes it even worse is that this does not seem to be some brand-new, isolated issue. You can find people talking about this on Reddit, Steam, and other platforms for months now. Around three months of players reporting the same kind of problem, and still there has been no real acknowledgement.

Not a fix. Not a workaround. Not even a simple message saying, “Hey, we know something is wrong and we’re looking into it.”

Just silence.

That silence is what really kills any remaining goodwill. Bugs happen. Technical problems happen. But when a game has been around this long, and when players are clearly struggling just to launch and play it, pretending the problem does not exist feels insulting.

PUBG has been around long enough. It has made enough money. It has had enough time. And somehow, after all these years, it still feels like the developers never managed to turn it into the game it should have become.

Worse than that, they managed to break it badly enough that even someone like me, someone who forgave the cheaters, the bugs, the monetization, the lack of Linux support, years of technical issues, and even installed Windows just to keep playing (when I returned to it), finally gave up.

I didn't quit because I stopped caring.

I quit because I cared for too long, and PUBG made that feel pointless.

560 hours later, I have come to the conclusion that PUBG is less of a video game and more of a personality disorder.

I downloaded this game thinking I was about to become some tactical operator straight out of an action movie.

Instead, I became a highly trained expert in opening doors, picking up attachments for guns I don't own, and getting shot by people I never even saw.

And somehow, I love every second of it.

PUBG is one of the few games where you can spend 20 minutes looting like a raccoon with anxiety, find your dream M416 setup, level 3 gear, enough meds to survive a zombie apocalypse…

…and then immediately get sent back to the lobby by a guy named "PochinkiPresident" using iron sights and pure hatred.

The emotional rollercoaster this game provides should honestly qualify as cardio.

I've experienced:

• Fear.

• Hope.

• Panic.

• Overconfidence.

• Betrayal.

• Acceptance.

• And several near-heart attacks.

I've had moments where I felt like John Wick.

I've also had moments where I accidentally threw a grenade and discovered that I was, in fact, the enemy.

PUBG has taught me many valuable life lessons:

Never trust a quiet house.
Footsteps are louder than my own thoughts.
The blue zone is not your friend.
Trees are emotional support animals.
"Just one more game" is the biggest lie ever told.
The frying pan is not an item.

It is a way of life.

No matter how many times I get sniped from another ZIP code, run over by a Dacia traveling at the speed of light, or lose because I forgot the zone existed, I always come back.

Because when PUBG is good…

IT'S REALLY GOOD.

Nothing compares to those final circles.

Hands sweating.

Heart pounding.

Two players left.

One mistake and you're back in the lobby questioning your existence.

And then…

WINNER WINNER CHICKEN DINNER.

For approximately 30 seconds, you become the greatest soldier who has ever lived.

Then the next match begins and you're immediately punched to death by a naked guy in Pecado.

Perfectly balanced.

560 hours in, and Erangel still feels like home.

10/10.

By and large, the biggest thing I noted about Palworld, that matters a lot to me, is the interaction and functionality that Pokemon lacks. Because that is the big thing, try and escape it, this game will always be up against Pokemon, it is inescapable. Sure, pokemon isn't a survival game thing. But I was telling someone how your pals provide practicality and functionality as opposed to Pokemon, once upon a time Pokemon had to use HMs. But that was just a means of clearing obstacles to actually progress in the terrain. You could use cut to cut the grass but thats about where it ended for a utilitarian purpose outside of cutting small trees. Fly was just to fast travel, surf to move, not the worst things but you could tell Fly had the most use all things considered whilst surf was just a necessity. The pokemon utility abilities in Sun and Moon were just transportation flair, and I think the only real time it came close to what I thought of for functionality was Scarlet/Violet? Where they just hand you the most boring legendary pokemon ever to use as a bike, a mainstay item in the pokemon series?

Another close comparison would be ARK, functionality and all that provided by a large variety of dinos, prehistoric creatures, and mock prehistoric creatures. You're with these creatures regardless for any tasks, and it kinda sorta branches over to how once upon a time your pokemon followed behind you but that seemed to be an on and off gimmick. But that's where the strength is, giving ARK a pokemon reskin of all things somehow just creates magic, it's like the magic of wording and phrasing.

The point is I recommend it. This practically feels like a pokemon game that just expands upon what you do with pokemon. Over 1000 pokemon and you only get 6 for battling. And here? There's just a full exploitation of the variety it brings for the pals.

My biggest point is Pokemon has so much and does so little, this has little, and does more. To Gamefreak, I'm not asking for pokemon with guns, I'm asking for interaction with the world that constantly expands, interaction beyond small token efforts that seldom carryover between games.

In Summary:
Every dollar you spend on a game should be at least 1 hour of entertainment. This game is an absolute steal for the price and I would recommend it to anyone who wants a fun time running around catching Pals and daydreaming.

Palworld feels like a game created by people who asked, "What if we combined creature collecting, survival crafting, automation, exploration, base building, and multiplayer chaos?"—and then refused to remove any of those ideas.

The result is a wonderfully unpredictable adventure that constantly generates memorable moments.

It may not be the most refined survival game available, but it is one of the most imaginative. Whether you're capturing rare Pals, constructing massive factories, or exploring distant regions with friends, Palworld delivers a sense of freedom and discovery that keeps pulling you back for one more adventure.

Overall 9/10, -1 due to Pals can't evolve

I cannot recommend Team Fortress 2 in its current state.
The game still has small moments of fun, but they are buried under years of neglect. Performance is bad, with constant micro-lags, freezes, and random stutters. Casual matchmaking is often unbalanced, and if you queue with friends, you can easily end up joining a team that is already losing because half the server left. Autobalance can also split you from your party, which makes the experience even more frustrating.

The anti-cheat and moderation are also in a terrible state. VAC feels ineffective, reporting players barely seems to matter, and there is almost no visible moderation for griefing, toxicity, or people ruining matches. A lot of servers feel like complete chaos, with players doing whatever they want and no clear structure or control.
Community servers can sometimes be better, but there are not enough good ones, and many of the enjoyable servers have high ping depending on where you live. The result is that maybe 5% of the game is still genuinely enjoyable, but you may need to waste hours just to get one decent match.

The community is also a major problem. There are still good players, of course, but a large part of the experience is toxic chat, random kicks, strange “unspoken rules,” and people with thousands of hours making the game miserable for newer or casual players. 
At times, it feels less like a proper multiplayer game and more like a browser chatroom with a shooter running in the background.

The recent official promotion of a Moscow LAN in 2026 also made me lose even more respect for how this game is handled. Promoting an event in Russia while the war against Ukraine is still ongoing feels completely tone-deaf and disrespectful. Seeing people in the comments mock Ukrainians, post pro-war symbols, or treat the situation like a joke makes it even worse. This is not “just gaming” when the official promotion gives visibility to that kind of environment.

TF2 had a lot of potential and still has a unique style, but right now it feels like an abandoned project kept alive mostly by nostalgia and a small number of good community servers. The best way to enjoy it is probably on a private server with friends or bots. As a public online game, I honestly think it is not worth your time.

There are better ways to spend your time and money on Steam. Avoid this game unless you already know exactly what you are getting into.

I was trying to introduce a friend to the game recently, and we kept getting such awful matchmaking through the casual queue that we switched to Mann vs. Machine.

The state of matchmaking in this game is unconscionably bad. All but one PVP round we played last night featured a death spiral: high-performing squad carries team A while team B gets the snot beaten out of them. Half of team B requeues or quits, and quickplay can't backfill them fast enough, resulting in what is effectively an unwinnable 12v6 for team B until team A scores enough to start a map vote. We were on team A one time, and on team B for the rest of the matches before we switched to MvM.

I was unable to actually use the map vote feature. Clicking on the map options did nothing and there were no keyboard prompts. My friend is patient and understanding and willing to play more MvM, but if this had been my first time playing TF2, unassisted by a veteran player, I would have nuked the game from my hard drive faster than you can say "need a dispenser here".

Any further forays into PVP on my part will be using the "legacy" server browser only. I would rather fellate a pipe grenade than continue to use the queues.

Team Fortress 2 needs no introduction as a masterclass in game design and one of the greatest multiplayer shooters of all time. I returned to the game recently after feeling particularly nostalgic for the fun I had with it when I regularly played it in the early 2010s.

Unfortunately, I don't think it's worth playing at this time, if only for the fact that its now seemingly unmoderated community is spoiled by its innumerable bad apples. Every casual match feels like a coin toss to witness or experience some form of abuse or harassment beyond a threshold that I can accept, even in the context of an online game, as not even the lowest-hanging fruit are systematically dealt with. Avoiding the use of the in-game text or voice chat, a precautionary measure that shouldn't even be necessary in a game centered around teamwork, is not enough to spare you from this, as especially toxic players will exploit everything from item customization, to Steam community integration, to votekicks in an attempt to reach you. It's actually baffling sometimes just how randomly players will get targeted after joining a match and doing literally nothing but playing the game normally, let alone if they do anything that could be considered drawing attention to themselves.

It's tragic, but whether you're an older player considering a return to a former favorite or a fresh face considering a new-old game to get into, you really can do a lot better than TF2 these days.

I bought a video game, not a motherboard project.

Requiring players to update or flash their BIOS just to access a game they already paid for is completely unacceptable. A BIOS update isn't the same as updating a graphics driver—if something goes wrong, you can end up with a PC that won't even boot. The risk may be small, but it should never be a requirement for playing a game.
The worst part is that all of the risk falls on the customer. If a BIOS flash goes wrong, Activision isn't replacing your motherboard or fixing your computer. You're the one left dealing with the consequences. Fighting cheaters is one thing. Pushing legitimate players into firmware-level changes that can potentially brick their systems is another. If your anti-cheat requires customers to risk their hardware just to launch the game your a ♥♥♥♥ company

Honestly, I know almost nothing about "JttW" going in. But I have to say Game Science did pretty well for their first AAA Game

[h2] Gameplay [/h2]

It plays like a hack & slash, semi open world somewhere between God of War and a soulslike, but not nearly as punishing as an actual souls game. Still, it's not easy either.

What I really love is how rewarding exploration is. Every time you go off the main path to hunt boss or mini-bosses, you come back with materials for upgrading weapons, stats, armor, and buff items. Gear has passives designed around different playstyles likes Smash, Pillar, Thrust so you can actually build around how you like to play. The skill tree covers Foundation, Staff Stances, and Spells, all sharing the same skill point, but you can reset freely without spending any items, which is great.

[h2] Story [/h2]

Personally I think the story is presented well, though it can be hard to piece together since the game picks up after the events of JttW. On top of cutscenes and dialogue, there's a journal that unlocks entries as you defeat enemies a ton of lore in there, though I'll be honest, I didn't read all of it. There's just a lot. But I do think you can enjoy the story just fine without knowing anything about JttW beforehand.

You play as the Destined One, a silent protagonist sent to collect six relics to revive Sun Wukong. And that's actually my biggest gripe  he can't speak. From what I know, the real Wukong is mischievous and playful, and you really feel that loss in Act 3 when you're travelling with Zhu Bajie. Zhu Bajie is an absolute highlight from the moment you meet him, full of personality and colour. I couldn't help thinking how hilarious it would've been if our guy could actually talk back. Such a missed opportunity.

Character design across the board is great. Voice acting and the soundtrack are both excellent. And the cutscenes what i wanna say is "absolute cinema", no notes.

One thing I'd criticise though: character development feels a bit lacking. By the end, I didn't really feel like I grew alongside Wukong the way I was hoping to.

[h2] Pacing/ Level Design [/h2]

This is honestly the most disappointing part of the game and where it falls shortest. The Pagoda Prison chapter is a good example  a mechanic that drains 50% of your HP periodically, combined with annoying enemies that constantly try to knock you into pits. That's not challenging design, that's just frustrating. It feels like the developer didn't know what content to add, so they just threw in more enemies instead.

The invisible walls are also all over the place and feel completely inconsistent. Some areas look wide open and worth exploring, but all those places that look worth exploring are just decorative props sitting behind an invisible wall. Then in other area, a ledge that clearly looks jumpable turns out to be blocked, which is completely contrary to the Pagoda Prison map, where it's full of pits and cliffs, yet there are absolutely no invisible walls to block players from falling off. It makes no sense.

The locations themselves look stunning and feel epic, but once you look closer, every map is basically a straight line taking you from one boss fight to the next. Compare it to God of War or Sekiro, which have branching paths that still connect back beautifully ,you can feel what's missing here.

[h2] Performance [/h2]

No major issues on my end. I ran it on a pretty average laptop with DLSS and Frame Generation on  occasional dips but nothing frequent, and the game still looks gorgeous. Only real bug I hit was falling into a pit and not dying, then somehow walking through the map geometry lol. A few shadow glitches here and there, but zero crashes. I think they did well here.

[b] Overall [/b]: it's a genuinely fun game that can easily eat up 50+ hours on your first playthrough, and it has solid replay value too. There are still plenty of areas to improve, but what the game proves is this  whether your protagonist is a monkey or a pig, if you make something good enough, people will show up, buy it, and care about it. No forcing it, no woke nonsense needed. And On top of that, it's given Chinese literature a real moment  JttW already had its fans, but this game has clearly brought in a whole new crowd judging by how much it blew up.

Overall Rating = 7.5/10

A Breathtaking Mythological Masterpiece
​Black Myth: Wukong is an incredible action RPG that beautifully brings the classic Chinese tale "Journey to the West" to life. It features some of the most fluid, fast-paced, and visually spectacular combat in the genre. Rather than a pure Soulslike, it plays more like a modern boss-rush action game with deep customization through spells, transformations, and staff stances. It is a challenging but deeply rewarding journey.
​🟢 Pros
​Spectacular Boss Designs: Packed with dozens of unique, challenging, and visually stunning boss fights, each with distinct movesets and lore.
​Fluid and Varied Combat: Combining fast staff combos with spells (like Immobilize) and the ability to transform into defeated enemies keeps combat fresh and exciting.
​Breathtaking Visuals: Built on Unreal Engine 5, the landscapes, character models, and cultural details look absolutely stunning and highly atmospheric.
​Rich Lore and World: A brilliant, deep dive into Chinese mythology that respects the source material while offering a dark, cinematic narrative.
​🔴 Cons
​Invisible Walls: The level design can feel restrictive, with many invisible walls blocking paths that look completely accessible, making exploration a bit frustrating.
​Performance Heavy: Due to the cutting-edge graphics, it requires a powerful PC to run smoothly at high settings, and you may experience frame drops in dense areas.
​Spikes in Difficulty: A few specific boss fights feature massive difficulty spikes that might feel a bit unbalancing compared to the rest of the chapter.
​Final Verdict: 9/10
​Highly Recommended. A phenomenal achievement in the action-RPG genre. If you love high-skill combat, epic mythological boss fights, and gorgeous worlds, this is an absolute must-buy.

I picked up Black Myth: Wukong looking for a game I could just sit back, relax, and play casually. What I got instead was something much bigger. Despite not being an open-world game, there’s a surprising amount of exploration, hidden content, and side paths to discover, so I ended up spending a lot more time searching every corner than I originally expected.

The first two chapters were amazing. The setting, atmosphere, and Chinese mythology felt fresh and different from pretty much every other action RPG I’ve played.

Then Chapter 3 happened.

The Pagoda Realm is honestly one of the most annoying locations I’ve ever had to play through, and from that point on the game started feeling more and more like a Chinese version of Elden Ring. I really tried not to compare the two, but after Chapter 2 it became hard not to notice where a lot of the inspiration came from.

That being said, Chapter 4 ended up being my favorite part of the game story-wise. I didn’t particularly like the area itself or its theme, but the story and characters there left the biggest impression on me out of the entire game.
 
As for the bosses, I didn’t find them particularly difficult. Most of them went down in five tries or less. The only boss that really made me stop, learn movesets, and change my approach was Erlang, the Sacred Divinity.

Overall, I had a good time with the game. If you enjoy soulslikes, fun combat, memorable boss fights, and a unique setting, I’d definitely recommend giving Black Myth: Wukong a shot.

Left 4 Dead 2 is still one of the best co-op games ever made.
Even after all these years, few games manage to match the chaos, teamwork, and replayability that this game delivers. Every campaign feels different thanks to the AI Director constantly changing item spawns, enemy placement, and pacing.

The gameplay is simple to understand but incredibly fun. Fighting through hordes of infected while trying to keep your team alive creates nonstop tension and chaos, especially on higher difficulties. The special infected are what truly make the game shine - every enemy forces teamwork and communication instead of just mindless shooting.

The atmosphere is also excellent. The game balances horror, action, and humor surprisingly well, and the maps are memorable from start to finish.

The good:
- One of the best co-op experiences ever made
- Extremely replayable
- AI Director keeps matches fresh
- Fantastic special infected design
- Simple but incredibly fun gameplay loop
- Great campaign variety
- Strong atmosphere and pacing
- Workshop support adds endless content
- Still has an active community
- Versus mode is chaotic and addictive

The bad:
- Public lobbies can be toxic
- Bots are sometimes painfully stupid
- Gunplay feels dated compared to modern shooters
- Melee weapons can feel overpowered
- Some campaigns are much stronger than others
- Versus mode can be brutal for new players
- Valve barely updates the game anymore
- Teamkill incidents are basically guaranteed with friends

Overall, Left 4 Dead 2 is a classic for a reason. It’s one of those games that is endlessly replayable, especially with friends, and it still holds up better than many modern co-op shooters.

# Unturned Review: The Best Free Survival Game You've Probably Overlooked

In a gaming landscape dominated by massive survival titles that often cost $20, $40, or even $60, Unturned stands out as one of the most surprising success stories in the genre. Developed primarily by Canadian developer Nelson Sexton, Unturned combines zombie survival, crafting, exploration, base-building, and multiplayer gameplay into a package that is completely free to play. Despite its simple blocky graphics, the game has maintained a loyal player base for years and continues to attract new players looking for a deep survival experience.

## First Impressions

At first glance, Unturned looks almost comically simple. Its low-poly art style often leads newcomers to compare it to games like Minecraft or Roblox. Characters have rectangular limbs, vehicles are simplistic, and environments lack the visual sophistication of modern AAA titles.

However, judging Unturned solely by its graphics would be a mistake. Within minutes of starting a new game, players discover a surprisingly complex survival system hidden beneath its minimalist presentation.

The game drops players into a zombie-infested world where survival depends on managing hunger, thirst, health, stamina, and radiation exposure. Finding supplies becomes a constant priority, and every expedition into abandoned towns carries risks and rewards.

## Gameplay and Survival Mechanics

The core gameplay loop is straightforward but highly addictive. Players search for food, water, weapons, clothing, and medical supplies while avoiding or fighting zombies. Resources are scattered throughout cities, farms, military bases, and remote locations, encouraging exploration across the map.

One of Unturned's greatest strengths is how naturally progression unfolds. Early-game survival feels tense and dangerous. A simple baseball bat or kitchen knife can feel invaluable when ammunition is scarce. As players gather better equipment, they gradually transition from desperate survivors into heavily armed scavengers capable of tackling tougher challenges.

Crafting plays a significant role in progression. Players can create tools, fortifications, storage systems, and advanced equipment from gathered materials. The crafting system is easy to learn but offers enough depth to support long-term gameplay.

Unlike many survival games that become repetitive after a few hours, Unturned constantly presents players with new objectives. Building a secure base, gathering vehicles, exploring military zones, farming crops, and collecting rare weapons provide ongoing motivation.

## Combat

Combat in Unturned is simple but effective. Melee weapons provide a reliable way to conserve ammunition, while firearms introduce a satisfying risk-versus-reward dynamic. Guns attract attention, often drawing additional zombies or hostile players.

The weapon variety is impressive for a free game. Players can find pistols, assault rifles, sniper rifles, shotguns, submachine guns, bows, and explosives. Each weapon feels distinct enough to encourage experimentation.

Zombie encounters remain engaging thanks to different zombie types and environmental challenges. While the AI is not particularly advanced, large groups of zombies can quickly overwhelm unprepared players.

The combat system is not perfect. Hit detection occasionally feels inconsistent, and melee combat can sometimes appear clunky. Nevertheless, it remains enjoyable and functional within the game's broader survival framework.

## Multiplayer Experience

Multiplayer is where Unturned truly shines.

Public servers range from traditional survival experiences to highly modified gameplay modes. Some servers emphasize realism and roleplay, while others focus on faction warfare, economy systems, or large-scale PvP battles.

The social interactions generated by multiplayer survival create some of the game's most memorable moments. Players may encounter friendly strangers willing to share supplies, heavily armed raiders hunting newcomers, or organized groups defending massive fortresses.

Trust becomes a valuable resource. Every encounter carries uncertainty, making player interactions tense and unpredictable.

The modding community has also played a major role in extending the game's lifespan. Custom maps, weapons, vehicles, and gameplay systems allow servers to offer experiences that differ dramatically from the base game.

## Maps and Exploration

Unturned features a variety of official and community-created maps inspired by real-world locations. Each map offers unique environments, loot distribution, and challenges.

Exploration is consistently rewarding. Military compounds contain powerful gear but attract significant danger. Rural areas provide safer opportunities for gathering supplies, while hidden locations reward curious players.

Vehicles further enhance exploration. Cars, trucks, helicopters, boats, and aircraft make traversing large maps enjoyable and add another layer of progression.

The sense of discovery remains one of Unturned's strongest qualities, especially for new players learning the layout of each world.

## Graphics and Performance

Visually, Unturned will not impress players seeking cutting-edge graphics. The blocky art style is intentionally simplistic and often appears dated compared to modern survival games.

However, the visual design offers practical benefits. The game runs well on older hardware, allowing a wider audience to enjoy it. Performance optimization is generally excellent, especially considering the size of many maps and multiplayer servers.

Over time, the game's visual identity has become part of its charm. What initially appears crude eventually feels distinctive and recognizable.

## Strengths

* Completely free-to-play without requiring major purchases.
* Deep survival mechanics.
* Excellent multiplayer experiences.
* Massive modding support.
* Strong replay value.
* Large selection of maps and servers.
* Low system requirements.
* Active community despite its age.

## Weaknesses

* Graphics may discourage some players.
* Combat can occasionally feel awkward.
* New-player learning curve can be steep.
* Public servers sometimes suffer from balance issues.
* Animations and AI show their age.

## Final Verdict

Unturned is one of the most impressive free games available today. It delivers a surprisingly deep survival experience that rivals many paid titles while maintaining accessibility through its simple visuals and low hardware requirements.

What makes Unturned special is not any single mechanic but the way exploration, survival, crafting, combat, and multiplayer interaction combine to create memorable stories. Every session feels different, whether you're building a fortress with friends, scavenging for supplies after a narrow escape, or navigating tense encounters with other survivors.

While its graphics and occasional rough edges prevent it from achieving perfection, the sheer amount of content and freedom available makes it an easy recommendation for fans of survival games.

**Score: 8.5/10**

Having spent 4,000 hours in War Thunder, I can say with confidence that this game is a complete mess. The developers seem far more interested in squeezing money out of players than in actually improving the gaming experience. Every update somehow makes the grind worse, the economy more painful, and the monetization more aggressive. Premium vehicles, premium time, and bundle prices that cost as much as AAA games—the whole system seems designed to force players to pay if they want to progress without losing their minds.

The community has been complaining about this for years: pointless grinding, hundreds of vehicle clones, terrible economic balance, and updates that feel more like money-grabbing schemes than actual improvements. There have even been major community protests and waves of negative feedback because players are simply tired of how the game is being run.

The way developers avoid or suppress discussions about the war in Ukraine within their communities is a disgrace. For a game with a massive international player base and modern military technology, the silence surrounding such a major real-world conflict feels deliberate and uncomfortable.

War Thunder could have been one of the best military games ever created. Instead, it feels like a project where monetization comes first, and the gaming experience is far behind.

War Thunder could be an amazing game. It’s just a great concept, but right now it feels like frustration stacked on top of frustration rather than actual gameplay.

A tragedy 10+ years in the making.

If you want a game where the devs best interests are to sap every last penny from you at every turn? This is the game for you.

I have played this game for over a decade, starting on the original WarThunder in-house launcher, swapping to console and putting in 7k hours there before recently returning to play on steam, and this game has had its few ups, and MANY downs. There have been a handful of truly good changes implemented in the last few years, and even still, Gaijin manages to find a way to half-#$$ the only smart decisions they make (Those 5 battle 100% rp bonuses for having a top tier vehicle really make a difference, 5 WHOLE BATTLES where you get maybe an extra 1k rp per battle on a vehicle that can cost 400k??? how generous). Ghost shells and busted damage models (im looking at you Russian tank fuel tanks and trunnions eating up spall) are still issues that have been present for MANY years now and will most likely continue to be an issue as Gaijin is too concerned with more "profitable ventures" than to fix these game breaking bugs. Even after implementing enough premium packs and GE vehicles to make an entire tech tree, they CONTINUE to implement en masse premium vehicles every single update, some costing upwards of 70+ USD, and even though the community has protested this idea of implementing vehicles for many years, Gaijin is too money hungry to care. After battle replays don't even show the enemy team half the match, and when they do, they don't show you the player view of enemies strangely, not sure if that's an oversight or a "feature". The in-match loadout viewer for friendly aircraft was implemented in a way that doesn't even show the actual loadout of your allies planes, instead showing YOUR OWN LOADOUTS for said plane, which makes the feature completely useless. And then we have volumetric.......... which is a bucket of worms i will not dive into on this review, for new players, all you need to know is its not a good feature, at least in its current state.

Top tier matches in both air and ground are insufferable, with air you often spend half the match on the defensive against ARH missiles, and ground you spend half the match being bombed by Su30s and their pamphlet missiles. Mid tier and Low Tier matches are in a better, but not perfect, state. Uptiers will often put you against vehicles that are near-unkillable for ground vehicles, an example of this is 5.7 America vs 6.7 Germany, if you have enough skill, you can make this work, but newer players are often the casualty of these, with lets say a level 40 US player fighting a level 100 German player with far stronger vehicles. They can also place you against far superior aircraft, in the case of jets, sometimes sending you against aircraft with missiles that are often unavoidable in the aircraft you are flying. Uptiers should simply be removed from the game, they do not fit the climate of matches in WarThunder and only serve to annoy those experiencing one. Balancing is also an issue, with certain vehicles implemented that absolutely dominate most others on release, that either get nerfed later or have even stronger vehicles added to counter them, leading to a cycle of players rushing to get them to stay on top of the game. This is less of an issue now, as so many top tier vehicles have been implemented in all tech trees that all of them are mostly balanced, but it was a huge issue back when, for example, the F14A was added. Gaijin also seems to lack quality control, the amount of game breaking bugs that are implemented every single update is impressive, with one of the most recent ones forcing a certain prop plane to spawn in at mach 2.7, making the vehicle unplayable for a few days to a week, possibly longer until a fix was found. Gaijin also artificially nerfs certain vehicles for reasons beyond the human psyche, with the Abrams line of tanks being a prime example, there is evidence of them having much better armor irl, yet they refuse to implement this. There have also been quite a few controversies that i wont get into here, if you want to know about them, just search "warthunder controversy".

And what is single handedly the biggest issue of this game, the grind. I have about 8k hours played on one account, with probably 1k$ spent over the course of nearly a decade, and i do not have every tech tree vehicle in the game. You need to put an absurd amount of time into this game if you want to get certain vehicles, and more often than not, they end up being awful to play, meaning you more or less wasted your time unlocking them. Although this game is free, you are basically forced to pay 50 USD a year (assuming you get it on sale) for Premium account to actually get a reasonable amount of research points to unlock vehicles. 


I could continue to ramble on about the issues of this game, as there are far more than whats described here, but these are some of the big ones and some issues i personally have with the game. Overall, i simply cannot recommend playing the game in its current state, and i hope that this review has convinced even a single person to reconsider getting into this game.

I’ve been playing *War Thunder* for years, and watching this game slowly decay into a soulless cash grab has been incredibly depressing. What used to be a detailed, passion-driven military simulator has officially crossed the threshold into corporate laziness, and the developers at Gaijin seem more interested in cutting corners than fixing their fractured game.

The absolute breaking point has been the encroaching use of AI. It’s insulting as a customer to pay $70+ for premium vehicles only to find out that the company can’t even be bothered to pay actual human artists to texture and design them. Discovering AI placeholders, weird artifacting, and literal AI watermarks (like the Gemini logos left on in-game assets and profile icons) shows exactly where their priorities lie. When called out, their response isn't to hire talented artists to fix it—it’s to just scrub the watermarks and leave the cheap AI slop in the game. When a company stops respecting its own art department, you know they’ve completely stopped respecting the players.

Aside from the AI controversies, the actual gameplay loop is getting worse with every consecutive update.

* **The Broken Meta Pipeline:** The monetization model has become outright predatory. Gaijin routinely drops a blatantly overpowered, broken premium vehicle (like the recent BMP-T or various top-tier jets), watches the community buy it en masse to survive the meta, and then quietly nerfs it into the ground six months later after they've milked every dollar out of it.
* **Constant UI and Economy Regression:** The interface updates over the last year have been atrocious, overcomplicating things that worked perfectly fine. Meanwhile, core game modes have been stale for half a decade. We are still playing the same repetitive team deathmatches on terrible, unbalanced maps where you can get sniped directly into your own spawn from across the map two minutes into a match.
* **The Soul-Crushing Grind:** Unless you are running premium time and high-tier premium vehicles, progressing through a single tech tree takes a ridiculous, agonizing amount of time. Instead of making the game rewarding to play, Gaijin treats its player base like data points to be optimized for maximum frustration-spending.

Don't feed the Snail. *War Thunder* has completely lost its way, trading genuine craftsmanship and historical passion for automated asset generation and endless premium slop. Save your sanity, your time, and your wallet, and play something else.
So they've added AI generated slop to the game. Looks like I won't be discovering more of that game any time soon.

I’m writing this from the bottom of the rabbit hole. I have thrown 2,100 hours of my life and wayyy more than $400 into this black hole, after only trying it playing f2p. I ground out top-tier tanks and jets for the US, Germany, and Russia. My first was Germany, without any premium account nor vehicles to the top, completely f2p. The others with lineups of premium vehicles... What do I have to show for it? Zero actual joy, a mountain of regret, and a textbook case of gaming Stockholm syndrome. War Thunder is just a frustration machine built to make you spend real money to fix problems the developers intentionally created!

Forget "Russian Bias". The reality is way worse. Gaijin doesn't care about flags and petty national pride; they are just a corporate machine using engineered imbalance to print cash. The broken volumetric armor, the magical fuel tanks that swallow shells, and the ghost rounds are coded on purpose to make you lose your mind. They want you to feel completely powerless so you look at the thousands of hours left on the grind, crack, and pull out your credit card.

But the second you buy a $70 premium to escape the misery, the matchmaker throws you straight into a premium sweat-shop bracket, your vehicle gets secretly nerfed in a minor patch, and you are right back to being food. They actively tank the economy and slash rewards so you barely make enough in-game cash to cover basic repair costs unless you run a premium account and a premium vehicle at the exact same time. ESPECIALLY if you have skill issues. Not me tho, I'm built different... jk-

Top-tier ground is pure anti-fun. You spend months unlocking a modern tank, only for it to spawn in completely broken with no laser rangefinder, no thermals, and no fire extinguishers. You will literally sit there and burn to death from a minor engine scratch because you didn't pay the digital tax to unlock a basic fire extinguisher. Then you deal with level-one players who buy high-rank premium packs, rush and die once because they don't know the maps, and quit immediately, leaving your team half-empty in three minutes. If you survive that, Close Air Support vets or whales bomb you from kilometers away while you sit in a tank with absolutely no way to shoot back, all on tiny arcade maps made a decade ago for WWII Shermans.

The current developer cycle is a joke. They drop copy-pasted tech tree vehicles AND/OR a broken, overpowered premium &/ top-tier vehicle, let it ruin the matchmaking for months so everyone buys it out of desperation, and the moment sales slow down, they nerf it under the guise of "historical balancing" before dropping the next broken vehicle to start the loop over. Reminder that they once firmly stated they'll never let players buy to the top...

The laziness has reached an absolute breaking point. Look at the recent Senrai Maiden packs. They are charging $70 for copy-paste vehicle clones that already exist for free in the normal tech tree, wrapping them in cheap anime gooner skins. They don't even pay real human artists anymore. The community caught them red-handed leaving visible Google Gemini AI watermarks on the premium decorations and M60 camouflage textures. When caught, they blamed an "outsourcing partner", erased the watermark, and left the exact same AI slop in the paid packs anyway. From profile pictures to voice lines, they use generative AI to cut production costs to zero while charging you luxury collector prices. They've done this for a while now, with all the AI generated profile pictures you can buy, that they've slapped an "artist" name on...

Even the company structure is a mess. Huge whistleblower leaks from long-time staff like Keofox exposed that fired employees kept admin access to internal player data and Steam backends for years. They even admitted that Gaijin explicitly codes pop-up review prompts to only trigger right after a player wins a match or gets a lucky prize, intentionally rigging the metrics to mask how miserable the player base actually is on Steam.

The ultimate tragedy is that War Thunder is a monopoly holding military history nerds like us hostage. It sits in the perfect dead zone between the arcade nonsense of World of Tanks and the stressful complexity of DCS. Because there is zero competition, Gaijin knows you have nowhere else to go. They actively exploit your love for aviation and armor, trapping it behind a digital casino layout. They ignore every plea for new game modes, bigger battlefields, or a tank-only mode because they know there is always a new wave of players willing to take the abuse.

They do not care about history, balance, or whether you are actually having fun. They care about keeping you trapped on a treadmill of digital misery just long enough to bleed your wallet dry-

-~-~-~-~-~-~-~-

If your love for military history is too strong and you ignore this warning anyway, do yourself one favor: treat this game strictly like a digital museum. Play the low tiers, appreciate the WWII armor, and uninstall the absolute second you hit Rank III.

~The moment you chase the modern era, the historical simulator ends, and the predatory progression wall takes over. Break the cycle before it starts. Save your sanity, and play literally anything else~

I think it’s time to add a more grounded perspective to the discourse around Monster Hunter Wilds. Some of the criticism it’s getting is fair—but a lot of it feels like long-time players complaining about changes that don’t meaningfully harm the experience and, in many cases, actively improve it.

For context, I’m not a diehard fan of the series. I played Monster Hunter: World, skipped Monster Hunter Rise, and came back for Wilds specifically because it addressed some of the friction points I’ve always had with the series.

Focus Mode:

Focus Mode doesn’t “ruin” the game—it’s optional. If you don’t like it, don’t use it. That’s the simplest reality.

For many players, especially newer ones, it improves responsiveness and reduces the feeling that you’re fighting your own character instead of the monster. There’s a recurring mindset in parts of the gaming community that clunkiness equals skill expression. It doesn’t. Sometimes something just feels bad—and smoothing that out doesn’t dilute the experience, it refines it.

If someone finds themselves using Focus Mode despite claiming to hate it, that says more about how it improves gameplay feel than anything else.

Wound System:

If there’s a system that actually impacts gameplay balance, it’s this one.

The Wound System was over tuned at launch and, even after adjustments, still leans toward being too strong. It can trivialize encounters by enabling repeated staggers and high-damage windows with minimal risk. As a result, fights often feel shorter and less dynamic than they should, with monsters going down in just a few minutes.

This is where criticism should be focused; not on optional quality-of-life features, but on mechanics that meaningfully affect combat depth.

Mounts and Travel:

Auto-travel isn’t particularly exciting, and it does feel like a convenience feature rather than a meaningful addition. That said, the argument that it “removes the hunting from Monster Hunter” doesn’t really hold up.

The series has never truly been about hunting in a simulation sense. Monsters are marked almost immediately, and the gameplay loop has always been about engaging them in combat rather than tracking them in a systemic or emergent way. There’s no real risk of losing a trail, no deep ecology systems to learn, and no meaningful punishment for poor tracking.

At its core, Monster 'Hunter' is more-so just a name than it is a suggestion of its gameplay loop and has always been closer to an arena-based action game than a true hunting simulator. Certainly not like something even somewhat controversial in hunting communities, like theHunter: Call of the Wild.

Where its seen as a watered down hunting game, this one doesn't even barely begin to reach the basic foundations to be really considered one. You're chasing after a monster to fight it, you aren't exactly hunting it when there's nothing but surface level flavor text outside of missions to give an illusion of that.

Alongside that, I highly doubt that a large portion of players are sitting here taking in the vistas. Monster Hunters in any form aren't exactly pushing the graphical boundries and while the set pieces of their world can be cool to see, like any game's environment, it's guaranteed to get old for the vast majority of players by even the 5th time going through it, so I don't feel like this is any better of a complaint. 

There are few if any exploration rewards or reasons to spend a load of time in these zones beyond fighting the monster or grinding ingredients to fight the monsters and the zones themselves aren't to the quality or beauty of something like RDR2, Dark Souls titles, Crimson Desert, and more which all focus more on the exploration quality than this series ever did and likely ever will.

These maps are just that: Set pieces to an epic battle between some giant beast and a human. That's why most players got into this series and most players will keep playing it, not because the tiny map you can run around in 15 minutes looks nice.

Grind:

Complaining that the game doesn’t demand excessive grinding is hard to take seriously.

Reducing artificial time sinks isn’t a flaw. If players can build viable gear without repeating the same hunt dozens (or hundreds) of times, that’s respecting their time. Players who enjoy grinding can still do so voluntarily—but designing progression around extreme repetition shouldn’t be the baseline expectation.

A better direction would be adding meaningful challenges—harder variants, unique encounters, or skill-based rewards—rather than padding playtime through low drop rates.

Broader Issues:

Where Wilds, and frankly, World before it actually stumbles is in content and post-launch structure.

Both games launched feeling incomplete, relying heavily on updates and expansions to reach their full potential. There’s also a noticeable reliance on reusing monsters to stretch content. This pattern suggests a broader issue with how Capcom is managing the series, rather than any single gameplay mechanic.

Final Thoughts:

A lot of the backlash toward Wilds feels less like thoughtful critique and more like resistance to change—especially from players whose experience and identity are tied to mastering older, clunkier systems.

But improvements to responsiveness, accessibility, and pacing don’t invalidate the series—they modernize it. And if those changes are optional, then they’re not even taking anything away.

Criticism is important, but it should be aimed at systems that actually affect the game’s depth and longevity—not features that simply make it feel better to play.

[h1] The Sad End Of An Era [/h1]

I never thought I would be writing a negative review for a Monster Hunter game seeing as it was and still kind of is one of my favorite video game franchises, but here we are. In my opinion, Capcom has completely and utterly dropped the ball on this game and ruined what was once an incredibly compelling and deep gaming experience. In this review, I will illustrate my thoughts on what went wrong and why this game should be avoided for the time being.

[h2] The Gameplay [/h2]

Since the release of [i]Monster Hunter: World[/i] there has been a slow shift to make the series more accessible to newcomers with the introduction of streamlined mechanics. As someone who started with World and went back to play older games, I can say that most of these changes were welcome additions, moving the series forward from its slow and sometimes clunky roots. Wilds tries to continue this trend, but I feel as though it streamlined some mechanics too far, trivializing the experience. 

Take the new Seikret for example; what should be a fun tool that makes map traversal easier is immediately ruined when you realize that this creature knows where the monster is on the map at all times and is able to automatically take you to its location with very little input from the player. At that point, it really doesn't feel like you are hunting the monster anymore and it gives the player very little incentive to explore the map or study monster behavior which used to be a core mechanic of older games that was beloved by fans.

Another drastic change that’s been made was the decision to split defensive and offensive skills between armor and weapons respectively. I am really not sure what Capcom hoped to accomplish with this choice, because all it really did was make builds way more rigid and took away a lot of freedom when it comes to armor skills. This has resulted in an endgame build that basically fits every weapon with not a lot of deviation which feels very boring.

Speaking of endgame, that is another thing that Capcom has completely neutered. I’m not going to beat around the bush here and say that this game was unfinished at launch. [i]Monster Hunter[/i] has always been a series praised for its deep and complex endgame grind, but there has been a concerning trend that started with Rise where they release the game with only a couple of monsters to hunt for end game gear, and slowly add more monsters with content updates. This trend has continued with Wilds where, at launch, this game had 2 endgame monsters to grind. There is also the aspect of the special weapons that players are able to make from grinding these endgame monsters, which are also incredibly flawed. Basically, you get these weapon pieces from high powered endgame monsters and can build weapons out of them. You can then “reinforce” them which is basically rolling augments for more buffs. Here’s the thing though: if you don’t get the right stats for these weapons, you have to [i]Disassemble[/i] the weapon, losing those parts that you worked hard to grind for. That is an insanely unsatisfying and unrewarding system that is basically just straight up gambling for the right augments. I know the Monster Hunter series is known for its RNG-based systems, but this is getting ridiculous at this point.

[h2] The Story [/h2]

[i]Monster Hunter[/i] has never been a series known for its riveting plot lines or deep character arcs but over time it seems that Capcom has been wanting to remedy this. In World we got fully animated and voice acted cutscenes which deepend our connections to the world and characters. Then there was Rise which gave all of the NPC characters names rather than just their job title which I thought was nice. But then we come to Wilds, where the team seemingly decided to put even more emphasis on the story while simultaneously not putting any effort into making these characters memorable or have any sort of character arcs. This is the first time where I would say that the story of a Monster Hunter game actively got in the way and hindered my enjoyment. First of all, the story overall felt very rushed. You are moving from one locale to another at a breakneck pace with barely any time to get familiarized with the towns and locals. But at the same time, your crew of NPC friends that you’re on this journey with are constantly gathering with you for mandatory “yap sessions” where you all stand in a circle and talk about what has already happened in the story so far. It is so beyond frustrating because I want to take my time and get to know the locations and monsters of the area, but it feels like the game is shaking me by the shoulders and yelling at me to just get on with it. The story is basically the same as every other entry in the series, but I really feel like the developers are trying too hard to make me care about these plot points and characters when that was never really why I played these games to begin with. It makes the whole experience feel like it was born out of corporate greed rather than passion.

[h2] The Performance [/h2]

I will include my PC specs in this review to show that I’m not working with the best or worst hardware here but also to illustrate that the PC launch of this game was [i]rough.[/i] From day one, I have experienced several stability issues that have made playing this game quite frustrating to play. Frequent crashes, long load times, and graphical bugs have made me dread booting up this game because it just becomes a production. Not to mention it has that classic modern gaming touch of having to preload shaders before it can actually start. This is super frustrating, especially if I haven’t touched the game in a while because it will now take 20-30 minutes to actually load into this game. I don’t know if the RE engine is just bad when it comes to PC gaming, but so many important aspects of this game's performance just don’t work. The worst part is that this is all for a game that doesn’t even look that much better than [i]World.[/i] Now, credit where credit is due: CAPCOM have released several stability updates that have improved performance significantly. But the damage has been done and the game still sometimes has stability issues. It’s a real shame because up until now Monster Hunter games have had pretty rock solid performance. I just hate to see one of my favorite series in such a state.

[h2] Conclusion [/h2]
Like I said at the beginning: I don’t like having to make a review like this. I spent about 5 years playing these games religiously. There really is no other franchise that even compares to this unique living world that the team has created. So it breaks my heart to see it like this. I have watched this series slowly but surely fall into the trappings of greedy modern gaming practices that it has become a shadow of its former self. Fortunately or unfortunately depending on how you look at it; a lot of these issues will be fixed with the DLC expansion that was recently announced. It’s just a shame that we will have to pay extra to get the “full” experience. You know, there’s a part of me that wishes this series never expanded, that it never broke free of its slower, more methodical if not clunky roots and stayed a niche series. But Monster Hunter is a series that loves to experiment; that’s something I love about it. Wilds, however, has been a clear failure.

It's definitely an enjoyable monster slaying action game with plenty to keep you busy for a good while. There's lots of gear to craft, items to collect, and creatures to capture. The game world is vibrant and many areas look very well done despite the somewhat washed out filtering going on throughout the game.

I liked having the mounts to speed up travel time and it's neat watching them wall run and jump between obstacles. The new combat features were also pretty sweet, as a dual blade character I really enjoyed the focus strikes. 

Somehow despite all of this I still find World to be the better game though I can't quite seem to put in to words why that is. I'd still say give this game a fair try though.

After 200 hours I haven't had any performance issues, I don't know how the other players with much better spces than me leave negative reviews talking about poor performance, the game works completely fine for me. I've only had some connection issues but that gets fixed just by updating my dirvers so I wouldn't say I've had any perfomance issues so far.

The combat is really fun and the monster fights are great, there are many mechanics that I like for example being able to aim your melee attacks to target a specific part of a monster and it complements the wound mechanic perfectly. You can also use the environment at your advantage by creating hazards to trap or damage the monsters and use the endemic life in your favor in order to heal or inflict status ailments to the monsters.

There are many weapons to choose from and everyone plays very differently so there is a weapon for any playstyle; one thing that I really like about it is that no matter if you choose a simple or a very complicated weapon because once you master it any weapon could be as good as the next in my opinion.

The bosses are well designed and are very distinct from one another so there will be a lot of variety in the many different hunts that you can take once you hit High Rank. The main story can feel easy at the beginning for some players but the late game bosses have more advanced patterns of attacks and it could get really dangerous if you mess up.

The developers clearly wanted to make a fast paced and action packed entry on the series; while MH WORLD has a more slow and meticulous approach that a lot of people liked becuse they felt like actual hunters WILDS gives you a bird like dinosaur that automatically rides you through the map so you can focus only on fighting and so removing the elements of exploration and monster tracking that makes World so appealing for most people.
I think that it is a valid criticism and I actually agree with the players who feel that way but I also feel that they are too harsh on this game just because is different while it clearly has no intentions on being like it's predecessor.

In conclusion, old school Monster Hunter players may not like the newer games and specially this one either for the easier difficulty or the quality of life that removed some elements form previous entries but every Monter Hunter game is very different from one another and everyone of them are able to excel on their own despite their differences.

Honestly this game is a 10/10 for me and I highly recommend it to any one who likes action/adventure games and to anyone who wants to get into playing Monster Hunter.
Still a great game and must play but definitely did not live up to Monster Hunter World.

You can see other hunters everywhere in camps and join anyone's quests to help whenever you want. I love the community feeling you get being surrounded by other hunters. 

The only negative patch this game had were optimization issues that persisted for a while but slowly got better bit by bit with continuous updates. Largely right now things are much much better. The guys at Capcom acknowledged it was a strong pain point for many and have made strides. So I wouldn't let old reviews deter you too much. Give it a go and you won't be disappointed. The game will only improve more and more over time in terms of content and optimization as usual with all Monster Hunter games. One year later the game is better than ever! Launch was a complete disaster, but now? Everything is good, my performance issues are gone and there's tons of difficult content now to devour, including Final Fantasy XIV collaborations.

The game itself is relatively fine, my gripe is with the Co-op.

My friend group saw it was a 4 player game and thought it would be a cool new one to try out (we've never played a game like this one before). Come to find out the process of playing together is so obnoxiously convoluted that it's not even worth playing.

You can't do actual missions together, you have to do the dialogue and npc following by yourself, then once there's a boss fight, it'll send an auto invite to anyone in your party to join (which isn't instantaneous btw, you'll have like 30-60 seconds of just you fighting by yourself at the very least; PLUS, it spawns your teammates at the base, not at the boss fight, so they have to run all the way to the fight, wherever it may be)

Oh, did you think you were gonna be able to just run around in the open environment together by just simply being in a party? You'd be absolutely right if it were any other game. This game requires you to be in a "link party" first, then be sure you've progressed at least an hour and a half through the game by yourselves, then you can make an "environment party", where you can just run around the open world together and fight basic monsters, no quest-related things, though.

I've never been so irritated trying to figure out how to simply play with my friends, and we would have all just returned the game but by the time we were able to finally get far enough to make it work, the 2 hour limit for returns was over.

If you're in my shoes wondering if you and your friends should get this game to play together, look elsewhere, it's genuinely not worth the headache to do barely anything with each other anyway.

So the core gameplay of the game is really solid, for the most part I love this game, but the state it's in is absolutely unacceptable for a full retail product. Crashes are constant, sometimes so severe that they require a full system restart. Assets failing to load is also common, controller configuration can break, and the game as a whole will not run reliably. For a game this far post-release, that is absolutely not acceptable.

Running on a 3060 Laptop, with -dx11 launch options, can say it runs pretty well, only crashed a few times, but most of the times its very much playable for hours and hours, if there were any crash, i recommend:

- sfc /scannow in command prompt as admin, as this will fix corrupted files
- clear DirectX Shader Cache in Storage-> Temporary Files, if GPU suddenly goes bye bye

So far its playable and enjoyable, But im on Low graphic settings, with alot of options on lowest and disabled, as i only have 6gb VRAM, and with these settings it is already using around 4gb VRAM

I hope this helps people able to do some light troubleshooting, as the game is on discount and i feel like alot of people are getting into the game with DX11 optimized GPU

The game itself is beautiful, and I never experienced the performance issues or the crashing that many people have reported.  My complains are mostly around the game itself.  Capcom has been trying to simplify Monster Hunter for a while now, and Wilds is the most extreme example yet.  Gathering and crafting is easy and just don't feel rewarding anymore. Hunts are short, and the automatic traveling system kills the feel of actually being in the environment.  I find myself just not having fun or the desire to play the way I have with other games in the series.

Initially a great game, especially with a full group of friends, but has since been on a steepening decline. There are several causes: limited testing of new enemies/features, artificially locking central wargame mechanics in stalemate, aggressive moves by publisher Sony, and a fairly dysfunctional relationship between the developers and community.

To summarize each point:

- Updates introducing new gear or enemy types have often released unpolished, causing sudden difficulty spikes in the higher difficulties. New sub-factions often dropped with exaggerated difficulty spikes (From insta-killing fire shotguns to burrowing bugs that cannot be killed until lunging and damaging the player to mechs that demand relentless firepower to destroy while constantly spawning in drops and idle patrols). At the same time, newly introduced gear often launches underperforming next to gear that's been in game for more than two years. Heavy backpack versions of existing weapons launch with little advantage over their counterparts.

-  The game is based around a "galactic war" similar to any strategy game battlemap, but the battlemap is largely controlled by the devs. The way planets are captured or defended by the playerbase heavily relies upon the number of players present. Outside of major orders, you will largely find that the same ten or so planets are always available to fight at, and there is little deviation from those locations.

-  Sony attempted to region lock the playerbase in 2024, requiring all players to use a Play Station Network account to access HD2. Thousands of players in countries unsupported by PSN effectively would have been DRM'ed out of the game. Steam graciously offered refunds to those who would be affected by the region-lock. A tie in with the Killzone franchise also ended up in disaster, as the store items required more currency than would be spent on an entire warbond. After a notably short time in the in-game store, the items disappeared for an entire year and have only recently returned, but now under a "premium" warbond with a higher credit requirement than any standard warbonds.

-  The playerbase is constantly at odds with the developers. Veteran players from the first year have highlighted nerfs to player equipment (, serious performance issues, ridiculous enemy buffs (common grunt enemies receiving heavy armor, enemies that have always had indestructible shields, new enemies with obscure or no weakpoints), abandonment of promised content (No new weapon attachments since first introduction, no customization of armor passive boosts or cosmetics, while helmets grant no bonuses and are cosmetic only, no use for samples after all ship upgrades), fix patches launch alongside new game content warbonds, and warbond system has been bloated with so much content it would take hundreds of hours of farming to unlock it all for free.

This was a fun game that is now teetering on its end of life. New factions pose substantial difficulty curves beyond reasonable difficulty levels, gear is locked behind paid content or countless hours of currency farming, devs and publisher have seriously eroded the playerbase's trust.


If you still want to play, wait for sale.

Helldivers 2 was one of the best co-op games I have played in years. Chaotic, hilarious, brutal, cinematic, and genuinely special. It felt like a glorious exercise in Managed Democracy, where every mission was a beautiful disaster and every dead Helldiver was a small price to pay for Liberty.

Unfortunately, Arrowhead now appears to be fighting a new enemy: their own game.

Recent balance changes have turned what was once fun chaos into a live-service cautionary tale. Weapons get “adjusted” in ways that make the game less enjoyable. Enemy balance swings wildly. Performance gets worse. Communication gets slippery. Patch notes say one thing, the game feels like another, and then suddenly we are told after the fact that a released patch was apparently just a “test.”

Ah yes. A test patch. Released to the public. With no clear warning in the original patch notes. Truly, the Ministry of Truth is working overtime.

The latest patch was supposed to improve graphics performance issues. Instead, for me and apparently a huge chunk of the community, performance became worse. Not slightly worse. Not “please verify your files” worse. More like “did the Automatons sabotage my GPU?” worse. When player surveys are showing overwhelming reports of degraded performance, that is not a minor edge case. That is a managed disaster.

The worst part is that the core game is still brilliant underneath the rubble. That is what makes this so frustrating. Helldivers 2 is not a bad game. It is a great game being slowly strangled by bad balance decisions, poor communication, and an increasingly impressive ability to turn community goodwill into orbital debris.

Arrowhead had lightning in a bottle. A rare thing. A game people loved, defended, memed, and wanted to see succeed. But instead of reinforcing what worked, they keep “balancing” the fun out of it and then acting surprised when the citizens of Super Earth start questioning command.

I want to recommend this game. I really do. There is still greatness here. But right now, I cannot recommend it in good faith.

Until Arrowhead stops treating the live player base like unpaid QA testers, starts being honest about what patches are actually doing, and remembers that fun is not an exploit to be nerfed, this game remains a tragic example of how to take a beloved success and march it straight into friendly fire.

For Liberty. For Managed Democracy. For the love of Super Earth, stop killing your own game.
