Full Review: Helldivers 2 is a game whose bones are so unbelievably solid its still extremely fun after years of mismanagement. However, as a multiplayer game the experience of playing it is highly dependent on the community and the discussions happening around the game. And therein lies the critical flaw with how Arrowhead has managed the game. HD2 has been painstakingly managed in such a way that they'd end up with a player base always primed for an explosion. As a game developer, one might feel its necessary to make unpopular changes to ones game for balance or in anticipation of other future changes, I accept that completely. However, one might expect that in an experience as openly buggy as HD2 with so many incomplete systems and oodles of tools that are never picked, some highly requested features might be regularly trickled in as well to ensure a consistently improving game experience. One might expect that in a game based on an extremely punishing  loop whose difficulty is primarily derived from how easily a mistake cam kill you, bugs and outright imbalanced aspects which disrupt that gameplay loop and cause your death even when you are doing everything right would have at least a little priority.

However, it does not matter how egregious a bug, how useless a tool, or how needed a change may be, Arrowhead will only consistently deliver when being review bombed. Basically any new issues in the game can be expected to linger for months or years unless the players immediately make their displeasure known, and features the studio has promised only seem to materialize when arrowhead needs to desperately regain player trust. Features that were functional and fun at games launch have simply been broken for months, issues which Arrowhead hasn't even indicated they care to fix despite consistent player feedback (looking at you hellpod steering). Meanwhile, when players come up with new ways to play the game or are too vocal about how much they enjoy certain pieces of equipment, it will be nerfed next patch, and such nerfs will be justified by saying they were player requested even though we can obviously tell that such feedback was the minority, if it existed at all. This is obviously going to create a community primed to explode on regular basis. Idk what they expected when running the game like this- they certainly had plenty of time to observe this cycle forming and nip it in the bud, but now their stuck with a community extremely sensitive to changes they don't like and somewhat sparing with praise. Now that they've dug this grave for themselves, they could put literally ANY effort into managing the player backlash before it starts by including requested buffs with nerfs and throwing in the features they know players want AND HAVE TALKED ABOUT ADDING SINCE RELEASE but they... simply choose not too. As I mentioned at the start of my review, like many multiplayer games this game is highly dependent on the vibes of its community, and Arrowhead seems content with a community brimming with resentment and rage. Which is a bold choice for a multiplayer game you hypothetically want people to keep playing. 

Various interviews and AMA's before and after the games release have made it clear Arrowhead set out to make a niche game, and accidentally made a popular one. I don't begrudge them their creative vision, quite the opposite in fact. Arrowhead, if you want to develop a more brutal grunt fantasy game with less spectacle, you ideally should have released the game in that state, but absent that you should just commit. Accept your gonna loose the vast majority of your players and make your vision. If pressure from Sony or other outside forces are preventing you from doing that by forcing you to change course anytime you loose too many players, as many in the community suggest, you need to accept the consequences of being beholden to people who don't care about your creative vision and make the game the community broadly desires. Right now, you are giving everyone involved the worst possible outcome,  as the mainstream players are treated like cash cows who are regularly lured back when needed, and the players who hypothetically desire the game Arrowhead envisions are constantly having their experience compromised for people who are growing more polarized and resentful of that vision, and proceed to bring that resentment into the game. Its simply not sustainable.

I use the word "sustainable" deliberately. This cycle will not be sustained for much longer. Make no mistake, I genuinely believe that this game has time to regain community trust, break the current extremely toxic dynamic that's formed, and dominate the top sellers list for years to come. I don't think it takes a genius or a massive AAA studio to see the vision of how that happens either- HD2 is so fun that even at its worst it provides more satisfaction than most games ever will, and its ceiling can be so, so, so much higher. But are nearing the point that so much damage will have been done, so much resentment fomented, the game will never recover from the toxicity that's poisoned its core. 

To summarize, purchasing Helldivers 2 at this point in time is the video game equivalent of making a new best friend whose also terminally ill and refuses to get proper treatment because of their "personal convictions". Fulfilling, but also detrimental to your long term mental health. If that sounds like a blast then this is the game for you!

I've tried a handful of MMO experiences in my time, and one thing that has kept me coming back to this one and leaving others behind is combat feel and a more simplistic design compared to others. I might be in the minority on this but I find MMOs to generally be too complex and their combat tends to feel stiff and clunky to play. Thankfully here the fundamentals of gameplay are tight. Guns are punchy and responsive, movement is fluid, and every character only has 4 abilities for you to think about instead of 12-15 or more. Creating builds is simple too. Instead of skill trees you just slot up to 10 mods in your warframe or 8 in your weapons to make them more powerful, and each mod can drastically affect how powerful you are. This can be done on the fly between missions so you're free to play around with whatever setup you like, and It's this simpler approach to gameplay and build crafting that's kept me hooked on the game where other MMO experiences have not.

The game also respects your time and money as a free to play player very well. most of things you can spend money are either cosmetic or can be farmed, and farming is kinda the whole point. I also like the story here. It can be hard to understand and doesn't always make sense, but I think there are aspects to get attached to. Customization is great as well. There's tons of skins, attachments, capes, colors, auras and more to deck out your space ninja with. there is limited room for roleplay in the character customization unlike on other MMOs but I'm personally okay with that.

On top of all that the game gets frequently updated with new content to try, QOL changes and general improvements to the game. The game has definitely had its rough patches (who remembers 2020-2022 warframe? yikes), but after a change in leadership the game has been getting some of its best new additions in years and the people running the game now have proven to be very perceptive to community feedback. If you've been thinking about getting into warframe I can't think of a better time in the game's history to give it a try and sere what you think.

This game is incredible. Fantastic community, wonderful stories, great gameplay. If you are seeing a billion reviews of people calling it woke slop, please, god, ignore them. No game does it like Warframe. They don't pander, they believe, wholeheartedly, in what they say. At its core, Warframe is a game about love, in all shapes. Major characters are gay, transgender, poc, and they're all treated with respect and care, because Warframe cares. The Tenno care. We all lift together.

[h3]A generational game, but not without flaws[/h3]

To preface this review: my only experience with any souls-like was 'The First Berserker: Khazan', and I never finished it.
Actually, I almost never finished Elden Ring either. A few hours in, and I was ready to quit, but the sheer number of players glazing this game was bugging me. "What do they see, that i dont?''
I couldn't tell you exactly when I found my answer; it was more of a gradual experience. But I do now understand, even if I don't fully agree.

The [b][i]world[/i][/b] that's built here is amazing. The ambience and overall vibes made me feel like this was a living, breathing realm full of despair and danger. Even without many cut scenes, I could still grasp the severity and gravity of the events that had taken place. (even if most of the time i had no idea what was happening in the story)

The [b][i]combat[/i][/b], while at times was a little frustrating, is still one of its best features. The build diversity and scope are something special that I can't really find a comparison to in anything else I've played before it. 
The bosses, too, were imaginative and challenging, and more than a few times I found myself jumping out of my seat and slamming my desk in excitement after I defeated them.

There's more I could praise, but I won't, because so many reviews have already done that. So instead ill bring up a few of the [b][i]negatives[/i][/b] that i dont see mentioned very often.
The target locking in this game is dreadful. I swear I died more times to this than actual bosses. It's mostly fine when you have a single enemy to deal with, but as soon as there are multiple targets, it fails miserably.
The other massive flaw I feel this game has is with its in-game systems/mechanics. I can't think of a single game I've ever played that required me to tab out and Google as many times as this. It really was ridiculous. There is just so much that isn't explained or explained well, and most players will miss out on a lot of content or waste a lot of time without Alt-tabbing and turning to guides. 

Overall though, I'm happy I put the time in and finished it. It was a great adventure.

The premise of Elden Ring was really quite simple. You know this iconic series of challenging action games FromSoftware is known to make? Take that and make it bigger, grander and epic. Make it Dark Souls, but open world. Then give GRR Martin yet another excuse not to finish the Winds of Winter by hiring him to create for you a backstory full of weird incestuous genderfluid gods. Finally, add to the mix a strange horse-goat of a mount and you have something really quite unusual. That's the premise and the promise. Now whether it delivers on that promise or not that's a different discussion. One that after spending a good 200 hours on this game's expansive world, perhaps I can answer.

Let's begin with the elephant in the room. The open world. This is one damn big elephant, truly ginormous. It goes on and on. Mountains, swamps, frozen over lakes, caverns, vast underground locations. This is one of the bigger games I have played and it's filled with details. It's also terrifyingly beautiful. The rich use of colour, the stunning landscapes, the dramatic vistas, the incredible heights. The game's art direction is top-notch. I would argue it's era defining. However there is an important caveat to consider. The open world is an incredible achievement yes, but how well does it serve the game's other mechanics.

Here the picture becomes somewhat less celebratory. You see the game retains its Dark Souls bones, the nuanced, environmental story-telling, the sparse quests driven by occasional meetups. Only now this spans a vastly larger surface and becomes a lot harder to experience naturally. Put simply, a great number of quests are next to impossible to do correctly without a guide. Not because of the minimalist design and lack of compasses, but exactly because of how incongruous the quest design is when applied to an open world. You execute a step in a quest. The character moves. Where? Go figure. In Dark Souls progression was linear and this was not much of an issue. Here, it is.

On gameplay this is largely Dark Souls still. Except significantly easier. This is likely another side-effect of the open world direction. I'm not saying the game is not challenging. Rush ahead and you will be punished. However, the game rewards careful exploration, as it rewards skilful combat. Generously. And there is plenty of rewarding side-content to conquer. Do that enough and by the endgame even superbosses won't be that scary. On a smaller scale, a disappointing element was the complete lack of a swimming ability. So many inviting islands close to the coast that are impossible to reach without gimmicks. The moment your character reaches the darker shade of waters down they go, as if they reached the proverbial end of the world. Be that as it may, this is a competently made and entertaining action game. Given its pedigree, I would expect no less.

Technically speaking the game is less impressive than its stellar art direction might suggest. Its graphical prowess in particular is decent but not extraordinary. There are sloppy technical decisions present that I wouldn't expect to see in a modern game. For example the lack of ultra wide monitor support. Even Dark Souls Remastered had that. But not Elden Ring? What's worse, the game is taking anti-cheating a bit too seriously and attempting to mod the game to include missing functionality can mark you as a cheater. I ended up having to play the game completely offline and with the use of extraneous software that didn't integrate quite seamlessly enough. All so that I could make the game work properly with my setup. FromSoftware really needs to do better here.

To conclude, does Elden Ring deliver on its promise. Is this Dark Souls but open world and is that a good thing? The first question is easy. Yes, it definitely is. The minimalist story telling, the challenging combat, the well crafted world. It's all here. The second question is harder. Dark Souls is not an open world game and making it one required changes that occasionally felt like discounts to the formula. In the end this is a lovingly made game of incredible ambition. It is beautiful and it is flawed. Is it worth playing? Absolutely, it's also worth pondering. There is something truly monumental just a few improvements away.

SILLY LORE/PLAYED BEFORE KNOWLEDGE I never played a soulslike before, and before this game the usual combat in game experiences I had were (in order) Zelda, Baldur’s gate 3, and assasin’s creed Origins. Oh, and some light dragons dogma 2. I heard of Eldenring shortly after completing Baldur’s gate 3 and was intrigued by its alleged amazing worldbuilding handled by none other than George R.R Martin. (known from ASOIAF/GOT) This is kinda how the ball began to roll, but it would take YEARS before I would give Eldenring an actual chance. Mainly: Allegedly, ER is a very massive, beautiful but challenging game. Up until joining an achievement hunter discord, where tons of folks told me just how fun it was and that I should give it a try. Before, my biggest challenge in games used to be that a villager would plant its butt down in animal crossing in the way of my terraforming and oh my, has that issue been overshadowed immensely by Elden Ring.

OVERRAL THOUGHTS ABOUT THE GAME.
DESIGN:
The game is in my honest opinion, but I don’t play many games like this very beautiful designed. The colours seem lush and lifely, while still keeping that wandering between two worlds kinda element, as if you are in a magical place beyond our world. I liked the watergraphics, the grass looked cozy as the guards knocked me face down into it, and I am sure my character enjoyed bleeding out on some of the amazingly beautifully designed bridges. The character creator was also very important to me, and I sure enjoyed making my EldenLord a few times over…maybe too many times…

THE GAME FOR ME
So as I said, the game is unlike anything I played before. From the leveling system, to the rather harsh punishments when you do lose, to the scary enemies and the darker themes this is not a classical game I would normally pick up to play. And maybe that is exactly what made playing this (eventually) so much fun! At the first few times I felt like I was failing really badly and that I’d never make it past the first boss but after help from my friends and kinda learning that soulsgames=dying and learning I slowly became more confident. Bosses that used to send me running, or jumpscares that caused me to close the game became tests to test my reflexes and with every victory (big or small) I regained more and more confidence and learned along the way. The world that once seemed so overwhelmingly big became smaller as I uncovered pieces of the map and once my weapon was upgraded, and I actually paid attention to what a boss does during a fight, I had an absolutely almost unhealthy amount of joy playing it, jumping enemies in a violent way and screaming that their time had come, making concerned neighbors of mine wonder if everything was truly alright. Is ELDENRING for everybody? I think not, but what game truly is? It was a challenge, and it did what it promised. It challenged, it changed the way I’d approach a fight in a game and maybe even me along the way. 

10/10 would recommend.

Elden Ring took everything that made Dark Souls great, dialled it up a bunch of notches, and set you loose in an open world.

I wasn't initially sold on the idea of an Open world souls game, but I can honestly say that I think it was a fantastic move to make the genre accessible to new players. Far more so than in the souls series, if you hit a wall you can often pick another direction very easily, find new dungeons or encounters, and hunt for loot and runes without specifically banging your head against a wall. Pair this with the spirit ashes allowing summons in almost every boss fight that you can level with you, and this is a great starting point for newcomers to the genre.

If you're a veteran, the game has you covered too, since there's no NEED to engage with summons if you don't want to. The fights escalate a lot as the game goes on, and really make you feel rewarded for learning the dances in a way I feel is only beaten by Sekiro in the Genre.

Elden Ring is one of the most impressive open-world RPGs ever made. Developed by FromSoftware and directed by Hidetaka Miyazaki, the game combines the challenging combat the studio is known for with a massive world full of exploration, secrets, and unforgettable encounters.

The Lands Between is a stunning setting that constantly rewards curiosity. Every region feels unique, from haunting castles to underground cities hidden beneath the world. Unlike many open-world games, exploration rarely feels repetitive because there is always something interesting around the next corner.

Combat is deep and satisfying, offering a huge variety of weapons, spells, and character builds. Boss battles are a major highlight, requiring skill, patience, and strategy. While the difficulty can be intimidating for newcomers, the game's open structure allows players to explore elsewhere and grow stronger before tackling tougher challenges.

The story is mysterious and often told through environmental details, item descriptions, and character interactions. This approach won't appeal to everyone, but it creates a sense of discovery that fits the game's world perfectly.

The few drawbacks include occasional performance issues and a lack of traditional quest tracking, which can sometimes leave players confused about where to go next. However, these flaws are minor compared to the overall experience.

Overall, Elden Ring is a masterpiece that successfully blends challenging gameplay, rewarding exploration, and rich world-building. Whether you're a longtime fan of FromSoftware games or a newcomer looking for an unforgettable adventure, it is a game well worth playing.

Recomendação instantânea, não como o sucessor da série Souls, mas como um excelente jogo de aventura.

terraria, oh where do i even start...

At first glance, Terraria looks like “2D Minecraft,” but that comparison honestly doesn’t do it justice. The game evolves from chopping trees and building tiny wooden houses into fighting screen-filling gods, exploring corrupted biomes, crafting hundreds of weapons, and slowly becoming an unstoppable force of destruction. The progression system is insanely satisfying because every boss unlocks new materials, stronger gear, harder enemies, and completely changes how the game feels.

The exploration is phenomenal too. Every world feels alive with hidden caves, floating islands, underground jungles, deserts, oceans, temples, corruption/crimson biomes, and secrets buried literally everywhere. The “just one more cave” effect is dangerously real. I’ve lost hours mining because I kept finding something new.

The boss design is another massive highlight. Early bosses feel manageable, but later fights become absolute chaos in the best way possible. Dodging lasers, explosions, flying swords, and giant eldritch horrors while your screen turns into a fireworks show never stops being exciting.The soundtrack deserves praise too. Some tracks are weirdly relaxing while others perfectly capture the tension of boss fights and dangerous biomes. The music becomes surprisingly nostalgic after enough hours.

Multiplayer makes everything even better. Building giant bases with friends, preparing arenas for bosses, and accidentally summoning something horrific before everyone is ready creates some of the funniest gaming moments imaginable.

At this point, Terraria feels less like a game and more like a massive adventure sandbox that somehow keeps rewarding curiosity no matter how long you play.

Exploring this game as a product of its time and as a milestone of gaming history, it's an interesting experience.

From a modern perspective, and when looking for something fun to play with friends, it's not worth it, and Terraria has been outclassed so severely by its successors that it's not something 

Many aspects of the game pad out its runtime, keeping players busy with mindless work to distract from the lack of content in what is essentially a boss rush game with optional base building stock full of debatably aesthetic choices. 

Modern games that are creating social and creative spaces have figured out that constantly respawning unthreatening enemies padding the game time without adding anything of value are not satisfying either type of player engaging with the game. For people that want to make progress, the event is unrewarding noise, and for the builders and decorators, it's an impediment to creativity.

The events are so samey that the pattern quickly gets grating; if it's a "Pirate Attack", a "Martian Invasion", or a "Blood Moon", it all plays out the exact same way with copy pasted mobs, loot tables, and tons of uninteresting gear offering no choices but the illusion of content. At times the game spawned "quirky" events back to back for hours. The developers have added an endgame item to skip the noisy mess, showcasing that even they don't think it's worthwhile to have them around, but padding.

Combining the above design issues with the outdated controls, graphics, and repetitive shrill noises in a poorly sound-balanced world and its persistent multiplayer issues where connecting to the server fails half the time the game and requires a restart, the game can be rather taxing for what ultimately amounts to a flat 40-hour experience with little care given to a post-endgame experience over the years. 
Being a very "Wiki-forward" experience, with most mechanics hidden from the player, from item stats to basic mechanics, the game expects you to play it while looking things up more so than modern titles.

Terraria is a game I initially disliked a lot, however I have since learned that was a skill issue. This game has easily one of the most in depth and intricate progression systems I have seen in a survival game and I love that about it.

My first experience of Terraria was a cracked alpha file I found on some shady website in late 2011. At the time, the Wall of Flesh had just been added, and it was more than enough for my young mind to content with. I remember staying up all night til sunrise playing that janky version of the game, and adoring it.

Over the years I played the 360 when it released, and the xbox one after that. I've always enjoyed the game, but finally decided to do a full dive on the Steam version. Prior to this "session" I'd only seen the mech bosses. 

What a fun little gem. I used to tell people "its 2D Minecraft!" but its just *not* anymore. Terraria has evolved into its own thing several times over, starting with a similar experience but rapidly evolving into a boss rush with ever increasing rewards. The game does a wonderful job with *growth*, allowing you to feel stronger the more time you put into a given character. Characters also carry over between worlds, allowing you to have an entire network of worlds that interact with one another via the player. 

It's 10$, what are you waiting for?

For the entire period of Early Access, this game requires a paid access (or a friend code). You need to basically purchase $30 worth of microtransactions to get access to this game. Today they finally put the game on 50% off but very sneakily swapped out $30 pack for a $15 pack, called it the exact same thing, and marked it at $30 with a 50% discount. 

No, this is not a 50% discount on a $30 package, this is a new $15 package at full price.

Path of Exile 2 delivers deep character customization, challenging combat, and a much stronger campaign experience than most action RPGs. The visuals, boss fights, and skill system are impressive and rewarding for players who enjoy experimenting with builds. However, the game can feel overwhelming for newcomers, and some progression and balance issues are still noticeable during early access. Overall, it’s a very promising ARPG with excellent gameplay depth, but it may require patience and a willingness to learn its complex systems.

Can we at least acknowledge that this game pretending they're doing a favor by having a free weekend on a game that was advertised as "going to be free" is weird behavior? It's been over a year. This isn't a beta test. They're releasing "major expansions" This is a cashgrab from a greedy company.

I've played every league since early access launched, and at this point the game has become genuinely exhausting. 

The core issue is the campaign and early game. Replaying the same story from scratch every few months is simply not fun, and the early game does nothing to make that easier. Your character feels sluggish, enemies are overtuned for how little your skills do, and the whole thing drags in ways that feel more like friction than challenge. By the time you finally escape into the endgame, you've already resented the journey.

Not recommended, unless GGG fundamentally reconsiders the campaign-replay loop.

Good game. I hate putting a review based on price and such, but once I heard this, I can't ignore it. 

The game was 30-ish USD. You got 300 points ingame to spend on cosmetics. 

I bought the game 50% "on sale". for 15-ish USD. I got 150 points ingame.

They did not sell the game 50% off. They packaged the deal with half the points (that actually hold value) and called it a sale. 

For me personally, I don't care. I see it as I got the game for half price. I just got less "cosmetic points". But this behaviour is not okay. This is a dishonest scam. 
Setting the "sale" to 50% off strongly implies that the same product was previously $30 and is now $15. (WHICH IT EVEN STATES) But the new product contains half of the points. It is NOT the same product. It is a different, less valued product, sold as "The same thing on sale".
That the game is the main product is not a valid argument. I, as a customer, is buying the whole pack. Again, the pack is advertising as discounted from a previous value. It is not. The contents of the pack is different. 
If a store sold me a spoon and 300 grams of icecream, and a pack called 50% off with 150 grams of icecream, claiming "the spoon is the product" or whatnot, I highly doubt that would pass.

Again, I personally don't mind getting the game cheaper. But this behaviour, is not acceptable by any means. This is very dishonest deception, and people who give more of a ♥♥♥♥ than I do, should take action. I expected more from Grinding Gear Games. Disgusting.

Path of Exile 2 is easily one of the most satisfying ARPGs I've played in years. The combat feels impactful, bosses are memorable, and every class has enough depth to keep experimenting with new builds.

Unlike many modern ARPGs that focus on mindless screen-clearing, PoE 2 rewards positioning, timing, and learning boss mechanics. Every upgrade feels meaningful, and finally overcoming a difficult encounter is incredibly satisfying.

The game is still in Early Access, so there are balance issues, performance hiccups, and some systems that need more polish. But the foundation is already excellent. The visuals, atmosphere, soundtrack, and skill system show just how much passion Grinding Gear Games put into this project.

If you enjoy challenging ARPGs with deep character customization and long-term progression, this is absolutely worth playing. Even in its unfinished state, it already feels like one of the strongest competitors in the genre.

Writing this negative review because it is 2026 and we still don't have proper, native Linux support. The developers have been dodging this for years, hiding behind the same old excuses, but let’s be real—the current ecosystem completely allows for it.

With modern Wayland protocols, robust graphics pipelines, and the tools available today, bringing Wallpaper Engine to Linux is entirely doable. The technical limitations they used to complain about simply don’t exist anymore. At this point, it’s not a matter of capability; it’s pure laziness from the dev team.

I honestly believe they are just comfortable collecting money from the massive Windows user base while completely ignoring the growing Linux and Steam Deck community that wants native desktop integration, not just a mobile companion app.

I’ll gladly flip this to a positive review the second the developers stop being lazy and give us proper Wayland support. Until then, it's a hard pass.

I woke up after 50 hours jumping like a frog on some platforms like a turn-based Mario.

There are no tactics. There's a mnemonization of spells, items, and stats. Instead of thinking about which hill to place your shooter on, you wonder how the Magic Brooch of Ass you're wearing affects your rolls against the Sudden ♥♥♥♥ Spell.

They advertise that you can fight with the environment. Okay, you set something on fire, the enemy burns. It took a staggering 5% of their HP. So, back to the Sudden ♥♥♥♥ Spell stats. The only effective way to play the environment is to push them into a cliff, but the developers designed a loot loss to prevent anyone from playing like that. But if they stuck to "realism," the loot from a burned fireball victim would be unusable too.

The characters are crap. Protagonists and antagonists. Most attack both melee and ranged at the same time, making tactics pointless. They're jumping around like monkeys (a turn-based Mario), making it impossible to create any sort of structured gameplay. Battles are chaotic in the worst sense of the word.

The monkeys jump around like in a zoo, but at the same time, there are strict rules for blocking characters. You can't send your tank to the front because your team is blocking it. And it's set up the way it is because scripted dialogue set you up at the beginning of the battle. So, good luck with a tank at the back. This happens many times; you'd have to trigger conversations with the tank to keep it in the front. But why would you choose someone else as your main character if a tank with low charisma is supposed to talk?

Therefore, to do ANYTHING in this game, you need to have online help open because nothing is intuitive. There are difficult games, very difficult games, but intuitive. Where losing is fun. Here, you have great sessions with Gemini and discussions on how to prevent Gale from dying in the first turn. In BG3, losing is frustrating because you have no control over your initial setup, every enemy has a ranged attack, and everyone can jump like a monkey. It ends with you being surrounded and pelting your team with everything they can: arrows, spells, bottles of various substances.

That's what the Viet Cong did to every commando unit they spotted in the jungle in Vietnam. But those commandos were hiding, and my team of clowns has to engage in open battle. Often after swaggering dialogues.

As for the lesser sins, the inventory menu is clunky. Like something from 2000.
The camping system is tedious. Halfway through a dungeon, you have to return to camp because your spells and special moves have run out. Furthermore, camping is clearly at odds with the main plot, which counts down to a specific event in single days. Your camping days are unrelated to this; the dialogues constantly state you have a few days left until the event, but you've already camped a dozen times.

If you grit your teeth and endure what I've described, the game is beautiful. Maybe you're into D&D stats, then this is the game for you. But I had to tell the truth about the gameplay because it was touted as a game with good tactical combat, which it isn't.

In my 54 hour playthrough, I've had the pleasure of receiving 3 black screens as my files repeatedly got corrupted. But why stop there, it also crashed my computer twice out of generosity, specifically on act III. The developers must have smoked one to come up with a spaghetti engine.

Starting this game up is like russian roulette, your game will run for countless sessions but it's inevitable that it will give up and die on you, taking your game files and hours of save files to the void. Not to mention if your session does work, you have the delightful chance of experiencing the game as a deaf person; having all your game audio absent, or as a confused demented person; having quest markers pointing you off the cliff or to a phantom character outside unreachable boundaries of the world itself, making numerous quests tedious.
I am truly convinced this game allows for multiple ways to interact with quests, or skip them, is because one way or another, a part of the quest will certainly break.

Before you even consider dropping 60,- (or whatever your currency is) for this meal filled with broken glass shards, these issues are not exclusive to me, but for countless of consumers who bought this between 2023 and the present. So if you can accept your fate before starting your playthrough, that Krampus will snatch your files from you unannounced, at any given moment in time, then you'll be alright.

I've been trying to give this game a fair chance because I'd heard so many good things about it, but honestly, I just don't think it's that good. To me, it feels like one of those massively hyped games that ended up being far more impressive in reputation than in reality.

The story has been surprisingly difficult to follow and stay invested in, while the mechanics often feel clunky and frustrating. Too many times I've had the game do something I never intended, forcing me to reload a save and replay sections just because the controls or interface got in the way. Instead of feeling smooth and intuitive, it often feels like I'm fighting the game itself.

The characters haven't helped much either. Most of the companions and NPCs are forgettable, and I honestly don't understand the popularity of some of the fan favorites. Astarion feels incredibly overrated and irritating, while Lae'zel spends most of her time coming across as obnoxious and unpleasant. I know a lot of people love these characters, but they simply didn't click with me.

As someone who genuinely loves D&D and runs two games a week, I was expecting something that captured the spirit of the tabletop experience. Instead, this often feels like a game wearing a D&D costume rather than actually embodying what makes D&D special. So many mechanics and design choices just feel off, and the overall experience rarely captures that freedom and excitement that make the tabletop game so enjoyable.

Considering the price, I don't think it's worth paying full price for. If you can pick it up during a major sale, then maybe it's worth giving a shot. Otherwise, I personally wouldn't recommend it at its current cost.

When I was fourteen years old, I played my first RPG: Fallout New Vegas. That game blew my mind at the time, introducing me to a type of experience with is truly unique to the video game medium. I did not think I would ever have an experience like that again as I grew older. Things simply surprise and excite you less, video games especially in my case. I am very glad to say that the feeling I got when I finished Baldur's Gate 3 came very close to the one I had when I wrote my review for Fallout New Vegas 12 years ago. And I'm very glad about that.

Baldur’s Gate 3 stands out as an RPG where every choice genuinely matters. Conversations, major decisions, friendships, and betrayals all shape the direction of your journey, making each playthrough feel different and meaningful.

What impressed me most is the incredible depth and freedom the game offers. There’s rarely a single “correct” way to handle a situation — you can battle your way through, use persuasion, sneak around obstacles, explore hidden paths, or experiment with completely unconventional ideas. More than once, I was surprised that the game actually supported something I didn’t expect to work.

The world feels alive because it responds to your actions. Companions react to your behavior, past decisions come back later, and even minor choices can create major consequences. That level of interactivity and player agency is what truly makes Baldur’s Gate 3 exceptional.

If you enjoy RPGs that reward creativity, freedom, and meaningful decision-making, Baldur’s Gate 3 is an easy recommendation.

Brawlhalla is what happens when someone looked at Super Smash Bros. Ultimate and thought, “What if we removed the soul, the personality, and the budget?”

The game feels like it was assembled entirely out of clip art and unfinished flash animation. Every character moves like they’re skating across an oily cafeteria floor, and somehow every match devolves into the same brain-melting routine: jump, dodge, spam side-air, repeat until your will to live disconnects before the server does.

The roster is massive in the same way a gas station candy aisle is massive: technically a lot of choices, but 90% of it tastes exactly the same. “Legends” are basically recycled animations holding different weapons while wearing costumes designed by someone who thinks neon gradients are a personality trait.

And the community? Imagine if energy drinks gained sentience and learned racial slurs.

The hitboxes are held together by hope and server lag. You’ll get punched from three zip codes away by a guy named “xXShadowWolf420Xx” playing on Wi-Fi powered by a microwave. Every ranked match feels less like competition and more like being trapped in an argument with a malfunctioning blender.

The sound design deserves special recognition for making every weapon impact sound like two plastic rulers colliding in a school locker. Nothing in the game has weight. You don’t win fights — you just out-annoy someone long enough for them to uninstall.

And despite all of this, the game survives because it’s free. Not good. Free. There’s a difference. People don’t play it because it’s the best platform fighter; they play it because downloading disappointment costs nothing.

Still, buried underneath the chaos, spam, and cosmetic overload, there actually is a surprisingly high skill ceiling. Which somehow makes it worse, because now you’re getting obliterated by someone who has spent 4,000 hours mastering cartoon hammer physics in a game that looks like a browser ad from 2014.

If you want a masterclass in psychological warfare masquerading as a free-to-play fighter, look no further. This game takes everything rewarding about fighting games—precision, expression, skill—and aggressively flattens it into a rage-inducing grid of circles and clip-art aesthetics.

Here is the honest truth about this game:

The Illusion of Choice: A roster of 60+ "Legends" sounds great until you realize they are all just stat-swaps. Every single character sharing a weapon type has the exact same light attacks. The only difference is three Signature moves (Sigs). It's not a fighting game roster; it's a glorified weapon-skin simulator.

The Coward Meta: The engine actively rewards running away. With infinite jumps, massive movement options, and the Spot-Dodge, the player who actually tries to fight first loses. High-level matches are just two people bouncing on the walls like caffeinated flies, waiting for the other to get bored and whiff an attack. It's a test of patience, not skill.

The Hitbox Travesty: Don't trust your eyes. Visual animations mean nothing. The game calculates hits using massive, invisible geometric circles. You will literally hear the sound effect of your weapon hitting their face, only for the server to decide their circular hurtbox was a pixel away, leaving you to get instantly punished.

Balance is a Myth: The devs just rotate which weapon gets to be brain-dead every patch. You can spend 20 minutes learning intricate, high-skill Scythe strings and dodge reads, only for some guy with an Axe or Lance to erase your entire health bar by pressing the heavy attack button twice.

Flash Game Visuals: For a game making millions on crossovers, it still looks and feels like a 2012 mobile flash game. The stages are all identical flat blocks with different background wallpaper, and the servers love to hit you with micro-teleports right when you're trying to recovery back to the stage.

Peak Toxicity: Between Sig-spamming, Wall-hugging, and an entire library of toxic post-game emojis designed specifically to tilt you, the player base is weaponized toxic waste.

Final Verdict:
Mastering this game doesn't bring joy; it just means you've learned how to tolerate the absolute nonsense better than the poor soul across from you. Save your sanity. Play literally anything else.

I started playing Garry's Mod last year, and it became one of the best games I have ever played. It changed my life in a way I did not expect.

The reason I started playing it was because my dad banned me from playing Roblox because of my grades. At first, I was upset and did not know what to play. Then I downloaded Steam because I thought it might have some games that were as fun as Roblox. While looking through the store, I found Garry's Mod. I thought, "Wow, this game looks really cool!" I asked my mom if she could buy it for me, and thankfully she did.

When I first started playing, the game was a little difficult for me to understand. There were so many things to learn, and I did not know how to use all the tools. But after spending more time in the game, I started getting better and having a lot of fun. I learned how to build things, use mods, and explore different maps.

What makes Garry's Mod special is that there are almost no limits to what you can do. You can create your own adventures, build amazing things, and play with thousands of community-made addons. Every time I played, I found something new to try.

Garry's Mod changed my life because it helped me discover creativity and problem-solving. Instead of just playing a game, I was making my own fun and learning new things. Even though I started playing it because I could not play Roblox anymore, I am glad that happened because I found a game that I truly enjoy.

Overall, I would highly recommend Garry's Mod to anyone who likes creativity, freedom, and endless possibilities. It is one of the best games I have ever played, and I will never forget the memories I have made in it.

Garry's Mod, sorti en 2006 par Facepunch Studios, n'est pas vraiment un jeu : c'est une boîte à outils basée sur le Source Engine de Valve. Aucun objectif, aucun but, juste un moteur physique, des props HL2/CS:S à spawner et un workshop tentaculaire qui contient quasiment tous les modes possibles et imaginables. 25 millions de copies vendues, sacré par le Guinness en 2024 meilleur PC exclu de tous les temps. À 20 ans, il continue de tourner mieux que la majorité des sorties AAA.

[list]
[*]🧰 La liberté totale, t'as un physgun, un toolgun, et littéralement tout le contenu de Valve à disposition, le seul plafond c'est ton imagination
[*]🎭 Les game modes communautaires sont infinis : Trouble in Terrorist Town, Prop Hunt, DarkRP, Sandbox, Murder, Deathrun, chaque session peut être un jeu complètement différent
[*]🛠️ Le workshop Steam est probablement le plus actif de toute la plateforme, des millions de mods, maps, addons, toujours quelque chose de neuf à tester
[*]👥 Le multi avec des potes est une des meilleures expériences qu'on puisse avoir en 2026, t'allumes un serveur sandbox et tu rigoles pendant 4h sans même savoir comment c'est passé si vite
[*]🧠 Pour les bidouilleurs, le Lua est ouvert et permet de coder ses propres scripts et modes, c'est une vraie école pour pas mal de devs aujourd'hui
[*]💸 Le rapport temps de jeu / prix est absolument indécent, 10€ pour quelque chose de littéralement inépuisable
[/list]

[b]Ce que j'ai moins aimé :[/b]
[list]
[*]🦖 L'âge du moteur Source se sent, l'UI est restée bloquée en 2006, c'est moche et pas intuitif pour un nouveau
[*]🧱 Beaucoup de modes communautaires demandent les assets HL2 ou CS:S sinon tu vois des textures roses/noires partout, faut prévoir
[*]📚 La courbe d'apprentissage en solo sans tuto officiel est velue, il faut accepter d'aller fouiller YouTube pour piger
[*]⚠️ La modération des serveurs publics est aléatoire, prévoir de jouer sur des serveurs whitelistés ou entre potes pour éviter les gosses qui rage en micro saturé
[/list]

[b]Conclusion :[/b]
Garry's Mod est tout simplement un monument du PC gaming. Ce n'est pas un jeu qu'on note, c'est une plateforme. Si tu n'as jamais touché à GMod, sache que tu rates un pan entier de la culture PC : le TTT à 16, les sessions Prop Hunt qui dégénèrent, les builds débiles en sandbox, les RP DarkRP qui durent 6h. À 144h sur le compteur c'est encore peu pour un truc pareil, et il fait partie de ces trois ou quatre jeux qui resteront installés à vie sur ma machine. Indispensable.

Garry’s Mod is a legendary sandbox game that still holds up incredibly well thanks to its massive modding community and endless creativity. With 6.9 hours on record, I’ve only scratched the surface, but it’s clear why so many people keep coming back to it after all these years.

The game gives you total freedom. You can build crazy contraptions, roleplay, make movies, play custom game modes, or just spawn random stuff and cause absolute chaos. The physics are still some of the most entertaining around, and the sheer variety of mods and addons means you can turn the game into almost anything you want.

That said, the game is significantly more fun with friends. Messing around together, building dumb machines, playing horror maps, or just trolling each other creates some of the funniest moments you can have in multiplayer. Solo is good for building and experimenting, but the real magic happens when you play with others.

Even today, Garry’s Mod feels fresh because the community never stopped supporting it. Whether you want serious roleplay, nextbot chases, or just pure nonsense — there’s something for everyone.

If you enjoy creativity, chaos, and playing with friends, Garry’s Mod is an easy recommendation. A true classic that’s still going strong.

Destiny 2 was never just a game to me.

It was a place where time disappeared without warning. A universe filled with late nights, familiar voices, victories that felt bigger than they should have, and silence that sometimes said more than words ever could.

Somewhere along the way, this game taught me things beyond mechanics and raids. It showed me patience, connection, disappointment, and the strange way virtual worlds can leave very real marks on a person.

There are memories here that I will always carry with me, not because they were perfect, but because they were real.

And maybe that is why saying goodbye feels heavier than expected. Because in the end, we are not only leaving behind a game; we are leaving behind versions of ourselves that once existed inside it.

Still, I do not look back with bitterness.

Only gratitude.

For every moment that made this world feel alive.
For every lesson that stayed with me after the screen went dark.
And for the reminder that even temporary places can become permanent memories.

Thank you, Destiny 2.

It's better than 99.99% of the games on Steam. It's simply the best social co-op game I've ever played.

There's nothing better than sitting in a dungeon or on a mission with three friends, or being in a Discord call with six people while tackling a raid. Working together to defeat a boss, executing mechanics, making callouts, chatting between encounters, and sharing the loot afterwards creates an experience that's hard to find anywhere else.

The game isn't just about the gameplay—it's about helping each other, teaching new players, and creating unforgettable memories with friends. Those are the kinds of moments you simply won't get in most other games.

The gunplay is absolutely top-notch. The variety of weapons is incredible, and each one feels unique in the way it looks, sounds, and plays. I've never seen another game offer this much weapon diversity while still making every weapon feel satisfying to use.

The Witch Queen is one of the most iconic campaigns I've ever played. The storytelling, missions, and atmosphere are outstanding, and the cinematics are absolutely stunning. Few games manage to deliver moments on that level.

If you're new and need people to play with, look for LFG Discord servers. Not everyone will be willing to help, but if you mention that you're new and looking for a clan, you'll find plenty of passionate players who are happy to teach, guide, and help you get started. The Destiny community has its bad apples, but it also has some of the most dedicated and welcoming players I've ever met.

On a side note, I don't believe the game is reaching the end of its journey because players stopped caring. In my opinion, it's largely the result of decisions made by the developers over the last two years.

The game was put on the back burner, less money and effort were invested into its development, and resources were redirected elsewhere. On top of that, several questionable design and business decisions hurt the overall player experience and drove people away.

Despite all of that, the core game remains incredible. If the developers had continued to invest in high-quality content, listened more closely to player feedback, and given the game the attention it deserved, I genuinely believe it would still be one of the most-played games on Steam. The demand was always there, and the community was passionate enough to support it. It declined because the support behind it wasn't what it used to be by the developer anymore.

Destiny still offers some of the best co-op experiences, raids, dungeons, gunplay, and memories in gaming. That's why so many players are still hoping for a true successor.

Rainbow Six Siege is a great tactical shooter that has been systematically dismantled by the company that owns it. This isn't a review about the game. It's an autopsy.

THE COMMUNITY

The game is rated M for Mature. That rating exists for a reason — the content is violent, the strategy is complex, and the environment requires a certain level of emotional regulation to function. None of that is present anymore.

The majority of the active playerbase skews young. Not young adults. Young. Kids who aren't supposed to be playing this game, surrounded by adults who've regressed to match them — because that's what the culture rewards now. The older crowd, the people who came for the tactics, the map knowledge, the slow methodical grind of actually learning something, they're still there. But they're outnumbered and they're tired.

This didn't happen by accident.

THE STREAMER PIPELINE

Ubisoft signed a formal contract with an influencer marketing firm to flood the Siege X launch with sponsored creators at scale. They have an official Creators Program with perks, benefits, and commission structures tied directly to in-game purchases. The streamers you're watching aren't fans. They are a marketing channel with a Twitch banner.

The most visible product of this arrangement is Jynxzi — genuinely funny, genuinely talented, and genuinely unaware of how he's being used. Watch his content and you'll laugh. Then slow down and describe what you're actually watching: a grown man performing hysteria for an audience of children on a game those children aren't supposed to be playing. The desk slams. The drooling. The screaming. The pelican noises. Anyone doing that off camera would feel embarrassed. On camera, with a revenue stream attached, it becomes a behavior model — handed directly to kids who absorb it and bring it into your lobbies.

Jynxzi has complaints about the game. Operator balance. Gadget interactions. Things that sound like real feedback. But listen closely and ask yourself — who benefits from the community being focused on operator tuning? Ubisoft does. Because operator tuning is a number change. One person, one afternoon, done. Meanwhile the servers lag, the DDoS protection is a joke, the anti-cheat is a storefront waiting room, and none of that ever gets addressed because fixing infrastructure costs real money and real engineering. Jynxzi's complaints, whether he knows it or not, are the complaints Ubisoft can cheaply answer. The ones that actually matter never make the agenda.

That's not a conspiracy. That's just incentives. And Ubisoft built every one of them.

THE CHANGE LOOP

Here is the cycle: you learn an operator. The operator gets nerfed. You learn a map. The map gets rotated out for three months. You learn a recoil pattern. The recoil gets adjusted. You master a site setup. The destructible geometry gets randomly regenerated. You adapt. Everything changes again.

They present this as development. It isn't. Recoil is a number. Changing it from 2.73 to 2.83 and back to 2.40 based on community reaction isn't engineering — it's one person with spreadsheet access and twenty minutes. A billion-dollar company with years of revenue behind it holds press releases about decimal adjustments and calls it hard work.

The reason nothing stays stable is because stability would allow mastery. Mastery means the older, patient audience can settle in and stop needing new content. And the younger audience — the one that was never supposed to be here — gets bored without constant novelty. So the floor keeps moving. Not to improve the game. To keep people chasing.

THE BOTTOM LINE

Siege is rated M for Mature. It is being actively marketed to audiences it is not rated for, through creators financially incentivized to keep those audiences engaged, while the actual problems — servers, cheating, infrastructure — go unaddressed because fixing them doesn't generate content or clicks.

The game that Siege used to be is still in there somewhere. The bones are good. The tactical foundation is real. But Ubisoft has spent years chasing an audience that was never theirs to have, and in doing so they've driven away the audience the game was actually built for.

They are hanging on by a thread and pulling the wrong direction.

If you're looking for the Siege that was — it's gone. What's left is a lobby full of people who learned how to play from someone who slams his desk for a living.

🧱 🪓 Tom Clancy's Rainbow Six Siege – Originally released back in 2015, this tactical, wall-shattering shooter is one of the most resilient, high-stakes games on the market. It is an absolute masterpiece of competitive tension—but let’s be brutally honest: this game is currently breathing, surviving, and thriving solely because of one single man: Jynxzi.

The Jynxzi Carrying Effect
It is an undisputed fact that the entire modern player base, pop-culture relevancy, and hype surrounding Rainbow Six Siege is being carried completely on the back of Jynxzi. Before he exploded into the king of Twitch and content creation, Siege was slowly coasting into a quiet, forgotten corner of the tactical shooter market.

His unhinged energy, tournament streams, and clip culture single-handedly injected millions of new and returning casual players back into the servers. Jynxzi did more for this game's marketing and active player count than a multi-million-dollar corporate campaign ever could. He is the literal life support machine keeping the lights on in this franchise.

The High-Rank Hellscape: Emerald and Diamond Realities
When you look at the core mechanics, the game is absolutely amazing. The adrenaline rush of pulling off a 1v3 clutch, sound-whoring an enemy through a bullet hole in a soft wall, or perfectly executing a hard-breach strategy is completely unmatched in the FPS world. The tactical depth is practically infinite.

... Unless, of course, you are completely hard-stuck Emerald or Diamond. If you hit this specific tier of MMR, the game transitions from a fun, tactical masterpiece into an absolute toxic, psychological nightmare. High-tier ranked matches are completely infested with cheating, wall-hacking losers on PC, and anti-recoil XIM/Cronus cheaters on consoles who use a mouse and keyboard to obliterate legitimate controller players. The sweat levels are so high and the community ego is so fragile that if you don't play perfectly with a coordinated 5-stack, you are guaranteed to tank your rank and lose your sanity.

Great Devs vs. The Trash Corporate Machine
The ground-level developer team actually deserves an immense amount of credit. Year after year, they continue to push out fantastic seasonal updates, rework classic maps to balance them for modern metas, and introduce innovative operator gadgets that prevent the gameplay from growing stale. They genuinely try to patch the holes in the ship and keep the competitive scene healthy.

The Ubisoft Corporate Tax:
While the actual developers are great, Ubisoft as a corporate entity is completely trash. The suit-and-tie executives continuously poison the well with predatory battle passes, overpriced monthly membership subscriptions, lazy cosmetic marketplace monetization, and an anti-cheat infrastructure that feels like it's held together by duct tape and prayers. The devs build a beautiful tactical machine, but Ubisoft corporate keeps slashing the tires for a quick buck.

🟢 Brilliant, unparalleled tactical gunplay and fully destructible environments that reward real brainpower over raw aim
📣 An incredible community resurgence and meme culture fueled entirely by the streaming phenomenon of Jynxzi
🛠️ Fantastic, consistent seasonal content and operator reworks from a dedicated ground-level dev team
🔴 A completely toxic, miserable experience if you are hard-stuck in Emerald or Diamond due to rampant cheaters and XIM users
🔴 Predictably greedy monetization and terrible corporate subscription decisions from upper management at Ubisoft

8/10 – "A flawless, high-octane tactical masterpiece at its core, but it's a game that is currently running on pure Jynxzi fuel while constantly dodging corporate sabotage from its own publisher."

Rust is a survival game where you start naked on a beach with a rock, a torch, and a dream.

That dream usually lasts about 47 seconds before someone with an AK, night vision, and serious personal issues turns you into a loot bag.

I have spent 5,000 hours playing Rust. In that time, I have built bases, farmed stone, crafted weapons, made alliances, been betrayed, been raided, lost everything, rebuilt, got raided again, and somehow convinced myself that this time will be different.

It never is.

Rust is basically an abusive relationship with extra door codes.

You spend hours building the perfect base, carefully honeycombing it, adding traps, sorting boxes, upgrading walls, feeling like a genius. Then you log in the next day and find a hole where your house used to be and a sign outside saying “thanks for the loot”.

And yet, I keep coming back.

I die a lot.
I build a lot.
I get raided a lot.
I still have no idea how some 12-year-old with a bow has better aim than a trained sniper.

After 5,000 hours, I can confidently say I have not won Rust.

I am not even sure Rust can be won.

Rust is pain, paranoia, farming, betrayal, and waking up at 3am to check if your base still exists.

10/10, would lose everything again.

I installed Rust at 3:14 AM and immediately my refrigerator started speaking Romanian. The loading screen lasted 8 years, but emotionally it felt like a Tuesday. First thing I did was punch a tree, which caused a nearby horse to file taxes under my name.

The gameplay is unbelievable. One minute you’re building a house, the next minute a naked guy named “Soup Dentist” hits you with a rock while reciting ancient microwave frequencies. I tried farming pumpkins but accidentally joined a maritime cult and became mayor of a wolf.

Graphics? Incredible. Every sunset looks like beef jerky flavored depression. The water physics are so realistic I almost drowned in my own keyboard. At one point I heard footsteps behind me in real life and immediately crafted three wooden doors and a furnace.

The community is extremely welcoming if you define “welcoming” as being called a dishwasher by a 9-year-old with military-grade audio equipment. I made one friend but he betrayed me for half a corn and a broken flashlight. Honestly fair trade.

10/10 experience. I no longer trust forests, metal fragments, or human civilization. My doctor says I have “Rust paranoia” and legally cannot own a sleeping bag anymore.

After 20 hours of gameplay, everything starts to feel the same! There’s very little action.

Basically, there are two modes: a fast 5-minute mode and a long 15-minute mode.

1st mode — Fast mode — playing with bots.
Up to level 5, the game feels fine, but after level 5 the bots get destroyed so quickly that you’ll be lucky if you even manage to take down two of them.
So there’s no real enjoyment — 3 minutes of sailing and only 2 minutes of shooting.

2nd mode — Long mode — playing against online players.
You spend 3 minutes sailing into position and 2 minutes fighting, but then all the active players get eliminated, leaving just 5 vs 5 players in a huge ocean.
Those remaining 5 vs 5 players fire maybe once a minute at best!
And for the remaining 7 minutes, they just chase or search for each other to make at least one shot.

You fire once, then wait another minute, and go back to chasing or searching again.
So if you’re looking for intense, action-packed combat, you’ll only get about 2–3 minutes of it. After that, it’s 12 minutes of running away and chasing each other.

I used to enjoy ARK: Survival Evolved when the download size was around 100GB, but now it has become extremely difficult to keep installed because the game size has grown to over 300GB. While the game itself is still fun and full of content, the massive storage requirement makes it nearly impossible for many players with limited disk space to play or even update. It feels frustrating that a game I once could easily download and enjoy is now too large to realistically keep on my system. The optimization and storage demands seriously hurt accessibility for longtime players.

The Only Game Where You Can Get Bullied By A Dodo
I bought this game thinking it would be a majestic, prehistoric adventure where I’d tame a majestic Tyrannosaurus Rex and ride it into the sunset. Instead, my first six hours consisted of spawning completely naked on a beach, punching a palm tree until my hands bled, and instantly getting liquidated by a dinosaur the size of a chicken that spat blinding venom directly into my eyes. ARK isn't just a survival game; it’s an ecosystem designed to remind you that you are at the absolute bottom of the food chain.

The Wake-Up Call (Hour 1 to 50)
The early game experience is pure, unmitigated comedy. You will spend forty-five minutes carefully gathering fiber, thatch, and stones to build a humble 2x2 wooden shack to call home. You'll step inside, feel a profound sense of accomplishment, and look out the window just in time to see a wild Spinosaurus wander down from the mountains. Within three seconds, your house, your bed, your storage boxes, and your physical form will be utterly erased from existence. You don't own property in ARK; you are merely renting it from the local wildlife until they decide they want a snack.

The Absolute Absurdity of Taming
The taming mechanic in this game is wild if you think about it from an outside perspective. To make a dinosaur your best friend, you have to beat it unconscious with a club, shove a hundred berries or raw meat down its throat while it’s asleep, and sit next to it for three actual, real-life hours protecting it from getting bitten by a passing raptor. If you leave for five minutes to get a glass of water, a random bug will bite your future pet, ruining its taming effectiveness, and you'll have to start the whole existential crisis over again. But the moment you finally put a saddle on a Triceratops, you forget all the trauma.

Alpha Tribes and the PvP Nightmare
If you are brave—or foolish—enough to play on an official PvP server, you are entering a digital wasteland ruled by "Alpha Tribes." These are groups of players who have not seen sunlight or touched grass since 2015. You will spend two weeks secretly building a base hidden deep in the jungle, feeling completely safe. You'll log off to go to sleep, and when you log back in the next morning, you'll find a single floating bag of your items on the ground. A mega-tribe flew over your base on a mutant, neon-green Wyvern at 3:00 AM and leveled your entire life's work with plasma cannons just because they were bored.

A Masterpiece of Bugs and Optimization
You cannot write a review for ARK without mentioning the technical wizardry holding it together. Your computer's cooling fans will sound like a jet engine trying to take off from an aircraft carrier. You will experience lag spikes that send you back in time, dinosaurs clipping halfway through solid stone walls, and your own character getting permanently stuck inside a rock because you walked at a weird angle. Yet, despite the fact that it takes up half of your hard drive space and crashes if you look at a waterfall too hard, you will still log right back in.

Final Verdict: 10/10. It is a gruelling, unforgiving simulator that will make you hate lizards, birds, and your own internet connection. Buy it immediately, get chased across a beach by a pack of Raptors, and join the cult.

An amazing game that's constantly updated, done almost entirely by a single developer until coding for multiplayer became a bit too tedious. When I say everything, I mean absolutely everything. The music, the graphics, the coding, which is a feat in and of itself. There's plenty of videos detailing the biography behind the development of this game, one more inspiring than the next.

The game itself is simple yet unique and can get intrinsically complex with how to optimize or simplify things. There's plenty of ways to make money, solidify relationships, or simply enjoy music and events in the game.

In essence, it's a farming game that plays on old Harvest Moon vibes, with much more in almost every aspect. This has a niche for every style of gameplay you could ask for. My nieces play it in my car. My Uncles play it in their free time, we always compare our farms whenever we have family gatherings. There's a magic to it that encapsulates every audience, whatever you choose to do in game.

Stardew Valley is somehow considered one of the most relaxing games ever made, but after playing long enough you realize it’s actually just an anxiety simulator disguised as a cute farming game.

People say:
“Just chill and enjoy the farm life.”

Meanwhile the game has you speedrunning your entire existence every single day because apparently 14 in-game minutes pass every time your character blinks.

Wake up.
Water crops.
Feed animals.
Check machines.
Collect eggs.
Talk to villagers.
Mine rocks.
Fish.
Forage.
Donate museum items.
Upgrade tools.
Rush home before 2 AM or collapse like a Victorian child dying from exhaustion.

Very relaxing.

The game constantly pretends to be cozy while secretly turning players into optimization addicts. Nobody starts Stardew Valley saying:
“I need maximum sprinkler efficiency and ancient fruit wine profit margins.”

But 30 hours later people are calculating crop rotations like they’re managing an agricultural hedge fund.

And the stamina system in early game is torture.
You chop down three trees and your farmer already looks ready for hospice care. Want to clear your farm?
Too bad.
Your character gets exhausted faster than someone climbing stairs after eating an entire pizza.

The villagers are also weirdly overrated.
Everyone talks about how “deep” and “heartwarming” they are, but half the town acts rude until you bribe them with vegetables twice a week.

“Oh wow thanks for giving me this random flower.
Now I no longer hate your existence.”

Groundbreaking social design.

And somehow every villager has the same schedule complexity as a CIA operative. Miss someone by 10 minutes?
Congratulations, now they’ve vanished into another dimension until tomorrow.

Fishing is another crime against humanity.
People either love it or want to throw their monitor through a wall. Why is catching a tiny fish harder than landing a spacecraft? Early-game fishing feels like wrestling a possessed bottle cap using pure disappointment.

Then there’s the mines.
Which are somehow:

* repetitive
* stressful
* grindy
* and weirdly mandatory

Because eventually the game stops being:
“cute farming adventure”
and becomes:
“industrial resource extraction simulator.”

Your beautiful little farm slowly transforms into a capitalist machine full of kegs, furnaces, preserve jars, crystalariums, and enough automation to concern labor unions.

And don’t even get me started on Pierre.
The entire community collectively agrees that Pierre sucks, and honestly that’s probably the most emotionally united the playerbase has ever been.

The calendar system is pure psychological warfare too.
Forget one birthday and suddenly an NPC dislikes you for the next century because you didn’t hand them mayonnaise on exactly the correct Tuesday.

The game also pressures you constantly with seasonal timing:

* missed crop season
* missed event
* missed fish
* missed recipe
* missed bundle item

Oops.
Guess you wait an entire in-game year now.

Fantastic.

And despite the cute graphics, the gameplay loop becomes disturbingly repetitive:

* wake up
* harvest crops
* refill machines
* organize inventory
* sleep
* repeat until real life seasons change too

The soundtrack is amazing though.
Which honestly feels intentional because the music is probably the only reason players don’t realize they’ve spent 6 real-life hours optimizing blueberry production.

The craziest thing about Stardew Valley is that it successfully convinces millions of people that doing farm chores after work is somehow a form of relaxation.

You’re not escaping responsibilities.
You’re just replacing your real responsibilities with pixel responsibilities.

The Forest as a game is a mystery to me - because I simply can not understand how an unfinished unoriginal game that has nothing impressive in it can go out of early access and still get this much praise.
[list]
    [*] I will start with the most obvious problem: basebuilding. While the game advertises itself with “build a base at day, defend it at night”, the base in question is broken on each and every level. First, building it takes a ridiculous amount of time because of the volume of the required resources. To make things worse - cutting all those trees to make a base makes the local population more aggressive and attacks on your base more often and as a result - instead of being more safe you end up being less safe with a base. The next reason why your base sucks that much is the fact that all of the things you build can be destroyed just in a couple of hits by some monsters or in under a minute by a group of cannibals - and destroyed buildings don’t just drop their materials - they disintegrate forcing you to waste even more time on logging and building. And the last problem with the base makes the whole situation just ridiculous - you don’t even need a base, as enemies are incapable of following you into caves or onto the boat where you can sleep freely and securely.
    [*] Survival elements. I will never stop hating survival systems where the food and water are abundant and extremely easy to get, but the bars run out so fast that you are constantly nagged with hunger and thirst. And just so you understand how easy the food situation is here - you can forage for berries, hunt for wildlife or eat your enemies - and each of those options can sustain you easily, not to mention that you can combine them however you like.
    [*] Combat. Oh god, I haven’t seen that much jank in a while. Sure, on one hand locals behave in an interesting way, with their running, screaming from a distance and everything, but when it comes to a fight having those lunatics run in and out without doing anything while you are incapable of catching up to them becomes annoying extremely fast. It becomes even more annoying when the same exact type of enemies either dies in 2 or 20 hits. It becomes infuriating when said enemies decide to ignore the staggering effect of your attacks and stunlock you with an unending flurry of blows. It could be tolerable if said combat was rare, but sadly you can barely pass a day without some lunatic trying to kill you or ruin your base. Combat just sucks and there is no other way to put it.
   [*] Content for content's sake. This game has a lot of stuff that is simply a waste of time. For example - there is a snow biome and you can waste a lot of time crafting frost protection gear. Sadly the snow biome itself has nothing in it - so all of that collecting was a waste of time.  Another example - there are 4 axes in the game. Even after finishing the game I can’t really tell if they had any difference between them or was it done just to make a list of tools bigger. Basebuilding too falls into that category - there is simply no use or function for it. I assume that at least some of those things are caused by an early release with some ideas gutted - but we will never know for sure.
   [*] Inventory. Sure, having all of the objects visible on a blanket is a neat way of showing what you have - if you are not holding 50 different items. Now imagine that you do - sticks, stones, spears, bows, aforementioned 4 axes, 5 types of hides, a dozen types of berries, plants and herbal mixes - and now try to find anything in this cluttered mess. Yes, with time you do memorize where your things are, at least those that you use often. Finding stuff that you use rarely becomes increasingly annoying the further you go into the game.
   [*] Exploration/spelunking. I get it, there are places even IRL with vast cave systems. I get it, the caves are dark without a proper source of light. What I don’t get at all - why isn't there a proper source of light in your whole god forsaken game? Lighter gives no light and goes off every few seconds. Flashlight is so bad that I could get more light from a single LED. If said caves were some kind of side content - it would be one thing, but it is your main plot route, how come exploring this route is inconvenient? Not to mention that there are a lot of places with nothing in it - like long swimming sections that serve only as a gear check to make sure you got a required item to pass - navigating those is not fun and is, in fact, extremely boring.
   [*] Zero direction. In most other games this would not be a problem. However in this one you are supposed to go through caves in a certain order - the first one has a map and a compass, the next few give you  tools that are required to get through the further caves. Sadly, with zero directions, we often entered caves that we cannot finish without items that we haven’t found at that point, and just as a cherry on top - we found the first cave (the one with the map) as our last cave. If your only chance to get the correct order is to check wiki - sorry, this is a bad game design.
   [*] Plot/world. On paper you have one. But the more you see the more questions you have and the less coherent the story becomes. Ancient artifact from dead space that can resurrect people? Ok, how does it explain the fact that there is an entire tribe of cannibals here? Or the fact that someone hauled hundreds of dead people into the caves? And where did those people even come from, why don’t their bodies decompose? It just feels like different bits were thrown into the game to give it a creepy vibe with a “figure it out yourself” attitude because doing so was much easier than making a believable story and world.
[/list]
Overall, I just can’t find even one thing in this game that was properly made. The whole experience was between boring and frustrating, I don’t think that I would even finish this game if I wasn’t playing with a friend. Don’t bother with this one and just go buy something that doesn’t suck.
