I enjoyed the first few hours but then I quickly came to find the gameplay loop a little too annoying to enjoy myself any longer. I don't really understand how this has such good reviews. There were some things i like and disliked about this. 

The Bad:
- The food and energy run out way too quickly and yet despite this there is an overabundance of food and water that is constantly respawning. It just turns it into an annoying tedious thing I have to constantly deal with. 
- Most of my time was spent walking around in caves which are scarry because they are filled with cannibals and eldritch horror monsters but I just can't see a thing while I'm fighting them, so again it is annoying. 
- There isn't really a reason to build bases other than to satisfy your creativity. The enemies just easily destroy your buildings like its nothing and you can actually accidentally hit your own building very easily if you are fighting cannibals near it. 
- I didn't finish the story but what i did play felt unintuitive to progress and not particularly interesting.
- The combat is terrible. it is very difficult to hit enemies with your weapons and basic enemies take something like 20 spear stabs to kill. Despite this, the enemies are laughably easy to kill because you just side strafe all their attacks. 
- you are constantly bombarded by patrols of cannibals so you never get time to rest, explore, and build. 
- Exploration wasn't very rewarding, I swam out to an interesting looking island only be rewarded with absolutely nothing being there.

The Good:
- The crafting system is interesting because there are no crafting recipes, just you jamming a bunch of stuff together until you find out what combines into what. 
- The game definitely has some scary moments.

Path of Exile 1 offers a huge amount of content, but for me, it ultimately becomes more frustrating than enjoyable.

One of its strengths is the freedom in the endgame. You can choose the type of content you want to engage with. For example, I personally enjoy tower defense mechanics, so I like playing Blighted Maps. The variety is definitely there, and compared to the campaign, which you have to replay over and over again, the endgame at least gives you some sense of control. The campaign itself is repetitive and annoying, but at least it is relatively short.

However, the real problem begins once you try to progress further into the endgame. At some point, the game becomes extremely difficult to push forward. I constantly hit the same invisible wall that I simply cannot overcome. Whether it is due to a lack of currency, inefficient builds, or just not being strong enough, progression feels blocked in a very discouraging way.

For example, I have never even managed to encounter Sirus, which highlights how inaccessible parts of the game can feel. No matter what I try, I end up stuck in the same loop without meaningful progress. It creates a sense that unless you invest an enormous amount of time or deeply optimize every detail, you cannot fully experience the game.

For a casual player, Path of Exile 1 is simply not very welcoming. While the game offers depth and freedom on the surface, in practice it can feel punishing, restrictive, and exhausting rather than fun.

Payday 2 is still the best game in the Payday series and remains one of the most fun co-op heist games ever made. With 12.9 hours on record, I quickly remembered why this game has such a strong legacy even after all these years.

The gameplay loop is excellent — planning and executing heists, going loud or going stealth, and working together with your crew creates incredibly satisfying moments. The sheer amount of content is massive thanks to years of DLCs, which add tons of weapons, masks, maps, and heists. There’s always something new to try or grind for.

It’s especially great with friends. The chaos when a stealth run goes loud, the clutch moments, and the yelling over voice chat make it an absolute blast in co-op. Even today, Payday 2 feels better than Payday 3 for many players.

The only real downside is that the game can be quite buggy at times, and some mechanics feel dated. However, the fun factor easily outweighs those issues.

If you enjoy co-op heist games and don’t mind the occasional jank, Payday 2 is still highly recommended. A true classic that delivers when played with friends. 💰🔫👍

Developed and published by MY.GAMES, Warface: Clutch is a free-to-play military first-person shooter that spent years establishing itself as one of the most recognizable online FPS titles in the free-to-play market. Built around fast-paced firefights, cooperative operations, competitive multiplayer modes, and an extensive arsenal of modern weaponry, the game attempts to deliver a complete shooter experience without requiring an upfront purchase. Its longevity in an extremely competitive genre is a testament to the strength of its core gameplay, even as it faced challenges throughout its lifespan.

At its heart, Warface: Clutch is designed around accessible but engaging combat. The shooting mechanics are responsive, weapons feel distinct from one another, and firefights reward both mechanical skill and tactical awareness. Whether engaging enemies at long range with precision rifles or pushing objectives with close-quarters weapons, the combat system consistently delivers satisfying encounters. The gunplay strikes a comfortable balance between arcade action and military-inspired realism, making it approachable for newcomers while still offering enough depth to keep experienced players invested.

One of the game's defining features is its class-based structure. Rather than giving every player identical capabilities, Warface: Clutch divides combat roles into specialized classes. Riflemen serve as versatile frontline fighters capable of supporting teammates with ammunition, Medics excel in close-range combat while reviving allies, Engineers maintain armor and equipment, Snipers provide long-distance elimination, and the heavily armored SED units introduce a unique combat style focused on durability and sustained firepower. This system encourages cooperation and ensures that team composition can significantly influence the outcome of both cooperative missions and competitive matches.

The competitive multiplayer modes form a major pillar of the experience. Players participate in a variety of match types that test aim, teamwork, and map awareness. The action is generally fast and intense, with short respawn times and constant opportunities for engagement. Matches rarely feel slow, and the game's movement mechanics allow players to remain active and aggressive. For fans of traditional military shooters, the multiplayer component successfully captures the excitement of quick, objective-focused battles.

Where Warface: Clutch separates itself from many competitors is through its emphasis on cooperative content. The game offers a substantial selection of PvE missions and larger special operations that place teams against AI-controlled opponents. These missions often feature scripted events, defensive objectives, and challenging encounters that require coordination between players. The cooperative content provides an excellent alternative for those who prefer teamwork against computer-controlled enemies rather than direct competition against other players.

Special Operations are among the most memorable activities available. These longer missions often introduce unique environments, larger enemy forces, and more demanding objectives than standard cooperative scenarios. Success depends on communication, class synergy, and careful resource management. These missions help diversify the overall experience and prevent the game from becoming solely focused on competitive multiplayer.

Weapon variety is another area where the game performs exceptionally well. The arsenal contains a large collection of rifles, pistols, shotguns, sniper rifles, machine guns, and specialized equipment inspired by real-world military hardware. Each weapon category offers a distinct feel and role, giving players considerable freedom when building loadouts. The extensive customization options further expand this flexibility, allowing players to tailor weapons to suit their preferred combat style.

Progression is structured around unlocking equipment, improving classes, and obtaining new gear. The constant stream of rewards provides a sense of advancement that encourages continued play. New weapons, cosmetic items, attachments, and upgrades offer long-term objectives, helping maintain engagement over extended periods. Players who enjoy collecting equipment and refining loadouts will find plenty of content to pursue.

Visually, Warface: Clutch remains respectable despite its age. Detailed weapon models, military-themed environments, and solid visual effects contribute to a convincing battlefield atmosphere. While it no longer competes with the latest AAA shooters in terms of graphical fidelity, the presentation remains functional and appealing. More importantly, performance tends to remain stable across a broad range of hardware, helping maintain accessibility for a large audience.

The game's greatest strengths are somewhat offset by recurring issues that have followed it throughout much of its existence. Monetization has frequently been a point of contention among players, particularly regarding access to premium weapons and equipment. Some members of the community felt that certain purchases provided competitive advantages that impacted overall balance. While dedicated players could still succeed through skill and experience, these concerns remained a persistent topic of discussion.

Technical challenges occasionally affected the experience as well. Server performance, matchmaking quality, and balancing issues sometimes created frustration within the community. Despite these problems, the underlying gameplay systems remained strong enough to sustain a loyal player base for many years, which speaks to the quality of the core combat design.

What ultimately made Warface: Clutch successful was its ability to offer a broad range of content within a single free-to-play package. Players could spend time in competitive PvP matches, tackle cooperative missions with friends, experiment with different classes, and pursue progression goals without feeling restricted to a single activity. This versatility gave the game a wider appeal than many shooters that focused exclusively on one style of gameplay.

Warface: Clutch stands as a noteworthy example of a long-running online shooter that managed to combine class-based combat, cooperative missions, competitive multiplayer, and extensive customization into a cohesive experience. While monetization controversies and technical limitations prevented it from reaching the highest level of acclaim, its enjoyable gunplay, diverse content, and strong cooperative systems helped it remain relevant for years. For fans of military-themed FPS games, it delivered an entertaining and content-rich experience that successfully balanced accessibility with depth.

Hi... I am writing this review after playing for over 100 hours, and I can confidently say that GTA IV has the best story in the entire GTA franchise. I loved the game so much that I grinded out 100% of the Steam achievements.

Everyone should play this masterpiece at least once to see what Rockstar cooked up before GTA V. However, if you are playing on a modern PC, there are a few things you need to know:

1. DOWNLOAD FUSIONFIX:
Before you even start the game, download the FusionFix mod pack. The original PC port is notoriously broken on modern hardware. If you play with an uncapped framerate (anything above 60 FPS), the game engine physically breaks. Quick-time events fail, minigames (like the dancing in the DLC) become impossible to beat, and the final mission can permanently soft-lock. FusionFix patches these engine bugs, fixes the lighting, and gives you a smooth, glitch-free experience.

2. THE DLCs ARE MANDATORY:
Just completing the base game with Niko is not enough. You absolutely need to play the two included DLC episodes (The Lost and Damned and The Ballad of Gay Tony). They aren't just extra missions; they contain massive, overlapping stories that directly connect to the main game. Playing all three campaigns is the only way to experience the complete story of Liberty City.

3. THE DRIVING PHYSICS:
Be prepared for the driving to feel very different if you are coming from GTA V. The cars here are heavy and have realistic suspension. It takes an hour or two to get used to, but once you master it, the car chases are some of the most satisfying in the series.

100% highly recommended. If you love open-world games with gritty, deep narratives, this is an absolute must-play.

[h2]Nota Geral | Overall Rating[/h2]
⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐

[b]GTA IV é um daqueles jogos que aparecem uma vez por geração e redefinem o que um mundo aberto pode ser. A Rockstar não estava interessada em entregar o maior mapa ou o maior número de atividades. Ela estava interessada em contar uma história de verdade, dentro de um mundo que respira, reage e pune. Niko Bellic não é um protagonista genérico de sandbox. Ele é um imigrante com trauma de guerra tentando sobreviver em uma cidade que devora quem chega acreditando no sonho americano. A física de ragdoll, a direção de veículos com peso real e um enredo que faz cada missão importar fazem desse jogo um marco que o gênero ainda não superou.[/b]

[i]GTA IV is one of those games that comes around once a generation and redefines what an open world can be. Rockstar wasn't interested in delivering the biggest map or the most activities. It was interested in telling a real story, inside a world that breathes, reacts, and punishes. Niko Bellic is not a generic sandbox protagonist. He is a war-scarred immigrant trying to survive in a city that devours those who arrive believing in the American dream. The ragdoll physics, the vehicle handling with real weight, and a story that makes every mission matter make this game a landmark the genre has yet to surpass.[/i]

[h2]Jogabilidade | Gameplay[/h2]
⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐

[b]Os carros têm peso. Cada modelo reage de forma completamente diferente ao asfalto molhado de Liberty City, e uma curva mal calculada em alta velocidade não termina num drift bonito. Termina num capotamento. O motor Euphoria torna a física orgânica e imprevisível, mudando completamente como você planeja uma fuga da polícia. O ragdoll do Niko e dos NPCs é no mesmo nível. Quando o Niko é atingido por um carro em alta velocidade, o corpo reage com um realismo que choca na primeira vez e continua impressionando na centésima. Os NPCs voam, tropeçam, batem contra grades. Nada parece animação engessada. O único ponto negativo é a ausência total de uma oficina de tunagem. Você dirige carros com uma física extraordinária mas não pode modificar nada neles. Para quem vinha do San Andreas, isso dói.[/b]

[i]The cars have weight. Every model reacts completely differently to Liberty City's wet asphalt, and a poorly calculated turn at high speed doesn't end in a clean drift. It ends in a rollover. The Euphoria engine makes the physics organic and unpredictable, completely changing how you plan a police escape. Niko's ragdoll and the NPCs' are on the same level. When Niko is hit by a car at high speed, the body reacts with a realism that shocks you the first time and keeps impressing the hundredth. NPCs fly, stumble, slam into railings. Nothing feels like canned animation. The only downside is the complete absence of a tuning shop. You drive cars with extraordinary physics but can't modify anything on them. For those coming from San Andreas, that stings.[/i]

[h2]Duração | Duration[/h2]
⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐

[b]GTA IV é generoso sem ser excessivo. A campanha mantém qualidade narrativa do início ao fim sem recorrer a missões de enchimento. As atividades secundárias, como os programas de rádio e as saídas com os amigos, adicionam camadas ao mundo sem parecer conteúdo obrigatório para inflar o contador de horas. As DLCs The Lost and Damned e The Ballad of Gay Tony entregam histórias paralelas que se cruzam com a campanha principal de um jeito que poucos jogos conseguem fazer funcionar.[/b]

[i]GTA IV is generous without being excessive. The campaign maintains narrative quality from beginning to end without resorting to filler missions. The side activities, like the radio programs and outings with friends, add layers to the world without feeling like mandatory content to inflate the hour counter. The DLCs The Lost and Damned and The Ballad of Gay Tony deliver parallel stories that intersect with the main campaign in a way few games can pull off.[/i]

[h2]Enredo | Story[/h2]
⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐

[b]A história de Niko Bellic não é uma fantasia de poder. É um conto de consequências. Cada decisão é movida por desespero, lealdade mal colocada e uma busca por redenção que o jogo nunca resolve de forma satisfatória. E é exatamente isso que faz a narrativa funcionar. O Roman é um dos personagens secundários mais bem escritos do gênero, e a relação entre os dois primos carrega o jogo emocionalmente do começo ao fim. Os antagonistas são credíveis porque o jogo leva tempo para construí-los antes de usá-los. O final, independente da escolha que você faz, deixa uma cicatriz. E isso é o sinal de que uma história foi contada de verdade.[/b]

[i]Niko Bellic's story is not a power fantasy. It is a tale of consequences. Every decision is driven by desperation, misplaced loyalty, and a search for redemption the game never resolves satisfyingly. And that is exactly what makes the narrative work. Roman is one of the best-written supporting characters in the genre, and the relationship between the two cousins carries the game emotionally from beginning to end. The antagonists are credible because the game takes its time building them before using them. The ending, regardless of the choice you make, leaves a scar. And that is the sign that a story was truly told.[/i]

[h2]Visual[/h2]
⭐⭐⭐⭐

[b]Liberty City é uma conquista de direção de arte. A recriação de Nova York tem identidade visual própria, com a sujeira dos bairros periféricos, a iluminação noturna de Algonquin e a diferença palpável entre cada ilha. O jogo carrega a sua idade em certas texturas, mas a atmosfera cinza e pesada ainda funciona como pretendida. A chuva escorrendo pelas fachadas, o nevoeiro sobre o rio, os reflexos no asfalto molhado constroem um ambiente sem precisar de resolução 4K para convencer.[/b]

[i]Liberty City is a triumph of art direction. The recreation of New York has its own visual identity, with the grime of the outer boroughs, the nighttime lighting of Algonquin, and the palpable difference between each island. The game carries its age in certain textures, but the gray and heavy atmosphere still works as intended. Rain on facades, fog over the river, reflections on wet asphalt build an environment without needing 4K resolution to convince.[/i]

[h2]Audio[/h2]
⭐⭐⭐⭐

[b]O design de áudio é preciso onde mais importa. O som dos carros muda conforme a velocidade, o impacto dos ragdolls tem peso físico, e os tiros têm uma secura que combina com o tom realista do jogo. O grande destaque é o rádio. As estações de GTA IV são excepcionais, com curadoria que vai do eastern europeu do Vladivostok FM ao hip hop do The Beat 102.7, e cada uma constrói uma camada a mais da identidade de Liberty City. A dublagem do Niko, feita por Michael Hollick, entrega um personagem que convence nos momentos de violência e nos de vulnerabilidade. É um trabalho vocal que sustenta a narrativa inteira.[/b]

[i]The audio design is precise where it matters most. The sound of cars changes with speed, the ragdoll impacts carry physical weight, and gunshots have a dryness that fits the game's grounded tone. The standout is the radio. GTA IV's stations are exceptional, with a curation ranging from the Eastern European flavor of Vladivostok FM to the hip hop of The Beat 102.7, each one adding another layer to Liberty City's identity. Niko's voice acting, performed by Michael Hollick, delivers a character that convinces in moments of violence and vulnerability alike. It is vocal work that carries the entire narrative.[/i]

As an avid fan of ARPGs, I really like this game, and will continue playing it from season to season, but as it stands I cannot recommend it.
There are a couple of points that are very favourable, some big problems, and some other aspects that seem to be causing friction right now that I would like to address from a personal perspective. Let's start with the controversial:

[h2] Monetization [/h2]
[b]The game currently follows an unsustainable business model[/b] (I think. I don't have an earnings report).
Paying once for the full game with seasonal content that sticks post-season, bug-fixes, QoL improvements, drawn cinematics, voice-acting, marketing and a bunch of other things is at no point reasonable. I don't think that development can keep going indefinitely with no predictable influx of funds.

[b]The in-game cosmetic store is not the solution.[/b] I like that they went beyond armor cosmetics and made skins for skills, minions, portals and others, but in an isometric mostly single-player game, PAID cosmetics are the last thing most players think about, and given that, over long periods of time the earnings are greatly tied to how much is a minority willing to pay for cosmetics, which does not work with a declining playerbase (WHICH IS EXPECTED FROM MOST GAMES MIGHT I ADD AND PERFECTLY NORMAL). I love the game and have paid for a small amount of cosmetics, but [b]I rather have a transmog system considering how cool many of the game's items look[/b], and I'm pretty sure the reason there is no transmog system is because currently the only way to change your appearance is changing your equipment or purchasing cosmetics.

I have always personally prefered the idea of paying for a game, and then have paid expansions released over time. That way development in the base game still continues, but you have certain "checkpoints" over the years in which that continued development gets funded through the expansions. Now, although I would be willing and happy to pay for an expansion, there are many other points that I think need addressing first, and if the current business model does indeed work on the long run, great.

[h2] Bugs and Annoyances [/h2]
Currently the game is shock-full of bugs, and here is a modest list of some I have found over the last TWO WEEKS:
[list]
    [*]Haruspex Orian got stuck and portalling out and back didn't fix it
    [*]The "+X attributes if you have 7 corrupted items" doesn't apply if the last item you equipped is the item with that affix
    [*]The old bug of items appearing duplicated and then either appearing in the correct position or straight up vanishing forever (which I assume is a syncing problem in a system with full serverside authority) is now 2 years old YIPPEE
    [*]The "elite" enemies that revive after a couple of seconds sometimes appear completely invisible
    [*]I'm pretty sure I just saw one of those elites that summon a copy at 50% health summon 3 copies instead
    [*]Movement and collision issues (which once again, I'm sure has to do with serverside authority, and has also been an issue from the start)
    [*]The written dialogue between Majasa and Apophis always stops at the same part, while the voiced dialogue moves on
    [*]Unlike the entirety of the game's cinematics and events, the speech that Apophis gives cannot be skipped, but it does allow you to do all sorts of inputs, allowing you to open every menu and interact with the UI while it is hidden.
[/list]
There are many others I have found but cannot quite recall right now, as well as typos, and some other annoyances like:
[list]
    [*]The Weaver Tree's nodes with imprinted items allow the player to imprint invalid items which means no similar item will be dropped, and the player is not notified at all of this. It shouldn't allow it in the first place.
    [*]The player has no control over what points a skill loses when unequipping a "+X to Y skill" affix
    [*]The Druid has no control over which companion disappears if you have the node to summon a Spriggan when transforming
    [*]Etc etc etc...
[/list]

[h2] The Good [/h2]
Considering all the negatives I just said, how come I love the game? Well, because I find it fun, rewarding and correctly unbalanced.
Here are my favourite points:
    [*]The upgrade system is very flexible, allowing for some really insane and rewarding combinations of affixes, limited by the right amount of luck to keep you trying for a better item again and again.
    [*]The classes and subclasses are both sufficiently distinct and versatile.
    [*]The map's (the literal drawn map you open with [M]) transition between epochs is mesmerizing, and I have spent a good few hours just looking at it and talking about how cool it looks.
    [*]The main characters in the Divine Era are really cool, and getting to know their fate is heartbreaking
    [*]The combat is really dynamic and fun. Hits have impact and overwhelming power is enjoyable.
    [*]Boss fights are really iconic, understandable and fun. I still remember the struggle with Lagon in Hardcore season 1, and still prepare for it season after season
    [*]The "snapshot" mechanic the Possessed Armor has when summoned is really fun to play around
    [*]The seasonal content is great, and I really appreciate that it sticks around instead of being replaced/removed
    [*]I love dinosaurs
    [*]I also love cosmic horror
    [*]I also really love the body horror of the Imperial Era
    [*]I also really really love the more grounded "whimsy" of the Divine Era
    [*]The loot filter is great
Overall, the gameplay, the progression, the upgrades, the story, the visuals, the theme, the UI and pretty much the entire foundation of the game I find to be rock solid and great. It has all the potential to be absolutely amazing, but sadly the negatives previously mentioned contrast too much with the rest of the game.

To have such a fun and cool game be tainted by such stupid details is pretty sad, and I really do wish the best for the game and the developers. I will keep supporting and enjoying the game over the years, but many things need to be ironed out before I can recommend it to anyone else.

After almost 10 years, there's still no attack-move option ( See Grim Dawn).

Additionally, the devs decided to arbitrarily delete a very interesting part of the game: building characters around procs.

The standard defensive and offensive tools the game provides are, by themselves, really weak. This is fine in a vacuum (albeit very boring), since you can finish the campaign with them, and special items like sets, uniques, and special idol effects would be expected to bridge this gap as the player delves into end-game.
Buuuut, some skills are extremely over-represented, while many others skills have nothing, or as mentioned at the start - what little they had has been completely destroyed since 1.3

As an example for combat skills, with reflect you can kill most anything before it has a chance to move, all that needs to happen is for the thing to look at you to oneshot itself.
  By contrast, thorns totem, an otherwise completely trash and useless skill on its own, had an amazing and fun interaction with itself and a few other skills where you could proc it to spawn when hitting something, then use the spawned totem as fodder for other skills, sometimes as often as 50 times per second. No other supporting items (after almost a decade) so the viability was poor, but it was fun. The devs randomly capped this to once per second, completely killing the skill.

Some of the various defensive tools the player has, like dodge, armor, crit mitigation and block protect only from half of the damage sources, so the player is fully unprotected against the rest. Life scales very poorly. Endurance is now heavily restricted and capped. Resistances effectively set your protection to 0, else you would take extra damage...
Which leaves ward, with hundreds of uniques and effects dedicated to it, as the only meaningful form of defence.

I've been trying this game since beta but every change has been one step forward, two steps back; every single time, with no exception.

I launched Half-Life 2: Lost Coast again after 6 years, and it felt like opening a tiny time capsule.

It’s a very short experience — less than an hour — where you climb up a coastal cliffside monastery, fighting Combine soldiers along the way. The main focus isn’t really the gameplay itself, but the developer commentary, which explains the implementation of dynamic lighting on the Source engine and how the scene was built as a technology showcase. What makes it interesting is how compact and experimental it is. It doesn’t try to be a full game; it exists to demonstrate a specific visual feature and atmosphere.

For me, however, it’s also deeply personal. I first played this during the quarantine period, eating donuts from a local shop that has long since closed. Revisiting it now doesn’t just feel like replaying a tech demo. It feels like briefly returning to a past version of my life. Even though it’s a small experience, it carries a surprising amount of nostalgia.

[h1] AMAZING GAME [/h1]

Cyberpunk is exactly my kind of game. FPS + Fast-Paced + Dialogue Choices + Looter-Shooter + RPG + Open World + Futuristic + Romance, it's the absolute ultimate combination. The gunplay is pretty solid, the story is engaging, and the dialogue choices are interesting yet a little bit risky, so you can't just click through them without thinking.

[h2] The World & Loot [/h2]
The characters are interesting, though they could still use a bit more development and deep-diving into their backstories. Still, I love Johnny. He's probably one of the best companions in gaming since Elizabeth from Bioshock Infinite. He’s like an uncle who tells me what to do and warns me whether something is safe or not.

The looter-shooter mechanics are absolutely rewarding af. You don't have to level up or anything just to equip a weapon, which makes exploration feel so much more worthwhile. It’s unlike those other games where you’re like, [i]"Wow, I found this great looking gun after exploring hour after hour, finally!"[/i] only for the game to say, [i]"Congratulations on finding this gun! Now go waste some more time leveling up just to equip it."[/i] Like, bruh~.

Some people wanted this game to be in TPP, but not me. I’m relieved it turned out to be FPP. I play Fallout and Skyrim in first-person too, and even for survival games, I always prefer and enjoy FPP way more.

[*] 55 Melee Weapons
[*] 484 Clothing Items
[*] 14 Vehicles

I remember when this game blew up everywhere. I saw the memes, my favorite YouTuber uploading build videos, and all the complaints about it being a buggy mess in terrible shape. 

I got curious and searched YouTube for the gameplay, and damn, it looked cool af. I realized this was the exact game I had wanted for a long time. I wanted to play it so bad, but I didn't have a device that could run it. Even though it was buggy, I just didn't care. I ended up turning off the video and told myself:

[quote] "I will definitely play this game in the future." [/quote]

I stopped watching because I knew I'd play it eventually, and I didn't want to spoil anything. Time passed, and I kept saving money from school. It was a constant cycle: saving money > building a PC > components failing/faulty > saving money again > building a PC again > failing again > repeat.

Then, in 2024, I finally built a PC with an [b]i5-3470 + RX 460 2GB + 8GB RAM[/b]. An absolute potato, but hey, I could finally play! I will preface this review with the fact that I played and loved Witcher 3, so was expecting something along those lines, but more futuristic and of course with a new and different story.
Having seen the trailers and this being a new IP, I was excited, though knew from the start that I won't play on day one as I generally never do (for both monetary and bug reasons).
Turns out this could not have been a better idea, given the absolute shambles the game was in on launch.

After all of the bugs were sorted and the DLC was released, I finally decided to sink my teeth into the game, and when I say that, I mean it, as I have put in 230 hours into the game, finishing every main story mission as well as every side gig / side mission.
Given the amount of time I've spent, you would think that I was a big fan of the game, but sadly, Cyberpunk is no Witcher and the game ultimately disappointed me.
Without getting too into the weeds, the world just didn't pull me in, which mostly resulted from (in my opinion) poor writing, plot inconsistencies, characters that are not likeable and are either shoehorned in or are clearly missing something (presumably due to content being cut) and of course, the bugs.
Don't get me wrong, there are definitely parts where the game shines and some missions that were really impactful and well written, however all this was in a sea of inconsistencies and issues, which ultimately made me disinterested.
I started and stopped playing this game multiple times, but just kept pushing myself to go through everything and "experience" the game fully, but at the end I feel like most of it was a waste of time.

All in all, Cyberpunk is not a terrible game, but it is definitely very flawed.
While it is definitely a cultural moment in gaming history, it's remembered more for how it launched than for what it actually is. And that's the problem: strip away the redemption narrative and you're left with a good-looking game built on a shaky foundation. The highs are real, but they're rare, and they never added up to the experience I'd hoped for. I'm glad I finally played it, if only to form my own opinion - but I don't see myself going back, and I'd struggle to tell anyone they need to.

P.S. If you do end up playing it, the Phantom Liberty DLC is MANDATORY!

This game was genuinely somewhat responsible for ending a 2 year relationship i had with my fiance because of how real if felt and how nihilistic i became. one of the most real games i have ever played. no game comes remotely close to being as immersive or having characters that feel so genuinely alive. and the people who tossed this game to the side after a few crashes and never gave it a second chance, don't deserve the masterpiece that it is now. legit a 10/10 game.

The only thing I dislike about this game is that there isn't an infinite amount of it for me to enjoy.  I return to this game every few months, lose myself in it for a week, gush about how great it is to my friends, and then go back to the coldbox of other games like Bartmoss for another few months.

My only real gripes are that I wish there were more voice options, and I'd like a native option to narrow the field of random stats that populate when upgrading cyberware to stats that are relevant to your build.  Mods solve this problem handily, but it's something I hope they CDPR thinks about doing if this game gets a sequel.  Also, I personally am not a fan of any of the romance targets in the game; they all have some pretty crippling character flaws that would make me shy away from a serious relationship with them, so most of the time I just skip romance entirely.

9.5/10, sell me another one please -- preferably with fewer on-launch issues than this one.

Final verdict 6/10.

The game tries to be some jack of all trades combination of multiple genres, but falls flat due to not perfecting any mechanic.

The only thing Cyberpunk has going for it is the unique and fairly interesting story and the concept of implants. However that's where it falls off and the gameplay quickly becomes stale since the missions are either stealth or loud and both are really underdeveloped.


The stealth is really bland, the NPC's either stand completely still, or move between two points 10 meters apart. Every single mission is the exact same. Walk up to an enemy and press takedown, or distract them with a light and press takedown. Repeat for every enemy. 

The gunplay is also bland and braindead. The enemy AI is useless and either hitscan you from the other side of the map or stand still in front of you and miss absolutely everything.

 The melee combat is complete garbage. You spam R2 without a thought and the enemy hitbox is super clunky, often clipping through cover and teleporting straight on you when dodging or jumping away. The deflect mechanic is also broken, 9/10 times the deflected hit just deals damage anyways. 

The AI is extremely buggy, multiple times a session random cars drive off the road, spawn upside down or crash. You'll also notice that if you drive too close to a sidewalk, the NPC's will almost always glitch and jump BACKWARDS INTO YOUR CAR which gives you wanted level. 60€ btw.

Buy it if you like the style and story, but ONLY  on discount.

A epic open world first person game with many different factions and political elites. A great story that'll have you either laughing or emotional at times. There's a skill tree mechanic in which you can unlock new abilities that'll either help you in combat, survive or learn skills to help you progress by means of learning how to hack systems, open doors and ventilation shafts.

The character animations are nicely done and life-like. The quests or missions are super long compared to other games. You literally feel like you're hanging out with someone for 2-3 hours. With lengthy dialogues and animations. Also, whether you are picking someone up, driving to a location to gather supplies, checking out or gathering intel on an enemy base before entering, driving to a location to pick up your reward. And even after everything, sometimes dropping that person back off.

To make the missions faster and easier to navigate, you can either fast travel to your objectives or allow AI to drive you to your objective markers with auto-drive. You can also skip forward in time, which skips dialogue and animations for those who have to reload a save and don't want to go through it all over again.

The Phantom Liberty DLC is well worth the price as I thought it would never end. But in a good way. With much more content, such as additional missions, skill tree, tech, cyberware, vehicles and weapons. A new cast of characters and actors. Not only does the base game alone have multiple different endings based on the decisions you make in the game, but the Phantom Liberty DLC also has many different endings.

The only downside to the game for me is there's no vehicle garage or aircraft hangars where you can customize your vehicles. You can at least purchase many different types of vehicles and give them a custom paint job.

I recommend making a manual save for each mission you play as there are some missable achievements and you also may have not liked the choices you have made that will change the overall experience and ending.

Awesome game, a bit misunderstood and overhated at launch.

Graphics are incredible overall, especially after all the updates. Night City is one of the most detailed and immersive open worlds ever made, full of life, neon lights, crowded streets and beautiful environments. Character models and facial animations are excellent, and the combat animations feel fluid and satisfying. Some NPC behaviors can still feel a bit awkward at times, but the overall presentation is top tier.

Sound design is amazing. Weapons sound powerful, cars feel aggressive, the city ambience is immersive, and the voice acting is top tier across the board. The music is absolutely phenomenal, perfectly matching every moment, whether you're cruising through the city at night, fighting through chaos, or experiencing emotional story moments.

Gameplay is great and highly engaging. Let's start with the combat, because many people still think Cyberpunk is just "GTA with RPG elements", but it offers so much more than that. There are many different playstyles to choose from: stealth hacking, aggressive gunplay, melee combat, katanas, throwing knives, cyberware-focused builds, snipers, shotguns, netrunning, and more. Every build feels different and has strengths and weaknesses depending on your approach and preparation. Combat is fast, fluid and rewarding, especially once your build starts coming together. The movement also feels great, especially with double jumps, dashes and cyberware upgrades that make you feel increasingly powerful.
The progression system is great, allowing you to specialize heavily into your preferred playstyle. Skill trees offer meaningful upgrades that genuinely change how you approach combat and exploration. Cyberware customization is one of the best parts of the game, letting you turn your character into an unstoppable machine, stealth hacker, samurai or whatever style you prefer.
Looting and equipment are also very satisfying. Finding iconic weapons, upgrading cyberware and experimenting with builds keeps the gameplay fresh for dozens of hours.
Exploration is another huge strength. Night City feels alive and dense, with hidden events, side gigs, random encounters and environmental storytelling everywhere. Even just driving around the city at night can be immersive on its own.
The side content is surprisingly strong too. Many side quests are written with the same quality as the main story, often presenting difficult choices, morally gray situations and memorable characters. Some side quests end up being more emotionally impactful than entire main campaigns in other games.
Another great mechanic is how your choices and relationships affect quest outcomes, dialogue and endings. Some opportunities, conversations and endings are only available depending on how you played and who you connected with throughout the game.
These are only some of the great mechanics the game offers, the rest are for you to discover.

The enemies are varied and fun to fight. Different gangs use different weapons, tactics and cyberware, forcing you to adapt your approach. Boss fights and cyberpsychos can also be very memorable, especially when your build is tested properly.

The story is excellent, emotional and extremely well written. The characters are memorable, believable and full of personality. V and Johnny Silverhand have fantastic chemistry throughout the game, and many side characters leave a strong impression too. The role playing aspects are very well done, with meaningful dialogue choices, different character backgrounds and multiple endings depending on your decisions. The game constantly presents morally complex situations where there is rarely a completely right or wrong answer. Also some of the endings are brutal, so be prepared to a rollercoaster of emotions.

In short, awesome game, a bit misunderstood because of its disastrous launch. After years of updates and improvements, the game became what people originally hoped it would be. Many people still judge it based on old impressions without giving it another chance. If you enjoy immersive RPGs, great storytelling, strong world building and flexible character progression, this game is absolutely worth experiencing.

When this game first came out I refused to buy it it because of the state it was in. 6 years later I was able to get it on sale for like 15 dollars and thought why not. The boss fights are really not that great as is the melee combat. And especially bosses that primarily do melee or have knockdown attacks suck. But that's not really what the game was trying to go for anyway. 

Keanu Reeves carries hard. The game is definitely worth playing, and while flawed, is very fun, and has great worldbuilding behind it too.

Amazing experience overall—Cyberpunk 2077 isn’t perfect, but the atmosphere, characters, and slow-burning story really pulled me in. The diversity of transportation, from sleek cars to bikes, adds to the immersion, making Night City feel alive even when it doesn’t fully react to you. It’s flawed in places, but the vibe and moments it creates stayed with me long after I stop playing.

Cyberpunk 2077 was an amazing experience from start to finish. Its story hooked me right away and completely immersed me in its world. I don’t usually play games in this genre, but Cyberpunk 2077 made me appreciate it and even become interested in it.

What surprised me the most was that after finishing the game, I thought no other game could make me feel the same way again. Then I played Phantom Liberty, and it gave me that experience all over again. It was every bit as good as the base game, and I can understand why some people even consider it better.
An absolutely great story with gameplay just as good! The visuals in the game are very beautiful and the path tracing it has looks amazing with the new updates and lighting fixes!

Cyberpunk 2077 is the definition of ‘they fixed the game and nobody moved on fast enough to admit it.’

I’m on my fifth playthrough now, and somehow Cyberpunk 2077 still feels more alive than most new open world games coming out today. Night City isn’t just a map, it feels like a real place with exhausted people, fake dreams, dirty money, and nonstop chaos hiding behind neon lights.

What surprised me the most after replaying it so many times is how emotional the game actually is. Under all the chrome, guns, and explosions, Cyberpunk is really about loneliness, identity, survival, and trying to matter before the world forgets you. Few games make side characters feel this human. Panam, Judy, Johnny, Viktor they genuinely feel like people you knew for a while instead of quest dispensers.

And honestly? The atmosphere is unmatched. Driving through Night City at 2 AM with the radio on while rain hits the windshield is still one of the hardest vibes gaming has ever created. No game captures “beautiful but broken” like this one.

People talked nonstop about the bad launch, but after five runs, I can confidently say the comeback deserves just as much attention. The amount of detail, storytelling, soundtrack, world building, and freedom in combat is insane now. Whether you play stealth, netrunner, samurai build, or complete cyberpsycho, the gameplay actually lets you feel unique.

The craziest part is that Cyberpunk doesn’t try to make you feel like a hero. It constantly reminds you that in Night City, almost everyone loses something. And that’s exactly why the story hits harder than most RPGs.

Five playthroughs later and I still catch small details I missed before. That’s rare. Most games get boring after one ending. Cyberpunk somehow gets more personal the longer you stay in Night City.

“Wrong city. Wrong people.”
Still one of the coldest lines in gaming.

Dead by Daylight is truly one of the most welcoming gaming experiences ever created.

The developers listen to the community so much that every patch feels like a personal attack designed to test your mental stability. The balance is incredible! Every killer is overpowered until you play them yourself, and every survivor perk becomes useless the second you equip it. Perfectly fair. It's not because I'm bad at the game, definitely not.

The matchmaking system deserves special praise too. I was immediately introduced to 8,000+hours veterans with glowing cosmetics and enough experience to predict my movement before I was even born. Truly an educational environment for me.

And the community? Absolutely wholesome. Survivors will flashlight blind you for 14 minutes straight or teabag you at the gates just to encourage personal growth, while killers lovingly hit you on hook or hump you on a ground to remind you they care. Endgame chat is basically free therapy.

No other game creates friendships this strong. After one solo queue session, you’ll learn new languages, new insults, and at least three entirely original ways people can tell you to uninstall this game. Or say nice things about your mom.

Still, I can’t stop playing. Every match brings me so much joy.

Dead by Daylight has a strong concept, but in reality, it’s one of the more frustrating multiplayer games I’ve played. What should be a tense and fun horror experience often turns into something annoying and repetitive.

The gameplay feels very inconsistent and, at times, unfair. Matches can feel completely one-sided, and it often seems like luck or poor balancing matters more than actual skill. Instead of feeling competitive, it can feel like you’re just stuck in situations you can’t control.

The grind is another big problem. Progression is slow, repetitive, and honestly feels like a chore. Unlocking perks and leveling characters takes way too long, which makes it hard to stay engaged over time.

The monetization doesn’t help either. There’s a lot of DLC, and while some of it adds interesting content, it starts to feel excessive. It gives off the impression that the game is constantly trying to get more money out of you rather than just being enjoyable on its own.

Overall, Dead by Daylight feels more frustrating than fun most of the time. While the idea behind it is good, the poor balance, heavy grind, and constant push for DLC make it hard to recommend. There are better multiplayer games out there that respect your time more.

Hogwarts Legacy looks amazing. When you look at Hogwarts Castle and Hogsmeade, it feels incredible, and everything inside the castle is beautifully designed. The gameplay is fun too, especially when you experiment with different spells and potions.

But other than that, the game falls apart for me. The story is mediocre, and the voice acting is horrendous. There are barely any likeable characters in the game except for a few.

The open world also becomes too repetitive with its puzzles, and the side quests aren’t very interesting either. I would say if you’re into Harry Potter, you can give it a try, but as a Potterhead myself, I didn’t enjoy it as much as I hoped I would.

It’s hard for me to say whether I recommend it or not. Ultimately, let’s say I do, though the game has plenty of issues.

Pros:
- This game is an absolute treat for Potterheads. Hogwarts and Hogsmeade look simply fantastic. There are little details and nods to the series everywhere.
- The combat is quite engaging and enjoyable. In my opinion, the PC version could be a bit better optimized for keyboard and mouse.
- Some of the side and main storylines are quite interesting. In my opinion first half of the game is way more charming with attending classes etc.

Cons:
- [i]Revelio, Revelio, Revelio, Revelio[/i]. If players needs to constantly use some kind of special sight your exploration sucks. Period.
- Absolutely awful, disgusting, and unbearable ludonarrative dissonance. Our character is great at everything. Nothing is difficult for them. And of course they are a Chosen One. Plus, as a student who has barely started at Hogwarts, we go around killing Ashwinders, Goblins, and other magical creatures. Add to that the Unforgivable Curses, which you can cast anywhere and they don’t make an impression on anyone.
- Even though the world is filled with NPCs, it feels like a dollhouse. Non-player characters practically don’t react to anything happening around them. Open world feels equally empty. The map is enormous yet the amount of unique content is lost in its vastness.
- The open-world disease. That is, it’s filled with a handful of activities repeated dozens or even hundreds of times. Merlin’s Trials quickly become boring and repetitive. Clearing yet another enemy camp rewards us with nothing more than another trait. Catching magical beasts is just spamming an F so... Adding a mini-game to the Alohomora spell was also a terrible design choice.
- The game’s optimization leaves much to be desired, even on high-end hardware.

I maxed game achievements, but at times I had to clench my teeth as I waded through some of the developers' decisions.

So, basically, my wife enjoys it. I enjoyed it. It has a nice little cozy vibe of fairy tale game, there is some depth and complexity to its combat system, etc.
Dialogues are awful, story is questionable, some of the characters are grotesquely unbelievable, but the atmosphere is so good.

Hogwarts Legacy is a game that looks great on the surface, but the overall experience ends up feeling pretty disappointing.

The open world is visually impressive at first, but it quickly starts to feel empty and repetitive. Outside of the main areas, there isn’t much variety, and a lot of activities feel copy-pasted.

The gameplay also lacks depth. Combat is flashy at first, but it becomes repetitive over time, and there isn’t enough variety to keep it interesting long-term.

The story and characters are another major weak point. The characters feel poorly written, and I had zero emotional connection to them throughout the game, which makes it hard to care about what’s happening.

Overall, Hogwarts Legacy feels like a missed opportunity. It has a strong setting, but the repetitive gameplay and weak writing make it hard to stay engaged.

I've had my Steam account for over two decades, and in all that time I've only written one other review. I generally don't bother. Most games reveal what they are fairly quickly, the community reaches a consensus, and I move on.

Hogwarts Legacy is one of the few games that left me disappointed enough to actually sit down and write something.

The frustrating part is that the game isn't bad. In fact, the first several hours are excellent. Walking through Hogwarts for the first time genuinely feels magical. The castle is visually stunning, the atmosphere is strong, and for a brief moment it feels like the Harry Potter game fans have been waiting for.

Then the illusion starts to wear off.

The best way I can describe Hogwarts Legacy is that it looks like a beautiful onion. Every layer appears impressive, but the deeper you cut, the more you realize there's nothing at the center.

The story is serviceable but forgettable. Rather than telling a unique story within the Wizarding World, it relies heavily on familiar "chosen one" tropes and rarely takes any creative risks. I kept waiting for the narrative to surprise me, but it never really did.

More disappointing is how little the game supports the fantasy it advertises. You're supposed to be a student at Hogwarts, yet your choices barely matter, your relationships barely matter, and your actions carry almost no meaningful consequences. The game constantly tells you who you are supposed to be, but the systems never reinforce it.

The open world suffers from the same problem. It is enormous, beautiful, and filled with things to collect, yet very little of it feels meaningful. After a while I wasn't discovering anything interesting; I was simply clearing icons off a map. What initially felt like exploration gradually became a checklist.

There were also numerous immersion-breaking moments that repeatedly pulled me out of the experience. Some came from lore inconsistencies, others from character presentation and dialogue, but together they created a constant feeling that the game was more concerned with presenting the Wizarding World than understanding why people loved it in the first place.

That's ultimately my biggest criticism.

Hogwarts Legacy successfully recreates the appearance of Harry Potter. It recreates the architecture, the clothing, the spells, the music, and the imagery. What it struggles to recreate is the sense of wonder, consequence, discovery, and character that made the world memorable.

By the end, I realized I was exploring a world I loved rather than playing a game I loved.

For some people, that will be enough. It wasn't enough for me.

[b]Hogwarts Legacy é a prova de que dava para fazer um jogo de mundo aberto ambientado no universo de Harry Potter sem cair em mais um clone genérico cheio de pontos de interrogação no mapa. A Avalanche acertou no que mais importa: andar pelos corredores de Hogwarts, entrar numa sala nova e simplesmente parar para olhar em volta já é uma experiência por si só. O jogo entrega magia, no sentido literal e no figurado, e raramente perde o fôlego ao longo da campanha.[/b]

[i]Hogwarts Legacy is proof that it was possible to make an open-world game set in the Harry Potter universe without it turning into yet another generic clone covered in question marks on the map. Avalanche nailed what matters most: walking through the halls of Hogwarts and stepping into a new room is already an experience on its own. The game delivers magic, both literally and figuratively, and rarely loses steam throughout the campaign.[/i]

[h2]Jogabilidade | Gameplay[/h2]
⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐

[b]O sistema de combate é o grande trunfo aqui. Alternar entre Confringo, Incendio, Levioso, Accio e Glacius em tempo real, montando combos que abrem com controle e fecham com dano, dá um ritmo que nenhum outro jogo de magia conseguiu entregar até hoje. As árvores de talento permitem moldar um estilo próprio, seja focando em feitiços de controle, em poções e plantas para sustentação, ou em feitiços das Artes das Trevas para um combate mais agressivo. Fora do combate, voar de vassoura pelos terrenos de Hogwarts é satisfatório o suficiente para que você simplesmente desvie do caminho só para sobrevoar o lago.[/b]

[i]The combat system is the game's biggest strength. Switching between Confringo, Incendio, Levioso, Accio, and Glacius in real time, building combos that open with control and close with damage, creates a rhythm no other magic game has managed to replicate so far. The talent trees let you shape your own playstyle, whether focused on control spells, potions and plants for sustain, or Dark Arts spells for a more aggressive approach. Outside of combat, flying on a broomstick across the Hogwarts grounds is satisfying enough that you'll simply veer off course just to soar over the lake.[/i]

[h2]Duração | Duration[/h2]
⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐

[b]A campanha principal já entrega um volume generoso de conteúdo, mas é o mapa lateral que realmente justifica o tempo investido. Provações da Merlin escondidas em cantos do mapa, missões de relacionamento que revelam histórias completas dos colegas de casa, o Vault de cada aula liberando feitiços novos, e a Sala Precisa, que vira praticamente um jogo dentro do jogo conforme você expande a estufa, o vivário e a área de poções. Você termina o jogo com dezenas de horas no relógio e ainda sente que tem motivo para voltar.[/b]

[i]The main campaign already delivers a generous amount of content, but it's the side map that truly justifies the time invested. Merlin Trials hidden in corners of the map, relationship quests that reveal full storylines for your housemates, each class's Vault unlocking new spells, and the Room of Requirement, which basically becomes a game within the game as you expand the greenhouse, vivarium, and potions area. You finish the game with dozens of hours on the clock and still feel like there's a reason to come back.[/i]

[h2]Enredo | Story[/h2]
⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐

[b]O enredo principal cumpre bem o seu papel ao te jogar de cara no mistério da magia antiga, mas é a questline do Sebastian Sallow que rouba a cena. Acompanhar a queda dele em direção às Artes das Trevas, motivado por algo genuinamente compreensível, é a melhor escrita do jogo, e termina de um jeito que pesa de verdade nas suas escolhas. O fato de o jogo se passar décadas antes dos livros também funciona a favor, te dando liberdade para explorar a lore sem ficar amarrado aos eventos que todo fã já conhece de cor.[/b]

[i]The main plot does its job well by throwing you straight into the mystery of ancient magic, but it's Sebastian Sallow's questline that steals the show. Following his descent into the Dark Arts, driven by something genuinely understandable, is the game's best piece of writing, and it ends in a way that carries real weight on your choices. The fact that the game takes place decades before the books also works in its favor, giving you the freedom to explore the lore without being tied to events every fan already knows by heart.[/i]

[h2]Visual[/h2]
⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐

[b]Visualmente, Hogwarts Legacy é de tirar o fôlego. O castelo em si é um personagem: escadarias que mudam de lugar, retratos que comentam sua passagem, salas que mudam de aparência dependendo da estação do ano. As Terras Altas da Escócia ao redor servem de cenário perfeito, com Hogsmeade, a Floresta Proibida e a Floresta das Trevas tendo identidades visuais completamente diferentes entre si. No PC, com ray tracing ativado, ver a luz entrando pelos vitrais do Salão Principal durante o pôr do sol é um dos momentos mais bonitos que já vi em um jogo.[/b]

[i]Visually, Hogwarts Legacy is breathtaking. The castle itself is a character: staircases that move, portraits that comment on your passing, rooms that change appearance depending on the season. The surrounding Scottish Highlands serve as the perfect backdrop, with Hogsmeade, the Forbidden Forest, and the Forbidden Forest's darker reaches all having completely distinct visual identities. On PC, with ray tracing enabled, watching the light pour through the Great Hall's stained glass windows at sunset is one of the most beautiful moments I've ever seen in a game.[/i]

[h2]Audio[/h2]
⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐

[b]A trilha sonora de John Paesano honra o legado deixado por John Williams sem tentar imitá-lo, e isso é difícil de acertar. Os temas de exploração transmitem exatamente aquela sensação de mistério e maravilha que Hogwarts deveria ter, e os temas de combate sobem na intensidade certa quando os duelos começam. A dublagem em inglês é sólida em todo o elenco, com destaque para os professores, cada um com uma personalidade vocal marcante. Os efeitos sonoros dos feitiços também merecem elogio: cada magia tem um som distinto, o que ajuda demais a reconhecer o que está acontecendo no calor do combate.[/b]

[i]John Paesano's score honors the legacy left by John Williams without trying to imitate it, and that's a hard balance to strike. The exploration themes convey exactly that sense of mystery and wonder Hogwarts should have, and the combat themes ramp up at just the right intensity when duels begin. The English voice acting is solid across the board, with the professors standing out in particular, each one with a distinct vocal personality. The sound effects for the spells also deserve praise: every spell has a distinct sound, which helps a lot in recognizing what's happening in the heat of combat.[/i]

You do not even need to be a die hard Harry Potter fan to appreciate what an absolute achievement this game is. Though if you did grow up with the books or movies walking into the Great Hall or seeing the common rooms for the first time will genuinely give you goosebumps. It captures the pure magic of the universe in a way no game before it has ever managed.

The developers clearly poured an immense amount of love and reverence into designing the environment. The castle itself is a masterpiece, it's absolutely packed with moving portraits, hidden rooms and secrets! I spent my first several hours just wandering the corridors and taking in the atmosphere. Hogsmeade feels equally alive, bustling with chatter and backed by a phenomenal soundtrack that perfectly echoes the whimsical energy of the films.

The open world is vast and visually stunning and getting around is a blast. The broom controls are incredibly smooth and snappy to the point where flying mounts feel almost entirely redundant because the broom is just so much faster to mount and dismount.


Where the game unexpectedly excels is the spellcasting sandbox, the combat is fast, fluid and surprisingly deep. Stringing together combos like pulling an enemy close with Accio, lighting them on fire with Incendio and blasting them off a cliff with Depulso never gets old honestly. You also have access to potions and combat plants like the Chinese Chomping Cabbages (which are essentially 50% vegetable and 50% teeth) that can tear through encounters for you.

The game leans heavily into making you a one-wizard army. It’s definitely more of a beautiful relaxing journey than a brutal mountain climb; the game is very generous with its dodge-roll i-frames, and once you unlock your core talents, you will absolutely wreck anything in your path.


However, for all its magic the game does struggle to maintain that same momentum once you step away from the castle gates. The outer regions of the map feel a bit bloated and repetitive, eventually turning into a checklist of spider caves, bandit camps, and far too many Merlin trials.

There is also a noticeable lack of true "student life" mechanics. For a school setting the absence of curfews or any consequences for sneaking around at night feels like a missed opportunity... you can literally break into a faculty member's bedroom at 3 AM to steal a collectible while they sleep. Aside from standout companions like Sebastian and Poppy, most of the side characters feel a bit flat. They treat the protagonist as a blank-slate self-insert, resulting in a lot of "hello mysterious fifth-year friend" dialogue rather than meaningful relationships. I really hope the inevitable sequel takes notes from games like Bully, where attending scheduled classes actually rewards you with unique gameplay perks and mechanics.
A quick heads-up on optimization: the graphics are gorgeous, BUT the game is incredibly demanding on hardware. If you are running an 8GB VRAM card like an RTX 3070, turning on RTX will absolutely tank your performance... and cause heavy stuttering. Keep the settings reasonable and it runs beautifully, but it definitely eats up resources.


Even with the repetitive late-game collectible grind and the slightly hollow outer map, the sheer joy of exploring Hogwarts and blasting dark wizards out of the sky makes this a must-play. It lays a phenomenal foundation for the future of the franchise.

I got really into VRC during Covid, when everyone was stuck indoors. I started as a "questie"(using an Oculus Quest 2). Public worlds used to feel safe back then. People were fairly friendly and helpful. You could start conversations with random strangers, be a silly little creature, and it helped me get out of my shell. Back then most people didn't care if you were on quest. After a few years I was able to switch to PC and eventually get fullbody tracking. I watched VRC go through a lot of updates/changes, from removing modding, adding physbones to replace dynamic bones, etc.

Over time I met many kinds of people and made many friends. Most of them I had to cut contact with. I'd say about 70-80% of them eventually turned out to be hardcore alcoholics, narcissists, and creeps. They don't care about boundaries. Many of these people think its okay to walk up and touch you inappropriately because your avatar isn't a real body, especially if your model looks feminine in any way. Making genuine friendships now is near impossible when most of these people just want to use you for their own personal gain, whether it be for their "content" or just to gossip behind your back so they could get attention. These people don't care if you're in a relationship either, they will blatantly flirt with you or your partner right in front of you, even if you tell them you/they're taken and not interested. There's also literal high school bullies that just start fights, rumors, or simply hate you because they're bored or just hate themselves. 

Don't get me started on the gooners. Everything is oversexualized. I don't really care what you do in your free time as long as its legal, but wearing literal BDSM gear on your avis in public spaces is just gross.
On that topic, VRC is NOT for kids. For their safety and everyone else's. It took them way too long to implement age restriction, but it uses the same ID verification as Roblox(they may have changed this since I last played), which a lot of kids easily bypass. The platform has a REALLY BAD problem with predators.

About the alcoholics. Partying is very encouraged in a lot of communities, and it can be really fun! I knew a lot of people that hosted very fun events/game nights. However, I've witnessed so many people passing out or throwing up because they don't moderate the amount they drink and the people around them treat it like a game to get completely screwed up. I sometimes had to babysit drunk people to make sure they'd be okay and because of these situations, I rarely drink alcohol myself anymore. I watched people rot their brains and make very worrying decisions. In the end, its their choice. I just choose to distance myself from them.

There is still some good with VRChat. Some worlds are beautiful, games are fun, and not all of the people are terrible. I used to love making avatars for myself and doing photography and video making. Now the thought of hopping into VR makes my stomach churn. I'm utterly disgusted by the thought of it, and I've been much happier outside of it.

I thought I was starting a game about killing monsters.

Instead, I found a story about family, love, friendship, loss, and the people we would cross the world for.

Geralt's journey to find Ciri is one of the most memorable stories I've experienced in gaming. But what truly made this adventure special were the people around him. Yennefer and Triss aren't just romance options on a menu; they're characters who feel real. They have flaws, dreams, fears, and histories that make every interaction meaningful.

There were moments that made me smile, moments that broke my heart, and moments that made me sit quietly and think about the choices I had made. Few games manage to make you care this much.

The world is beautiful, but its greatest strength is its soul. From the muddy roads of Velen to the icy winds of Skellige, every place feels alive. Every quest feels like someone's story rather than another task to complete.

When the credits rolled, I wasn't thinking about the monsters I had slain or the loot I had collected.

I was thinking about Ciri.

I was thinking about Yennefer.

I was thinking about Triss.

I was thinking about the friends I met and the goodbyes I never wanted to say.

The Witcher 3 didn't feel like a game I finished. It felt like a world I had to leave behind.

A game that puts its predecessors to shame. There is a massive leap forward in gameplay, graphics, and world-building compared to the first two games in the series. The introduction is far better as well; the prologue is short, sweet, and does a wonderful job at introducing all of the game mechanics and gets you up to speed on the story, something the previous game sorely lacked.

The world is massive and alive. Cities are densely populated, while the numerous villages, keeps, and smaller communities all have their own people and stories. With all these characters comes a wealth of side-quests, most of which are interesting and well written. If you are someone who likes to complete everything, Act 1 can be quite daunting, as you are dropped into the world with seemingly endless content, which adds significantly to the game’s length.

As with the previous games, the story is very well written and extremely well paced. “Tension” is the word I would use to describe it best, as it does a fantastic job keeping you hooked and invested. You can complete it in many different ways and in many different orders, and the developers have foreseen a lot of unpredictable and interesting routes you can take.

The new alchemy system is much better than in previous instalments. Once you craft a potion or oil, you only need to meditate to refill it. Crafting has also improved, but still felt a little tedious, especially towards the end of the game. Skills and mutagens allow you to customize your playstyle and it felt like there were very few filler unlocks.

Gone are the days of dice poker and the annoying RNG behind it, as it has been replaced with something far superior: Gwent. This card game can be like crack to the right person, and it was truly a joy collecting every card and building every deck. Alas, as you start to get further into the game, you can effectively solve Gwent, which detracts from the enjoyment. It still remains one of the greatest mini-games I’ve ever encountered in an RPG.

The DLC deserve their own reviews, but I will briefly mention them here:
[list]
[*] Hearts of Stone is a small, but thrilling story with a lot of surprises and had me swinging like a pendulum when picking sides. The return of an old friend was great and certainly adds a lot if 	you are a longtime fan of the games.

[*] Blood & Wine is a truly masterful DLC in a beautiful setting. In my opinion, the story surpasses the base game, and the side content is great as well. The player housing is a great touch and finally gives Geralt a place to call home.
[/list]
Overall, I am not sure why I waited so long to play this game. Even over ten years later, it holds up remarkably well and stands out as one of the greatest RPGs of all time.

The Witcher 3: Wild Hunt is a true masterpiece and easily one of the best RPGs ever made. With 22.9 hours on record, I’ve only begun to scratch the surface of its massive world, but it’s already clear why this game is so highly regarded.

The story and characters are phenomenal. Geralt’s journey, the morally gray choices, and the deep, emotional side quests make the world feel alive and meaningful. Every decision actually matters, and the writing is top-tier across the board. The base game alone is incredible, but the two expansions (Hearts of Stone and Blood and Wine) are some of the best DLC ever released — especially Blood and Wine, which feels like its own full game.

Gameplay is engaging and rewarding. Combat feels satisfying once you get used to it, exploration is a joy thanks to the beautiful and varied world, and the side content is so good that you’ll often get more immersed in it than the main story. The atmosphere, music, and voice acting are all outstanding.

Even years later, The Witcher 3 holds up as a benchmark for open-world RPGs. It’s a long, rich adventure that respects your time and rewards curiosity.

If you haven’t played it yet, this is a must-play. One of the greatest games of its generation.

A phenomenal game with a grpping yet immersive story. The Witcher 3 will always remain one of my favourite and most memorable games I will ever play. Aside from the 10/10 gameplay, TW3 helped me navigate and persevere through a really difficult point in my life. 

What I appreciate the most about the game is how it, akin to the books, explores the complexities of the human psyche that is capable of great compassion and evil - both sides of the extreme and still everything in between. Yet the most poignant part is that such diversity of emotions and traits are not limited to certain steroetypes but are evidently hidden amongst all characters - from Geralt, the Bloody Baron, Olgierd, Detlaff/Syanna to emeperors, nobles down to peasants. The use of a world mirroring the political context of medieval Europe emphasises the toil everyone experiences, regardless of their socioeconomic background. 

Albiet being games from the same developer, I can't help but compare and contrast the Witcher 3 to Cyberpunk 2077 - both being my favourite games. Both of which serve as a pertinent commentary of humanity both in the past and what seems to be the looming future. 

Nonetheless, playing the Witcher 3 - from the combat to making morally-grey decisions on the spur of the moment - made me reflect a lot on the choices I've made in my life. In retrospect, we can never be too sure of the choices we make nor of the consequences they made have - on a single person or something much bigger. There is no right choice. 

“Mistakes,’ he said with effort, ‘are also important to me. I don’t cross them out of my life, or memory. And I never blame others for them.”

Among Us is one of the most engaging and memorable multiplayer games I have ever played. Despite its simple appearance, the game offers a surprisingly deep social experience that encourages teamwork, communication, critical thinking, and strategy.

One of the greatest strengths of Among Us is how it brings people together. Every match creates unique situations where players must work as a team to complete tasks while also trying to determine who can be trusted. The discussions and debates that take place during meetings are often intense, exciting, and genuinely entertaining.

The game's design is easy to understand, making it accessible to players of all skill levels. At the same time, mastering deception, observation, and deduction provides a rewarding challenge that keeps the gameplay fresh even after many hours.

The developers have also done an excellent job supporting the game with new maps, roles, customization options, and quality-of-life improvements. These updates have helped maintain the game's popularity and replay value over time.

Overall, Among Us is a fantastic example of how a simple concept can create an incredibly compelling experience. Its combination of strategy, social interaction, and suspense makes it a standout title that continues to be enjoyable years after its release. I highly recommend it to anyone looking for a fun and meaningful multiplayer game.
