While King Midas can turn whatever he touches to gold, Epic Games turns whatever they touch to ♥♥♥♥.

[h2]Nota Geral | Overall Rating[/h2]
⭐⭐⭐

[b]Fall Guys é um daqueles jogos que nasceu certo e foi destruído por quem não deveria ter chegado perto dele. A Mediatonic entregou, em 2020, uma das experiências multiplayer mais refrescantes dos últimos anos: simples, caótico e genuinamente divertido. Aí a Epic Games comprou o estúdio, fez o jogo free-to-play, e transformou o que era uma proposta honesta em mais uma plataforma de monetização disfarçada de jogo. O Fall Guys que existe hoje ainda tem momentos divertidos, mas carrega um peso que não existia antes: a sensação constante de que você está sendo manipulado para gastar dinheiro. Tudo que a Epic toca, apodrece.[/b]

[i]Fall Guys is one of those games that was born right and was destroyed by those who should never have gotten close to it. Mediatonic delivered, in 2020, one of the most refreshing multiplayer experiences in recent years: simple, chaotic, and genuinely fun. Then Epic Games bought the studio, made the game free-to-play, and turned what was an honest concept into yet another monetization platform disguised as a game. The Fall Guys that exists today still has fun moments, but carries a weight that didn't exist before: the constant feeling that you're being manipulated into spending money. Everything Epic touches rots.[/i]

[h2]Jogabilidade | Gameplay[/h2]
⭐⭐⭐⭐

[b]A jogabilidade continua sendo o ponto mais forte do jogo, e é o que mantém qualquer coisa de pé. O loop de correr, pular, empurrar e sobreviver em partidas de 60 jogadores ainda funciona porque a fisicalidade exagerada dos bonecos cria situações que nenhum designer conseguiria roteirizar. O caos é orgânico, e isso é genuinamente raro. Os minijogos clássicos como Fall Mountain, Hex-a-Gone e See Saw ainda são sólidos e divertidos. O problema não está na mecânica base, está no que foi empilhado em cima dela depois da transição para free-to-play.[/b]

[i]The gameplay remains the game's strongest point, and it's what keeps anything standing. The loop of running, jumping, pushing, and surviving in matches of 60 players still works because the exaggerated physicality of the beans creates situations no designer could script. The chaos is organic, and that's genuinely rare. Classic minigames like Fall Mountain, Hex-a-Gone, and See Saw are still solid and fun. The problem isn't in the base mechanics, it's in everything that was piled on top of them after the free-to-play transition.[/i]

[h2]Duração | Duration[/h2]
⭐⭐⭐

[b]Por ser um jogo multiplayer sem fim definido, a duração depende inteiramente de quanto tempo você aguenta antes de perceber que o modelo de negócio está trabalhando contra a sua diversão. A loja rotativa com itens que somem em 48 horas é um mecanismo de FOMO calculado: não é design de jogo, é design de ansiedade. Não existe sistema de progressão que recompense o tempo investido de forma genuína. Você não evolui seu personagem, não desbloqueia nada por habilidade. Você compra ou não compra. Para quem ignora a loja e joga pelo jogo em si, dá para tirar boas horas de diversão. Para quem cai na armadilha, a experiência vira uma relação financeira.[/b]

[i]Being an endless multiplayer game, the duration depends entirely on how long it takes before you realize the business model is working against your fun. The rotating shop with items that vanish in 48 hours is a calculated FOMO mechanism: it's not game design, it's anxiety design. There's no progression system that genuinely rewards the time you invest. You don't evolve your character, you don't unlock anything through skill. You either buy or you don't. For those who ignore the shop and play for the game itself, there are still good hours of fun to be had. For those who fall into the trap, the experience becomes a financial relationship.[/i]

[h2]Visual[/h2]
⭐⭐⭐

[b]A identidade visual original do Fall Guys era um dos seus trunfos: cores vibrantes, formas arredondadas e mapas com leitura visual clara. Simples o suficiente para você processar o caos sem perder o fio da meada. Os novos mapas jogam essa legibilidade pela janela. São cenários excessivamente detalhados, com camadas de informação visual empilhadas uma sobre a outra, que se misturam com a paleta já saturada do jogo e criam uma poluição visual genuína. Você literalmente perde o seu boneco no meio da correria porque tudo ao redor compete pela mesma atenção. Mais conteúdo não significa mais qualidade, e os novos mapas são a prova disso.[/b]

[i]Fall Guys' original visual identity was one of its strengths: vibrant colors, rounded shapes, and maps with clear visual readability. Simple enough for you to process the chaos without losing the thread. The new maps throw that readability out the window. They are excessively detailed environments, with layers of visual information stacked on top of each other, blending into the game's already saturated palette and creating genuine visual noise. You literally lose your bean in the middle of the action because everything around it competes for the same attention. More content does not mean more quality, and the new maps are the proof of that.[/i]

[h2]Audio[/h2]
⭐⭐⭐

[b]O áudio cumpre o básico. A trilha sonora tem energia suficiente para acompanhar o ritmo frenético das partidas, e os efeitos sonoros dos impactos e eliminações são satisfatórios. Nada aqui é particularmente memorável, mas também não atrapalha. O problema é que o áudio, assim como o visual, sofre da mesma poluição sonora que veio junto com os novos mapas e modos de jogo: muita coisa acontecendo ao mesmo tempo, com pouca hierarquia no que realmente importa ouvir.[/b]

[i]The audio delivers the basics. The soundtrack has enough energy to keep up with the frantic pace of matches, and the sound effects for impacts and eliminations are satisfying. Nothing here is particularly memorable, but nothing gets in the way either. The problem is that the audio, just like the visuals, suffers from the same kind of noise pollution that came with the new maps and game modes: too much happening at once, with little hierarchy in what actually matters to hear.[/i]

Borderlands 2 takes everything that worked in the first game and improves it. The gunplay feels better, the world is more interesting, and the story is way more memorable thanks to Handsome Jack, who completely steals the show. For me, Jack's a huge reason why I remember BL2 so fondly.  He is funny, charismatic, arrogant, and completely unhinged at the same time. Unlike many game villains who barely appear (and unlike the first game, where there wasn't a clear villain), Jack is constantly talking to you throughout the game, insulting you, joking around, or casually saying something ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ insane. Granted, for some, all the constant interactions can become tiresome and irritating, but for me, all his dialogue made the villain stand out and made me hate him even more.

The game still has that addictive loop of fighting enemies, finding ridiculous weapons, and constantly upgrading your character, but this time it feels more polished.

One of the best things about Borderlands 2 is its atmosphere. Pandora still feels dangerous and chaotic, but there is a lot more variety now, from snowy mountains to colourful cities and weird labs. The soundtrack is still fairly subtle most of the time, but it fits the world perfectly and helps the game switch between quiet exploration and complete chaos during fights.

The humour is a big part of the experience too. Sometimes it lands perfectly, sometimes it tries a little too hard, but the game has so much personality that it rarely feels boring. Playing with friends makes everything even better, especially during boss fights, and hearing all the playable character interactions is always fun, even though said interactions are still pretty limited.

The game definitely has flaws. Some missions drag on, enemies can become annoying damage sponges later in the game, and grinding for good loot can get repetitive. Still, Borderlands 2 is easily one of the best looter shooters ever made because it balances fun combat and memorable characters with a good sense of progression.

Obviously it's a good game. That said...

Euro Truck Simulator 2 is somewhat outside my usual milieu. A truck driving simulator rather than a cRPG or grand strategy game. At a glance, this isn't quite for me. Yet, by a twist of fate, trucking happens to be part of my heritage. I grew up spending much of my childhood travelling from one location to the next, seeing the world from inside one of those cabins. Scania, Volvo and of course MAN aplenty. They fill many of my childhood memories. If this game isn't made for me, I don't know who it could be made for. Now, after having explored much of Europe and built a trucking business empire, even if just virtually, let's put down a few words for the game that reminded me of so much.

You are a truck driver and you are in Europe. You do jobs ferrying cargo from one city to another. A minimalist premise. You, your truck and your next destination. Just get behind the wheel and go. The game's other aspect, the business sim layer, doesn't meaningfully change the equation. Yes, you can buy your own fleet of trucks, garages all over Europe and hire other truck drivers to do more jobs, but it isn't much more than passive income. It doesn't even scale well in the late game, particularly when it comes to purchasing specialized trailers in bulk. It doesn't take long to go from a somewhat entertaining power fantasy to a repetitive chore and eventually to simply obsolete once you have enough money coming in. The focus always returns to the driving sim aspect, that's the core. The game's key accomplishment, the map, is what adds the finishing touch.

The game offers a lavishly detailed highway map of Europe to explore. Scaled down obviously but places are instantly recognisable. Cities display their unique identities, with lots of landmarks carefully recreated and waiting to be discovered. Travelling in this game does feel like an approximation of the real thing and it's more than enough to trigger nostalgia. I remembered so many places, it was pure joy to just visit them again, from the convenience of my desk this time. The only glaring issue with the map is the monetization system. Unfortunately, it is sold piecemeal across numerous DLCs. I can understand this funds continuous development of the game but the sheer number of DLCs needed for the full experience is becoming excessive, particularly for someone starting anew. Perhaps if older DLCs were gradually integrated into the base game and retired, that would have been better.

As beautiful as the map is, the graphics in general do not impress. Yes, the trucks are fairly accurately modelled and the dashboards elegant but the engine shows its age and the end result is no more than decent. The most obvious omission is the lack of any kind of damage modelling, no deformations, not even scratches. Collisions in general are poorly simulated and lack believable impact. Hit any tiny little obstacle and it won't even flinch, yet your truck will be totalled. Be it the random titanium bush or the tollbar of impervium. What's a 70-tonne truck in comparison? The game is generally light on physics. This affects the driving experience. Traffic seems robotic, the cars moving as if they were on rails. Traffic laws are enforced in a rather arbitrary fashion, often to a comical degree. It's safer to slowly push a police car off the road and down a cliff than to overtake it.

Bringing it all together, I found Euro Truck Simulator 2 to be a relaxing and comforting experience. I sat back and just travelled to my next destination, taking in the scenery along the way. It satisfies both the nostalgia I feel for those trips and the sense of escapism they can facilitate. Yes, it is a bit janky, yes it could be a better simulator, but it has something that often is more important. It has a clear identity and a lot of heart. I warmly recommend this game to anyone with even a passing fascination with trucking and driving across Europe.

[b] Valheim [/b] is a game I really don't understand when it comes to its popularity. It never fully clicked for me, and after more time with it, it felt more disappointing than engaging. I can see what it is trying to do, but the execution just didn't work for me.

There are other survival games that do these systems better and feel more fresh. For me, Valheim is a big disappointment.

I can't recommend this game, but you can play it with a lot of nuances.

Pros:

1) Interesting building system that gives a nice relaxing vibe.
2) Beautiful surroundings (at least for the first 1-2 hours, and only if you don’t have rain, fog, darkness, rainfog, darknessrain, snowdarknessfog etc).
3) Curious low-poly art style.

Cons:

1) Almost ZERO Quality-of-Life features. Playing without simple QoL mods feels absolutely disgusting.
2) Loot system is terrible -- no proper tools for sorting or comfortably managing your inventory.
3) Planting something as simple as carrots without mods wastes an enormous amount of time.
4) Crafting takes way too much time.
5) Transporting resources is extremely tedious.
6) The combat system is awful. There’s almost no stamina management, every move drains stamina, and if you’re not fighting on perfectly flat ground you lose even more stamina and can’t even kill basic mobs.
7) Play with range weapon, everything else just make you glasscanon, and you must grind more items for lose them again and again
8) The big procedurally generated world feels unnecessary. It’s boring -- the SAME biomes, SAME structures, SAME enemies everywhere.
9) Bosses are a massive disappointment. It’s not about skill, it’s just a huge time sink -- 10 hours of dealing damage and spamming RMB + Shift. Only the first boss gives any real emotion.
10) Enemy pathfinding is horrendous. For example: In the frost cave mobs get stuck in "air"walls and can't move, but then can hit you through REALwalls.

My recommendation if you still want to play:

1) Play in co-op.
2) Play with the ability to transport ore through portals (transporting resources once or twice is fine, 50+ times -- not fun at all).
3) Set minimum death penalty (farming XP for skills in late game is pure time-wasting).
4) Use mods for quick item sorting in chests. And the ability to pull items from nearby chests when creating something.
5) Honestly, you can just build a ship, sail for 2 hours, see everything the game has to offer, and uninstall.

Have fun.

Valheim showed huge potential, but each step of Early Access rushes to the next major biome while leaving glaring issues with the prior one unaddressed. I'm not sure to what extent the Iron Gate team is reading feedback about the overall experience.

These issues start early, but begin severely compounding with the Swamp. Iron, used for support beams, is finite unless you farm a rare drop from a rare enemy for a trickle. A later biome showers you in a different metal that can't be used for support beams.

Sailing is a fundamentally empty experience, in spite of how essential it is to getting established and exploring new lands. This gets even worse when bad streaks of wind direction RNG force you to tack or reroute. Rather than the ocean gaining any content, the need to sail is instead trivialized once you go through the Ashlands and can make portals that don't reject metal.

Combat is increasingly plagued by issues that go well beyond gear gaps. Balance and AI are all over the place, as exemplified by the Ashlands. The entire biome is meant to have an alternative feel of constant hostility and a focus on sieging enemy fortresses. These fortresses are empty and can be dirt-pillared into. Your allies will die by recklessly charging through lava or trying to fight in it. The biome boss can randomly leave or camp in lava.

In conclusion:

The flow of Valheim is interrupted by sudden and persistent instances of broken, empty content, and the releases leave them unaddressed to add the next piece of content that will in turn suffer the same problems. 1.0 is approaching, and all that seems to be on the horizon is another content drop. Even if the Deep North ends up proving amazing when taken in isolation, there is too much jank along the way for me to recommend Valheim to new players.

Get it if you really, really like the building style and don't mind playing with material costs disabled. Otherwise, wait for post-release notes to see if the major gaps left by the wayside will be addressed.

You see my playtime. I’ve spent a decent amount of time playing Valheim with friends. And I will continue playing it until we’ve seen all content. It’s not a _bad_ game. But it’s not a good game, either. Since Valheim is still in Early Access at the time of writing, this review serves a double role of explaining why I wouldn’t recommend it, and providing feedback to the dev. There is no real order or logic to what will follow.

The tl;dr of this review is that Valheim introduces friction into every action. You have a goal you want to reach but Valheim gets in the way. That’s normal for games. Otherwise there would just be a ’you win’ button. Games are meant to have obstacles you need to overcome. But Valheim pricks you with a thousand needles every step of the way. That’s plain annoying. Many of these needle pricks come from cranking the realism slider up too high.

I would’ve liked to write more, but the character limit cuts this review short.

[h3]Enemies[/h3]

Especially in the early game, but also later, your best option – your only option to survive – is to run. Run as fast as the wind can carry you. But every single mob in the game is persistent like a bloodhound. When you’ve depleted and regained your stamina multiple times, they’re still after you. By that time, however, you’ve stumbled into the next enemy who will now also chase you. In the end you’ve crossed a whole ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ continent (the map in Valheim is composed of islands) just by running away from danger. Whatever it was you actually wanted to do at the beginning of that encounter, you’re now miles away from your target. Running away from mob after mob for minutes while you’re _trying_ to go in the opposite direction isn’t fun.

Another thing are the entry-level mobs. Throughout the game you tend to be in the overworld to farm some stuff – mowing down a forest for wood or mining a vein of copper. These things take time, but there’s also a meditative quality to spend a few hours (real-time) to eradicate a forest from the face of the earth… if it weren’t for the mobs. They’re often the very first enemies you find in the game, so they’re seriously underpowered by the time you need to farm this many resources.

What this effects to is that way too often you need to set down your actual task to one-hit some nuisances. In that stage of the game they don’t even deal damage because you’re so overpowered compared to them. But you still can’t ignore them because their attacks still have the knockback of a freight train. Seriously. Getting hit by a pebble somehow moves you 2 meters. So you _have_ to kill them.

There would be such an easy fix: Let me buy and equip an item that just shoos away boring enemies. Price it at 2k so it’s not immediately available. The item creates a bubble of, say, 50m radius that enemies whose attacks would hurt you by less than 10HP (accounting for your equipped armor) can’t penetrate.

[h3]The world is repetitive[/h3]

Valheim is a game of exploration. Its world is huge and procedurally generated. Unfortunately, it is boringly generated. There are multiple different biomes, and they are interesting. Each is completely different than the one before. But [i]within the same biome,[/i] everything is the same. And that’s a shame. If you’ve seen the biome once there is really no joy in finding the biome again.

At one point in our playthrough that became painfully obvious: For each biome there is a boss you need to fight in order to progress. However, not every [i]instance[/i] of the biome has the boss. For two biomes we had to spend literal IRL hours searching multiple different instances of these biomes until we found the boss. This reduces the gameplay to going to a new island, running across the whole thing as fast as you can and returning because it was empty after all. There is exploration in the sense that you fill in the map, but it’s dull. There is nothing interesting to find, nothing to grab your attention. Just a series of sprints over land that you’ve seen again and again. Running for hours over a barren landscape in order to progress isn’t fun.

[h3]Traversing the world is annoying[/h3]

As I’ve said the world is _huge_. To go to remote parts you build a ship and set sail… for a very long time. On our last long journey that I remember, _one trip_ took somewhere between 30 and 45 minutes. Crossing our known world at that time would’ve taken 60–90 minutes. Due to a game mechanic I won’t spoil here, you can go faster if enough people are on board.

Now here’s the WTF moment of this game: Only one person can steer the ship. [i]Everyone else on board has nothing to do![/i] They have no agency and no tasks. You [i]have[/i] to sail long distances, and it’s beneficial if many people are on board, but there’s nothing to do for them. The winning strategy for them is to go AFK and do something else. Now tell me that this isn’t bad game design. Even the person steering the ship has long stretches of time where there’s nothing to do. You just go in a straight line and that’s it.

Another annoyance that factors into the previous one, but is really a separate issue: The wind direction is random. In the early game that was fun. You’d have to wait for good winds before you could set sail and go exploring. But in the late game, when you need to cross kilometers and are thrown back to ruddering a good portion of the journey, you’ll just curse the game. It’s so boring.

In our little group we’ve turned towards just one person venturing out while the others do something better with their time. Again: A multiplayer game that punishes doing ♥♥♥♥ together. Make it make sense to me.

[hr][/hr]

There is one biome in particular that is just a pain in the ass from start to finish. I’m talking about [spoiler]the Mistlands[/spoiler]. Running and jumping in the game depletes stamina. You have to wailt for it to refill. In this particular biome you [i]must[/i] run [i]and[/i] jump [i]all the time[/i]. This leads to your ‘walking’ (let’s be gracious and call it that) for 20–50 meters and then wait for your stamina bar to fill up again. 30m more, wait, 50m, wait. I cannot for the life of me comprehend how anyone could opine that this is fun gameplay. At that point Valheim turns into an idle game.

Luckily this biome can be finished quickly (if you’re lucky with finding the boss). But that still accounts for several hours of gameplay. At the end of the biome our party unanimously voted this biome the ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ and all of us were glad it was over. We won’t be returning unless we absolutely have to.

This has the side effect that we won’t explore one of the game’s mechanics, [spoiler]wizardry[/spoiler] because we can’t be arsed to farm the required resources. Dev, you wasted your time putting that in. Sorry.

[h3]The programming is bad.[/h3]

This is a big one that has caused a lot of frustration: Disconnects. Every session we play, we get multiple disconnects. I think that the internet connection of my peers might be bad. If the connection gets lost, there’s nothing Valheim can do. That’s on us. However, if Valheim decides to kick one player off the server while our shared voice chat keeps working uninterrupted, then I blame the game. And I also blame the game if that player can’t simply reconnect. If one player disconnects, they often have to close Valheim and open it again before they can join the server again. There’s really no excuse for that. Fix the game!

We’ve had brittle connections in other games. We were much more tolerant with those games because they made re-joining painless. Valheim doesn’t. Another punch in the guts is that Valheim fails to keep the game world in a consistent state. On multiple occasions we have lost valuable items because the connection broke as one player was transferring items to/from chests. Sometimes items get duplicated, but subjectively that doesn’t make up for losing items. Losing your progress to a bug isn’t fun.

Great game, painfully slow development. Go buy another game like Abiotic Factor or Enshrouded

Valheim itself is a very good game. The atmosphere, exploration, building, and early progression are genuinely excellent, that's why i have nearly 900 hours. That is what makes the development pace so frustrating. They really wasted their chance. 

I honestly wont be buying another game from Iron Gate. The amount of content added since launch feels incredibly small compared with other survival/crafting games and similar indie studios. When you look at games like Satisfactory, Abiotic Factor, Enshrouded, and others, the difference in pace and communication is hard to ignore.

For a Viking game where sailing is such a central part of the experience, cutting the Ocean biome update  feels just feels so off, that its even hard do describe. The game also still has large gaps in progression, with magic only appearing very late, and many basic QOL features are still missing. There is no proper recipe search, no good recipe sorting, and many systems still feel unnecessarily clunky after all this time.

Performance is also still not where it should be. Multiplayer servers can lag heavily once people start building larger bases, and the optimisation simply does not feel good enough for a game that has been in early access for this long.

Valheim has a fantastic foundation, but Iron Gate’s development pace is painfully slow.

1/10 - Do not buy. One of the most frustrating games I've ever played.
I really wanted to like Valheim. Viking survival, base building, exploration — the concept sounded great. Too bad the execution is awful, especially once you hit the sailing part of the game.
The sailing mechanics are hands-down the worst I've ever seen in any game. Boats feel like they're made of wet cardboard. The wind system is infuriating — you spend more time fighting the wind than actually progressing. Trying to sail anywhere useful turns into a tedious, rage-inducing chore. The boats handle like absolute trash: turning is sluggish, speed is inconsistent, and God forbid you hit even a small wave or rock because you'll flip or get stuck instantly.
I lost count of how many times my fully loaded longship sank for no good reason or got permanently beached because the controls are so imprecise. The "realistic" sailing they were going for just feels like bad game design. Hours upon hours wasted tacking back and forth across the ocean while enemies snipe you from the water or sea serpents destroy your ship in seconds.
The rest of the game isn't much better once you get past the initial honeymoon phase. Combat feels clunky, progression is a massive grind, and the world is mostly empty boring ocean and forest. The building system is decent I guess, but it's not nearly good enough to make up for everything else.
If you enjoy slowly sailing in circles while your controller (or keyboard) gathers dust and your patience evaporates, then maybe Valheim is for you. Otherwise, skip this one. There are much better survival/crafting games out there.

Before I start the review I need to give the context I have played about 60 hours on xbox app and my friend somehow convinced me to buy it again.

This game is insanely over rated unless you literally only care about cozy vibe and nice looks. The building seems to be nice but is crippled by. The main problems are the insane amounts of grind and the combat.

The game starts out fine in a meadows area where you can build some things and hunt some basic animals. Everything seems fine at this point. It is kind of fun here. You cook your food and build a basic house until you fight the first boss who is also ok. We then move onto the next biome and this is where the game starts to show what the rest is going to be like. You need to mine copper and tin to make bronze and it takes a LONG time. You get to watch your stupid little man lift up and drop his pickaxe again, and again, and again until you have too much weight to carry everything back. 
In valhiem you have a limited amount of weight you can carry and a limited amount of inventory space. There is NO WAY to increase inventory space and there are NO armor slots. A large amount of your space you have is taken up by your clothes and basic things you need. The game encourages you to carry food, tools, weapons, clothes and other situational stuff and there is nothing like a backpack in this game. As the game goes on more and more needed items are needed and I hear once you get to the really late game you have like 5 slots left. The devs will not budge on this because they love the idea of pants being put on making you able to carry less.
Tools also need to be repaired, but you can do this for free. The only reason they need to repair is so that you keep running back to base to do it and you keep wasting your time. This game has a lot of padded out time in it from when the game had less content that remains. 
Anyway, The mining thing takes a long time because you need to mine it, carry it back in small amounts to not be over weighted, wait for it to smelt, and then carry it back to chests (this is more annoying than it sounds because of how little metal you can carry). Default settings make it so you can't even portal metal. Every thing in this game  is made to pad out runtime. After the second biome the next two are just the same thing with a different coat of paint but more insufferable for reasons I will get into later.

Lets talk about the combat and stamina system.
This is the worst part for me and the two are deeply intertwined. Stamina regenerates slowly in this game and is used by pretty much every action other than plain walking. Want to swing any tool or weapon? stamina. Dodge? stamina. Run or jump? stamina. And it is not a small amount for any of these. Stamina needs you to have a rested bonus to regenerate faster than a disabled snail. Rested bonus is your reward for making a cozy base and it is kind of ok but it is just a cozy way of the game making you run back to base every twenty minutes at minimum. Rested bonus has an evil twin though, wet debuff is terrible and often unavoidable. No matter what you do when you are wet you will always be barely scraping by on stamina. This is a major problem because one biome is the SWAMP where it rains 24/7. 
Ok time for some combat talk now that I have introduced stamina. This combat feels like if someone looked at soulslike combat and thought "huh, what if I made it worse in literally every way?" Every enemy feels like it attacks in slow motion but they hit like a truck. If you dodge but get caught on terrain I hope you made peace with any god you worship if you do that. Combat often evolves into walking away from the enemy you are fighting as you regain stamina before going back in to whittle down the often very tanky enemies. You can eat food that gives a lot of stamina instead of health but it does not increase the rate it regains. No matter what in a short amount of time you will end up waiting for stamina to regenerate regardless of if you ate stamina food or health food. (I think I forgot how apain it can be to get the food but trust me, it is a whole grind so you can do another grind without being oneshot by a fodder enemy. I actually got two shot by a mosquito once). The game also has an awful parry system that really does not work well. It gives you an extremely long window to parry after you put your shield up but you have to have a bunch of total HP if you want to not get one shot by the enemy you are attempting to parry. I am not kidding. If you don't have a magic amount of total HP the game does not tell you that varies between enemies and their attacks different attacks a parry just ends up being a hit on you. Parrying even when done right drains so much stamina that often you can't even punish them for getting parried. A parry also launches the enemy away from you, making it even harder to capitalize on the parry. Even with all I just said parries are by far the least insufferable way to do combat.
I did not even talk about how the swing animations can't be tilted up or down. This makes combat on a slight hill practically unplayable until you lure them onto a flatter area. This would be forgivable if one of the biomes was not THE ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ MOUNTIANS. I mean, genuinely what the hell were they thinking? These cliffs are insanely steep. You pretty much need to just shoot everything with bows or throw spears to kill anything safely. Combat is just so unapproachable in this area that I never really did any of it unless completely forced. 
You also need to collect 200lb eggs on this mountain to summon the boss so that is fun. That is by far the majority of the weight you can carry in one item that you need to collect a bunch of then bring to the top of a ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ MOUNTIAN.
So yeah I always get burnt out at the mountians. I also forgot that the boss fights can be so far away from spawn that you end up going through way later game areas to get to it and end up oneshot by some fodder like a mosquito or something. All the bosses and mini bosses are such tanks that you will be fighting them for 20+ minutes sometimes. Me and my friends end up cheesing them often not because they are hard but because it is just so much less mentally exhausting to cheese an enemy than to walk way from it for most of the 20 minutes as you regain stamina.
This game is just not fun and is 100% set on padding out runtime. I didn't even get into half of the grind because this review has been mentally exhausting to write kind of like this game is to play. The game is 50% hitting rocks, 20% walking way from enemies while out of stamina, 10% waiting at base for something/farming, 10% fighting, 10% building, and 10% of whatever else the game has. No part is really enjoyable other than some of the views in game.

Stuck in Dev hell with Devs that add useless bloat and focus more on their plushies then updates to the game,  Game is fun no doubt but Will never give a positive review to greedy devs who can't do their job lmao.
It's a very fun game and very easy to lose track of time in when you're having fun.

This game makes you feel creative and adventurous. Exploring the world, building bases, and surviving are fun, especially when playing with friends. It encourages creativity, exploration, and teamwork, and every adventure feels rewarding. The game has a great balance of challenge and freedom, which keeps every session exciting and memorable. ❤︎

Ridiculous grind fest. Took me 3 hours to gather, haul and smelt 4 times my carrying capacity in copper ore only to gets you 2 items out of it. I ain't doing that again.
The core of the game is very interesting and can be pleasant. I do enjoy the slower pace, but for the amount of time and ressources required for the smallest thing, I can't recommend.

The game, when it works, is fun and has been for years. What is not fun is the lack of demonstrable skill at Kinetic Games in understanding and developing their own game and giving it the respect it, and the players, deserve. 

The Customisation update that has rolled out has embarrassingly poor quality rigging and animation - animators please next time get a mirror and a video camera and try the poses out BEFORE you model them in game and learn about IK constraints to stop bone-breaking poses, critical mechanical issues that break gameplay - various items and tools do not function as they should leading to deaths and gear/money loss, and an overall rushed, untested, unpolished and frustrating experience. 

For a game this far into it's dev cycle and built off the back of pre-made and un-updated assets, and with a significant amount of income, it's shameful to push out something as poor quality as this. If the game mechanics don't work you cannot play the game.

Do better Kinetic Games.

Phasmophobia exhibits some of the worst qualities of modern videogame development. This ostensibly singleplayer game sports server-based account checking that has banned players for modifying their saves for more money or experience (when unlocks are behind level progression, but we'll get there,) and even for just modding the game, even if they only play with friends or solo.

Progression is locked behind level ups. Very quickly you'll find that you need to play in the hardest difficulties and perform all objectives for extra rewards at the end of the stage. That will get you 1/3rd of the way to a level up. This is just to unlock the chance to BUY items that would be fun to play with. Then you have to actually buy them, which for some reason costs thousands just to unlock the tier.

Fun, inoffensive bugs get patched out, like the ability to break doors off their hinges, solely because streamers could use it to go through doors slightly faster. Also, the game has been made progressively harder because of these streamers as well, including progression becoming snail paced. Turns out that if you play the same game for 5 hours a day, 5 days a week, you become pretty good at it; it would be a shame if you are then used as a measuring scale to make the rest of the playerbase have a miserable time in comparison.

Now we are doing 2x EXP and Money weeks? Just why? This game likes to believe it's GTA Online or some live service game while failing to realise that the playerbase that shot it up to fame left long ago when it became a slog.

I didn't even play the latest update after seeing what a disaster it is. The new player models look bad, break animations and the update has introduced numerous bugs. There are much better indie games out there you can play with friends which respect your time much more. Just play one of those instead. Maybe pour one out for the game this used to be.

P.S.: There's 4 years between Witcher 1 and Witcher 2, and 4 more years between Witcher 2 and Witcher 3. Six years later, this game is still in early access and we're still waiting for a real "horror" update.

I've tried to hold my tongue for as long as I can, I'm not a developer, so I don't know all the work that goes into making a game, but as a player I can say that the game doesn't even work the way it's supposed to. How exactly do you take an 'overwhelmingly positive' game and turn it into this, and keep it that way when more than half of your players are saying the same things? To me, it should have been reverted as soon as people were saying, well, everything that has already been said. I couldn't even get passed the training level, because the spiritbox didn't work. It could hear my voice, but the ghost just doesn't respond. How are you supposed to gain new players when nothing works? Especially since a lot of the players that have been here for years are leaving due to a lack of caring. 

It saddens me to see a game I loved in such a state. There is "jank" in a game, and then there is this. When the game stops working as it is intended, you do not say 'get used to it', you admit you did wrong and make it better. The "apology" we got came with a hidden message about the Alan Wake collab, and it was kind of just a slap in the face. It has been a little over two weeks and the fundamentals of the game are still broken to the point where it isn't enjoyable. Everything should have been put on hold until it was completely fixed, but according to the road map you're still working on new maps, and re-vamps, as well as the bug fixes. I wished nothing but the best for this game, but I don't see it changing anytime soon.

It is with a heavy heart that I write this review saying that I will be leaving Phasmo, and probably avoiding anything these developers may do in the future. I had some great times on here, but the fun is over for me. I can not recommend this game in its current state. I will be looking for another game that my friends and I can play.
Versions 0.17.0.0 and higher are just unplayable. The latest updates just ruined this game. The game became absolutely casual with millions of bugs.

Phasmophobia used to be one of those games you could hop on with your friends and instantly have a blast. Half the fun wasn’t even the ghost hunts themselves it was the stupid conversations in the truck, asking how many pickles a ghost could fit up its ass while everyone’s sanity hit zero. The game had charm, tension, and that perfect balance between horror and goofy co-op chaos. Bugs were rare, ghosts mostly worked as intended, and the challenge actually felt rewarding instead of frustrating.

Now? It feels like the devs completely lost sight of what made the game special in the first place. Patch after patch has chipped away at the identity of the game, and the costume update was the point where it really started feeling like Phasmophobia lost its authentic atmosphere entirely. Instead of creepy immersion, the game now feels like spaghetti code wrapped in cheap cosmetics and held together with duct tape sweating in the middle of July.

What makes it worse is how disconnected the developers seem from the longtime community. Veteran players who stuck with the game through all its growth give feedback because they actually care, and it feels like that feedback gets ignored, mocked, or flat out pissed on like you’re standing in an R. Kelly meet and greet line. The attitude basically feels like, “Don’t like it? Go play something else.”

Honestly, that’s exactly what a lot of people are doing now. Games like "The Other Side" are starting to feel more worth supporting because at least those devs seem to understand that listening to players matters. Phasmophobia used to feel like a passion project built by people who loved horror games. Now it feels like the soul got patched out somewhere along the way.

Aside from the bugs (Except getting stuck behind a toilet and dying one time), this game is tons of fun. I really enjoy playing this with my husband and our son when we can convince him to join us. It was a little scary at first but once I got into the flow, it was highly enjoyable.
how does this game feel less polished than it did 3 years ago? the animations are awful, the controls are somehow worse, and the equipment is ♥♥♥♥. what the ♥♥♥♥ happened?

I honestly don’t even know how to review this game properly… the ghosts are interactive?? 😭😂 lol jk

But kidding aside, it’s a really fun game to bond with friends. Most of the gameplay is basically arguing about who’s going to set up the equipment and who’s getting left alone in the ghost room while everyone else hides in the safest spots hahaha. It’s chaotic, scary, and super fun with friends. (˶ᵔᗜᵔ˶)ﾉﾞ

I've played this game for several years now and followed their journey to become a complete game. In the beginning, I was hopeful and excited for everything they had in store and with every update, it got progressively better. After all, with almost 300 hours on this game, it shows I've enjoyed it, and had some really good times hunting with friends.

However, the last two years have been anything but great for this game. To still be in early access, despite it not being that heavy of a game, feels like a joke. Their roadmap for 2026, still has major updates planned (e.g. horror 2.0) that has been promised since several years back. Their promises of release dates for major events, always pushed back due to different reasons. Their latest character update that has been in the works for years, somehow managed to take the game several steps back as it is packed with bugs, and quite honestly, looks absolutely terrible. Their holiday events are just copy paste of previous years. All these faults, and they choose to put their time and resources on things like unnecessary collabs, a board game and a movie instead. I'm not saying these are bad things, but I'd like to see a FINISHED game, before they pour their time and effort into other related things.

I want to love this game. I did when I first started playing it and I have been so excited for everything it had potential to be. But I feel like the people behind this game are doing anything but finishing the game. I have seen games with just a fraction of this studio's resources produce games of better quality, significantly faster. I have lost my trust and slowly losing my support for this game as it, somehow, manages to get worse over time. I don't recommend anyone to play this until it's out of early access. Which clearly, won't be anytime soon.
Back when the game was in its first iteration, I feel like it was more fun. Ever since it got revamped, it's just been very buggy and frustrating to play.

This game has been in early access for almost 6 years. It still looks like it was made before 2008. The recent disaster that was the "character creation" update has only confirmed to me that I fell for a scam game. The devs got their money and have done the absolute least with the game. Do not buy this game, don't fall for the scam.

as someone who loved this game, i've never been so upset with changes that have been made. now i understand that things had to be changed to release the game, but god almighty what happened? this ain't the game i know. 
and instead of i don't know fixing it, you do a collab with alan wake, that's sad. i want to love this game, but i can't with the state that it's in. i love ghost hunting and all things paranormal but this game has become something that angers me more then brings me joy and adventure. as someone who has played a lot of this game (not on this account) it's heartbreaking to see something so wonderful be so painfully abused by its creators. i'm deeply disappointed and will be letting this game rot in my steam library till something get better. rip the good phasmophobia, may you never be forgetten.

After years this game ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ sucks, the audio, animations, graphics, vr support such a joke. Then not even moving to unreal engine

They don't even care about their community so just money grab
Since the most recent update this game has lost its sparkle. If you want a spooky multiplayer experience, look into Outlast Trials or Repo instead. Unfortunate what they did to Phasmo :/

The devs have failed us as a community and no longer serve gamers but their wallets.

Save your wallet for a game with a kinder heart to it. Do not buy this game.

Used to be a very fun game, but now the devs refuse to listen to the players. Changed the most popular maps from good to terrible, changed the interation and item system from funtional and streamlined to animated and clunky, changed all the good parts of the game and refuse to fix the bugs. Don't waste your money and time, at least not until the dev team screw their head on straight.

I love Phasmophobia conceptually. I'm a big fan of the atmosphere, and I enjoy the depth of the ghost types and their behaviors. It's a really fun learning curve! Some people are pretty particular about the revamps of the maps, but I actually quite enjoy them— granted I'm not much of a competitive player (obviously considering my hours played), so take my opinion on that with a grain of salt because I'm fairly certain most problems are with the lack of viability for things like looping. But all in all I've enjoyed most of the updates that have come out, new content and adjustments to existing things alike. I even enjoyed the split media update! I like recording videos in the van and having audio recordings! 

I've always had a lot of issues with it on the technical side, that's nothing new even if it's a bit frustrating; as far as I'm aware all of these were pretty well known, but they were also mostly possible to ignore or work around. Prior to this update I'd mostly joke about the predictability of the problems and try to keep in good spirits (haha) while sorting them out. Unfortunately upon opening the game to play it with friends this time around I have to say it's become pretty much unplayable at this point :(

Mind you I'll still do so when I get to, I know myself better than to think I will actually put a game down just because it is frustrating, but the unfortunate reality is I can't reasonably recommend it to people anymore. Worse, I'm hesitant to recommend playing it with the people who do own it because of how much of a struggle it is for them to deal with the bugs. It feels a bit like a waste of money when it's been in EA for so long and so many basic things still aren't fixed. The voice chat fails half the time, when it does work the voice dependent things like the spirit box don't, the game still feels Terribly optimized in every way, and somehow the new update with the event has made it worse.

It usually runs not the best for those of us with less than stellar computers (not me personally), but something about the Alan Wake event has made it stutter excessively. I think it's the TVs in the lobby? I'm not 100% sure, but either way when your computer meets a bit above the minimum specs like theirs do, and everything is set to the lowest option possible, and it *still* chugs upon just loading in, it is a bit disappointing. Then when actually going to any given location and playing through an investigation there's a very high chance of it crashing or booting someone, which is equally disappointing. I'd blame it on internet, but it's not a problem we  really have with other games. The new update(s?) also somehow introduced some collision physics that I don't think were intentional, so I spent half the time playing with an item misbehaving. Fine and amusing when it's something like a freezer door being suspended midair, much worse when it's an hour or two of objects repeatedly getting stuck inside each other and banging very loudly.

Tangentially related, I feel like the lack of any kind of sound mixer is kind of annoying at this point. I know it may be a bit of a spoiled assessment, a lot of older or even just less intensive games don't have anything but a master volume slider after all, but the game has been around for like 6 years? And I can't even change the ambient sound volume? Good heavens, the carnival music in the crossover event is so loud compared to everything else that I don't even want to touch the camp at the moment. I figure it's to curb some sort of cheating online in the same way that mods to increase the player amount aren't allowed (which is unfortunate because God the bigger maps could really benefit from an extended party size. I do not think the amount of online content justifies the mod ban even if I'm not someone who cares to mod the game personally), but I can't imagine the potential to cheat outweighs the need for that sort of thing in a game where ghost footsteps feel like being hit directly in the skull when the master volume is above 10. 

Sorry, this is very long. I have more I could say about it all, but the summary is it's kind of sad. Again I acknowledge that my hour count does not carry very much weight here, but I feel so terrible for those who have devoted far more time to it over the years it's been out only to have their biggest complaints not addressed repeatedly. Especially when the patch notes imply things should be fixed. I want to see Phasmo grow and thrive. I want it to be in our game rotation. I want to fully enjoy the direction it goes in. I want to support the developers, because regardless of any sentiments expressed here I do believe they have worked hard to get the game where it is; whether or not they've gotten to the point things are rushed and unpolished rather than just being unlucky in the "bugs we didn't notice before passing the update through" department, I do believe that fact should be acknowledged. I'd like to go back to recommending the game to people, genuinely. My optimism hasn't faltered. The only thing that has is my ability to dismiss my frustrations as growing pains. 

[I apologize if any of this is rude or incorrect, I don't write game reviews like ever. If anyone wants to tell me if I'm wrong on something such as the volume sliders I'd be glad to correct the review. In fact, it would make me very glad to know I just somehow missed it over it being nonexistent. I don't want to beat down on the game, especially needlessly.]

Phasmophobia is a game that has always been broken and I believe the community always expected to be broken to some degree, but the newest character animations and customizations update has ruined it to a point that I don't believe it'll ever recover from. And, no, I don't see this as me being a doomer or whatever and thinking the game is ruined simply because I don't like the update. The devs have shown they don't know what they're doing all the way to a main dev in a now-deleted post saying everyone will get "used to the jank" that this update introduced. 

Ok, let's just ignore the animations jank for a second then and focus on what else this update broke:
Some maps don't work as well or at all, an example being Sunny Meadows Restricted which has somehow become unrestricted and now loads the full version of the map. How that gets broken in an update for animations and customizations is incredible to me.
Items have become far less unresponsive, an example being the various medias (photo/video/audio) working only half the time for me, especially so for tier 1 and tier 2.
And the game itself has become far more unplayable, an example being how sometimes I'm unable to select "next" after finishing a contract, meaning I don't get the money and xp rewards. This feels GREAT after an hour-long contract.

I'll finish this review by saying I think this update has made me realize how much of a cult the devs have managed to create for this game, not intentionally, but that's what they've done. They haven't used the millions of dollars they've made to hire professional people, no, they've continued to develop this nearly 6 year old game slowly and with talent that often doesn't know what they're doing outside of the amazing maps and ghost types they've added. The characters look worse now, the animations shouldn't be this bad, and the game shouldn't be suffering this much. 

And if one of the Kinetic Games fans finds this review, I'll say this (provided you've made it this far): don't bother wasting your energy typing a reply, you've already won. The company has made millions, they continue to get good sponsorships (like Alan Wake), and they have good merch sales. I gave them my money around 5 years ago, and there's no taking that back, not that I'd want to because I've enjoyed the game up to this point. I'll continue to play periodically, but it's clear they have no intentions of fixing what they've done to the game, so it's time I move on to the other games collecting dust in my library. 

Thank you for the amazing 5-ish years of ghost hunting, Kinetic Games, and I do mean that, but this is clearly the end of an era for the game. I will not change this review if the game does get fixed because I want it to serve as a timestamp, but I will add an "EDIT" or whatever to show I no longer feel the way this review says I do.

I think I miss Paladins.

Paladins is one of those games that people constantly compare to Overwatch, but honestly, it does enough things differently to stand on its own.
At its core, it’s a fast-paced hero shooter with tanks, supports, flankers, and damage dealers, but the loadout and card systems give it much more customization than most games in the genre. Being able to modify abilities and playstyles for each champion makes matches feel more flexible and creative.

The game also deserves credit for having a huge roster of champions, many of which feel genuinely unique. Some characters are simple and beginner-friendly, while others are complete chaos once mastered. Finding a champion that clicks with your playstyle is easy.

When Paladins is working properly, it’s honestly extremely fun. Matches are fast, abilities are flashy, and team fights can become absolute madness in the best possible way.

The good:
- Large and diverse champion roster
- Loadout/card system adds customization
- Gameplay is fast and satisfying
- Easy to get into
- Many champions feel unique
- Matches usually move quickly
- Good variety of playstyles
- Free-to-play without being completely unbearable
- Crossplay support is nice
- Some champions are ridiculously fun to master

The bad:
- Bugs and technical issues have always been a problem
- Matchmaking can feel awful sometimes
- Balance changes are inconsistent
- Hit registration occasionally feels questionable
- UI can feel messy and outdated
- Some champions become extremely annoying to fight
- Ranked mode can be frustrating
- Servers can be unstable
- Community can get very toxic
- Feels less polished than bigger competitors

Overall, Paladins is a surprisingly fun hero shooter that has managed to survive for years despite all its problems. It’s buggy, chaotic, and occasionally frustrating, but the gameplay itself is genuinely addictive once you find champions you enjoy playing.

I’ve played a lot of battle royale games over the years, but honestly, Naraka: Bladepoint feels like something completely different in the best possible way. Most BR games focus only on guns and positioning, but Naraka mixes fast-paced movement, intense melee combat, grappling hooks, mind games, and mechanical skill in a way that feels incredibly satisfying once you get into it.

At first, the game can feel overwhelming. There are so many mechanics to learn — combos, counters, focus attacks, movement tech, hero abilities, weapon matchups, and timing. But that’s exactly why the game feels rewarding. Every fight feels like an actual duel instead of just “who saw who first.” When you win a fight in Naraka, it genuinely feels earned.

One of my favorite things about the game is the movement system. The grappling hook alone makes traversal insanely fun. Flying through trees, chasing enemies across rooftops, escaping impossible situations, or suddenly turning a fight around with mobility never gets old. The game feels fast, fluid, and stylish almost all the time.

The combat is probably the strongest part of the game. It’s easy to understand the basics, but there’s a very high skill ceiling. Good players can outplay opponents through timing, reads, positioning, and smart decision-making instead of pure luck. Every weapon also has its own personality. Some weapons feel aggressive and explosive, while others reward patience and precision. Learning different weapons keeps the gameplay fresh even after many hours.

Visually, the game is beautiful. The environments, character designs, outfits, animations, and overall atmosphere are genuinely impressive. The art direction gives the game a unique identity compared to other battle royale titles. Some moments honestly feel like scenes from a martial arts movie.

Another thing I appreciate is that Naraka creates memorable moments constantly. Whether it’s surviving a chaotic 1v3, winning a duel with 1 HP left, landing a perfect counter, or swinging into a final fight out of nowhere, the game creates stories naturally. Few multiplayer games give me that adrenaline rush anymore, but Naraka still manages to do it.

Of course, the game isn’t perfect. The learning curve is brutal for new players, and sometimes matchmaking can feel rough when you run into extremely experienced players. There are also moments where server issues or ping differences can be frustrating. But despite those problems, the core gameplay is so strong that I keep coming back.

What really makes Naraka special for me is that it rewards improvement. The more time you invest, the more the game opens up. You start understanding mind games, spacing, combo routes, movement tricks, and team coordination on a deeper level. It feels satisfying to notice your own progress over time.

Overall, I genuinely think Naraka: Bladepoint is one of the most unique multiplayer games released in recent years. It combines skill-based combat, mobility, strategy, and cinematic action better than almost any other battle royale game out there. It can be difficult, frustrating, and competitive, but it’s also incredibly fun and rewarding once it clicks.

If you enjoy games with deep combat systems and high skill expression, I absolutely recommend giving Naraka a real chance.

After 1,000 hours in NARAKA: BLADEPOINT, I can say the game offers a great variety of modes, whether you prefer PvE, PvPvE, or classic PvP. Overall, the gameplay is excellent, but the UI is one of the game’s weakest aspects. It feels overly cluttered, almost like a pilot’s cockpit, and navigating through menus can be frustrating at first. However, once you get used to it, it becomes manageable.

The combat system is the game’s biggest strength. The fighting-game-style mechanics, including combos, blocking, and parrying, are both fun and complex, allowing for a high skill ceiling and many different playstyles. It genuinely feels like a unique and well-designed fighting battle royale experience.

That said, the game’s performance and server issues have become increasingly frustrating. The amount of high-ping players, especially players joining from other regions just to gank newer players, can make matches feel unfair. On top of that, the optimization has noticeably worsened over time. I used to run the game smoothly on my low-end laptop, but now I experience constant lag spikes, with my ping jumping from 60 to 200 unexpectedly, making the game almost unplayable at times.

Recently, the game feels like it has been going downhill due to these technical issues. If the developers do not improve the optimization and server stability soon, I may stop playing until those problems are properly addressed.

Half-Life and its incredible demo Uplink prove that, even after all these years, they are not museum pieces, but masterpieces that still play like absolute beasts today. One of the game’s most groundbreaking aspects for its time was its seamless storytelling that never takes control away from the player, alongside the HECU military AI that still outclasses many modern FPS games. Soldiers flanking you from behind and flushing you out of cover with grenades was absolutely insane for that era.

From the iconic crowbar sound to the experimental Gauss Gun, the weapon variety is fantastic. The HEV Suit perfectly reinforces the atmosphere, while the physics puzzles are paced incredibly well throughout the game. The multiplayer side, HL:DM, is pure reflex and speed; with bunny hop mechanics, it still captures the chaotic yet fair competitive spirit of old-school FPS games. And thanks to the mod support that gave birth to giants like Counter-Strike, the replayability is practically endless.

My only real criticism is Xen. After the near-perfect atmosphere of Black Mesa, the low-gravity platforming sections slow the game’s momentum down and can feel frustrating in first person.

Even with that flaw, Half-Life — together with the short but impactful Uplink — remains one of the most important and rock-solid turning points in gaming history.

I was introduced to Valve over a decade ago with a copy of Half Life 2 given by a friend, but only now have I finished playing the one that started it all. It was fun to see the seeds of various design practices that would evolve to define Valve and game/narrative design as a whole. In spite of all the jank and outdated graphics, the gameplay holds up remarkably well for a nearly 30 year old game and the presentation is very charming. I wasn't there when this game was released so I'll never truly know how mindblowing any of this was, but that I barely felt any issues in gameplay in my context of modern gaming could be a testament to this game's brilliance. Overall, I loved my time with it.

Firstly, and most crucially: The game is a relic of its time. I think every issue I had with the game—unclear paths, bugs, and janky controls—is just a consequence of the game being a revolutionary title from 1998.
However, the “unclear paths” point brings me to my biggest takeaway from this game’s design: it forces you to be immersed in order to succeed. You need to think for yourself, try everything, and let people live if you want to receive their assistance. You aren’t just told exactly what to do at every moment and handheld down a path, like so many games do today. You need to figure out how to escape Black Mesa on your own. To that end, the game’s demanding of your immersion and concentration is probably my favorite thing about it.
I looked up what to do several times, but in my opinion, every time I didn’t know where to go was just me being an idiot, except for two different occasions: first, me not obtaining a pistol at the start of Waste Processing because I didn’t follow the guard, leading me to do the whole chapter without it before googling the pistol’s location and restarting the chapter; and second, the flooding of the Lambda Core bugging so that it didn’t fill all the way because I turned the two valves in quick succession. Because of those two incidents, the only two changes I would make to this game are to have the guard die to the headcrabs he’s fighting, and to fix that bug with the flooding.
Sure, the game sometimes aggravated me or bored me, but I cannot in good faith say that any other part of this game could be changed without worsening the final product overall. The game’s goal is to immerse you into its world and make you feel the exact amounts of fear, tension, and power that Freeman himself would feel; it accomplishes that pretty much flawlessly. After finally beating Half-Life for the first time, I get why it’s a legendary classic.

Playing through the game for the first time as an adult and in today's time, one would think that the age of the game would drag it down a bit, but I am happy to say that even the bones of HL2 can truly be felt here; it's like when playing Destiny 2 and going back to Halo 1, and one is able to sense the "percussor." I know that may sound silly, since this is the Half-Life that preceded the sequel, but sometimes, sequels can feel really differently (for better or worse). The gameplay is not only solid, but so is the soundtrack; a lot of great songs here! A minor complaint I do have are the spawn locations of enemies, but it's not that big of a deal.

Fun to play, but absolutely miserable to 100%. Even though I mainly used the Meowcano glitch to kill monsters after defeating Shara Ishvalda and entering the Guiding Lands, the RNG in this game is still pure ball torture. And it’s not just RNG getting in your way, it’s also a bunch of game mechanics you barely notice during a casual playthrough. For example, the game forces you to leave an expedition and come back every 3 Paw Swap cycles, so you can’t grind it efficiently even if you have enough resources to do it all day. Combine that with RNG-based treasure tip drops and 60 total treasures, and congratulations - you’re stuck there forever.

Crown farming (even with investigation save-scumming and an overlay that shows whether a monster has a crown or not) was ridiculously inconsistent. Sometimes you get miniature and gold crowns from Nergigante back-to-back, and sometimes you spend 6 hours straight resetting for a stupid gold crown on Vaal Hazak. I’m honestly scared to imagine how people farm this garbage on consoles. I saw one guy on Reddit who farmed a gold crown for Vaal Hazak for an entire year and still didn’t get the crown he needed.

There are also a bunch of smaller issues:

1) The idiotic camera auto-focus on monsters after increasing Scoutfly level by picking up tracks, even if you’ve already encountered the monster before. That mechanic got me killed several times.
2) The inability to skip the time of day. You literally have to wait around 15 minutes, which is especially irritating when you’re trying to catch endemic life that only spawns during the day or night.
3) Another “fantastic” feature: you can’t skip the 60-second timer after killing a monster (which, as far as I know, you could do in previous games). During a normal playthrough you barely notice it, but when you’re farming elder dragons, which you can’t trap-spam (either for research levels or Master Rank), it intentionally slows down your progress.
4) The absolutely stupid Palico AI during battles, which you have zero control over (more RNG, because apparently there wasn’t enough already). This becomes especially irritating when performing the Meowcano glitch over and over again.
5) RNG-based investigation drops. No comment.
6) Broken hitboxes on some monsters, like Fatalis.
7) Flying bastards like Rathian and Rathalos, which fly across the entire maze that is the Ancient Forest, land for 30 seconds, and then immediately take off again.

To sum it up, this game is good but every part of this game is filled with ♥♥♥♥♥♥ kind RNG. Sure, sometimes it works in your favor, but personally, I’d rather have more control over the game instead of feeling like I’m fighting the rng more than the monsters themselves.

Monster Hunter: World pissed me off for the first two hours.

I am being honest. I had not really played a hunting game like this before, so a lot of it was user error, but also my ex did not explain how to play properly, which should honestly count as a crime against onboarding.

At first, I was confused, annoyed, getting knocked around, not understanding the flow, and wondering why everyone acted like this was fun. The weapons felt weird, the monsters felt rude, and I was absolutely not having my elegant gaming moment.

But then it clicked.

Once I finally started understanding the rhythm of the game, I fell straight down the rabbit hole. Suddenly I was tracking monsters, learning attack patterns, crafting gear, upgrading weapons, preparing for hunts, and getting fully invested in taking down giant creatures that had personally offended me.

The loop is addictive as hell. Hunt the monster, gather parts, make better gear, hunt bigger monster, get humbled, repeat. It sounds simple, but the satisfaction of slowly getting stronger and actually improving is so good.

The world is gorgeous, the monsters are incredible, and every hunt feels like its own little adventure. Sometimes you feel powerful. Sometimes the monster picks you up, folds you like laundry, and reminds you to respect nature.

Monster Hunter: World went from “I hate this” to “wait, I need one more hunt” real fast.

It has a learning curve, but once it gets its claws in, good luck. I was addicted.

If you are wondering whether to jump into Monster Hunter: World and its Iceborne expansion, the answer is a resounding yes. Between the two, you are looking at hundreds of hours of beautifully crafted, addictive gameplay.
Why You Should Play:
Unmatched Combat: The 14 weapon types are incredibly deep, each playing like its own distinct class with unique combos, playstyles, and satisfying weight.
Living, Breathing Worlds: The environments are stunning and full of life. Watching monsters turf-war against one another makes the map feel like a real ecosystem.
Iceborne is a Masterclass in Expansions: Iceborne doesn't just add content—it takes everything the base game did well and turns it up to 11. The Master Rank challenges, the Hoarfrost Reach region, and the deep endgame provide massive value.
Endless Grind and Reward: The core loop of hunting beasts to craft epic armor and weapons is as addictive as ever.
A Quick Tip for Newcomers:
The learning curve can feel steep, but the payoff is immensely satisfying. Just be sure to avoid using the overpowered "Defender" gear, as it is meant to rush you through the base game. Take your time, learn your weapon, and enjoy the journey!

I've been playing Monster Hunter: World/Iceborne for quite a while now (it's my first Monster Hunter game, too), and it has become one of my favorite titles of all time. I enjoy many single-player experiences, but this game truly shines when played with friends. I've completed it more than once with people I know, and I can confidently say it's worth it. That said, if you're more of a solo player, you'll still find plenty to enjoy in Monster Hunter, it works just as well on your own. 

The game features a wide variety of awesome monsters. If you own Iceborne, some of the classics are introduced as well, which is very cool! The designs are stunning, and the level of detail is incredibly well crafted. You can really see the effort that went into making this one of the best hunting experiences out there.  

On gameplay aspects, MHW runs smoothly. I haven’t come across any bugs or inconveniences during my playthroughs, so if you were worried about that, don’t be. Just make sure your hardware meets the necessary requirements so everything runs fluently. 

Important: Monster Hunter is about patience. The first few missions and some others can be somewhat tedious, but trust me, it gets better. If you're up for a challenge and enjoy farming monsters to craft massive weapons and strange yet cool-looking armor sets, while also having adorable animal companions by your side, give it a try! 

I do suggest getting Iceborne, since it adds a lot of incredibly good content to the base game. Monster Hunter: World without Iceborne can feel short and a little unfulfilling if you're looking for more. So, don’t miss out on the ultimate hunting experience!

One of the greatest skill-based ranked games ever made. I've been playing Rocket League for over a decade now and it still feels like the benchmark for pure competitive gameplay. The developers have done a phenomenal job over time with quality-of-life updates and cosmetic additions that keep the game feeling fresh without changing what makes it so good in the first place.

The biggest thing that turns new players away is the skill gap. It looks simple at first glance, but once you get into ranked you realize just how in-depth the gameplay really is. Mechanics, car control, aerials, rotations, game sense, all of it. It takes real time and repetition to build muscle memory, and a lot of people don’t stick around long enough to push through that early learning curve. But if you do, it’s easily one of the most rewarding games out there.

The main downsides are smurfs and the removal of trading, which definitely took away some of that community feel and long-term engagement. Outside of that though, it’s still an incredibly skill based competitive experience, filled with a beautifully toxic community that somehow makes ranked both frustrating and entertaining at the same time.

Rating: 10/10 — one of, if not the greatest skill-based ranked game ever made, and it’s aged insanely well.

There are near-unending ways to experience Project Zomboid, yet every single one shows you a little more of why this game truly is excellent at EVERY SINGLE THING it sets out to do. An actual blueprint for apocalypse simulator developers everywhere, a true hallmark of genius at inventory management, heat management, skills, combat, maintenance, proactive management of all types. On every death, you'll dust yourself off, and know so much more. On every death, you'll mourn, sigh, and get it better. Because one day, the title screen will be wrong.

Project Zomboid is one of those games where people swear it’s the greatest zombie survival experience ever made, but half the time it feels less like surviving the apocalypse and more like working an unpaid full-time job in a dead-end warehouse during a depression.

The game starts with:
“This is how you died.”

And honestly after a few hours your response becomes:
“Yeah no ♥♥♥♥.”

Everything in this game takes forever.

Want to fight zombies?
Enjoy swinging a frying pan with the athleticism of a sleep-deprived office worker while your character gets exhausted after killing three zombies like he just ran a marathon carrying a refrigerator.

Want to level skills?
Read books for 20 real-life minutes.
Then dismantle 400 watches.
Then cook soup.
Then organize cans.
Then die because you forgot microwaving metal is bad.

Peak gameplay.

People call it “realistic,” but sometimes realism just becomes anti-fun. Your character gets:

* depressed
* bored
* sleepy
* anxious
* panicked
* overencumbered
* wet
* stressed
* injured
* sick

Bro I already have real life for that.

And the combat feels like trying to fight zombies inside a dream where your arms barely work. Half the difficulty comes from your character moving like they’re trapped in waist-deep mud at all times. Missing one shove can turn into your character getting dragged down by three zombies because the controls decided today was your death day.

Then there’s inventory management.
Dear god.

Open backpack.
Open secondary bag.
Move canned beans.
Move nails.
Move hammer.
Move screwdriver.
Become overweight by carrying two extra spoons.
Drop item.
Pick item up again.
Repeat for 6 hours.

People say:
“The immersion is incredible.”

Yeah because it accurately simulates the soul-draining experience of doing chores during societal collapse.

And the game’s pacing is absurdly slow.
You can spend an entire evening accomplishing absolutely nothing except:

* boarding one window
* sorting food
* finding a can opener
* getting bitten by a zombie hiding behind a bathroom door

Amazing.

The graphics also look like someone remastered The Sims 1 using sadness and cigarette ash. Fans defend it by saying:
“It’s about depth, not graphics.”

Okay cool.
Does the depth include spending 45 minutes looking for a digital watch so I can know what time it is before dying from eating burnt cabbage?

And the fanbase treats suffering like a personality trait.
They’ll say things like:
“I survived 7 months before my character died.”

Brother that is not a flex.
That is Stockholm syndrome.

The game constantly wastes your time in the name of realism:

* car won’t start
* generator out of fuel
* food rotting
* power shut off
* water shut off
* injured leg
* exhausted
* overweight
* panic moodle
* depression moodle

At some point you stop surviving zombies and start surviving user interface mechanics.

And yet somehow despite all this…
when you finally build a safehouse, survive winter, escape a massive horde, and barely make it home injured at night during a storm…

the game tricks your brain into feeling proud.

Which is honestly the most dangerous thing about Project Zomboid.
It gaslights you into believing digital suffering is rewarding.

The game can be difficult and overwhelming at first, but once you learn how things work, it becomes really addicting. It’s definitely one of the most unique zombie survival games I’ve played.
