Red Dead Redemption 2 started as a complete mystery to me. I had never played the first game, barely knew any of the characters, and the three previous times I tried to start it, I quit during the snowy opening hours. The reason was simple: the Western genre has never been one of my favorites, and games in the style of GTA never really appealed to me either. This game seemed to combine both of those things, which made it difficult for me to get invested.

What changed was a friend who kept insisting that I give it a real chance. Eventually, I did. I started my journey full of doubts, expecting to drop it once again. Instead, those doubts slowly disappeared as I became immersed in what the game truly is: a work of art disguised as a video game. Every hour revealed more of the incredible care and attention poured into its world, its characters, and its storytelling. By the time I finished it, I understood why so many people consider it one of the greatest games ever made.

World & Atmosphere:

Even as someone who isn’t particularly interested in the Wild West, I found myself completely absorbed by the setting. The game constantly teaches you something about the era, whether it’s the conflicts over land, the changing nature of civilization, or the everyday struggles of people trying to survive in a world that is leaving them behind. It feels authentic, lived-in, and endlessly fascinating to explore.

More importantly, the world feels alive. Not in the way games often advertise, but genuinely alive. People remember your actions, conversations feel natural, and every location seems to have its own story waiting to be discovered. It is one of the few worlds where I truly felt like my presence mattered.

Music:

The soundtrack is exceptional. Every song appears at exactly the right moment, whether during a quiet ride across the wilderness, a tense shootout, or one of the game’s many emotional scenes. Even now, hearing Unshaken is enough to give me chills and immediately bring back memories of my journey.

Storytelling:

This is where the game truly separates itself from most of the industry. The amount of love and effort poured into both the main story and the side content is impossible to ignore. The game constantly finds ways to surprise you, whether through moments of tension, tragedy, reflection, or hope.

Some of the most memorable moments come from situations that initially seem insignificant. A simple side quest can suddenly become deeply emotional, while a random encounter can leave you thinking about it long after you’ve finished playing. Few games manage to create that kind of impact consistently.

Characters:

This is where I have to make the biggest point of all.

Arthur is one of the best-written protagonists I have ever experienced in any video game. I don’t want to spoil anything, but the fact that you help shape the kind of person he becomes throughout the story makes him feel incredibly personal. There were countless moments where I found myself thinking something during a conversation at camp, only for Arthur to express the exact same thought moments later.

That level of humanization is what makes him special. Eventually, you stop thinking of him as a video game character and start seeing him as a real person with beliefs, flaws, struggles, and dreams.

The supporting cast is equally impressive. Whether it’s the charisma of Dutch, the journey of John, the determination of Sadie, the wisdom of Hosea, the calm nature of Charles, or the unsettling presence of Micah, every major character brings something meaningful to the experience.

Final Thoughts:

Very few games reach the narrative heights achieved by Red Dead Redemption 2. It is the kind of experience that stays with you long after the credits roll. You don’t simply finish the game and move on. You spend days, weeks, and sometimes even months thinking about the people you met, the choices you made, and the journey you experienced.

I started this game expecting to dislike it.

I finished it convinced that it is a masterpiece.

Final Verdict: ★★★★★ (5.0/5)

A landmark achievement in storytelling, world-building, and character writing. A game that goes far beyond entertainment and becomes something genuinely unforgettable. Everyone should experience it at least once.

RDR2 is a prequel to RDR1, which is a spiritual successor to PS2/XBOX title called Red Dead Revolver. This game sets the stage for the events of RDR1, and deepens the story of it by leagues. You aren't getting the full story of RDR1 without this gem.

We follow Arthur Morgan, down and dirty gunslinger on the run from the law with his mentor's Dutch and Hosea, and their ragtag gang of miscreants, criminals, and ♥♥♥♥♥♥. The year is 1899, and the Wild West is dying. The changing times threaten their way of life, and the "freedom" they so valiantly claim to fight for.

Mechanically, it's similar to other Rockstar games, with a heavy emphasis on realistic physics and weighty actions. The gunplay feels great, punchy and loud just like it should be, but still slow enough to feel like a western shootout, with PLENTY of time inbetween those fights to just enjoy the world itself. It's so artfully crafted that it makes you want to take time out of rootin and shootin to instead brush their horses hair, or enjoy some beans on a nice trail by a fire. Maybe while at camp, you see a herd of Elk, and opt in to do some hunting, scoring an Elk and taking the hide into town to have a new saddle made. 

The game sneakily gets you lost in the open countrysides and winding valleys, constantly distracted by more and more things to do. It's a real treat when you can just *exist* in a game world, and not feel compelled to rush thru or play into a meta. I have so much more to say about the story but fear I can't without spoiling most of it. It's one of the best western stories ever told, and it deserves your time.

Подходила я ко второму рдру со знанием концовки. Казалось бы, зная развязку должно быть не так интересно потреблять тот или иной контент, однако в данном случае рдр - про путь к финалу, нежели сам финал. 
Начало тягучее, неспешное, порой даже слишком неспешное в каких-то моментах, но тебе как будто дают сначала пообвыкнуться с окружающим миром, чтобы потом пускать на поток истории. Поначалу квесты основные, членов банды и просто второстепенные сыпятся рекой, какие-то более запоминающиеся, какие-то выполнишь и тут же забудешь. Помню, как впервые нарвалась на квест с маньяком, было взбудораживающе и интригующе, квест Мэри был тяжелым в моральном плане, т.к. ты понимаешь, что уже на тот момент Артур набедокурил так, что ничем исправить подобное уже бы и не вышло (да и вопрос, вышло бы что-то и раньше).
Каждый переезд лагеря был чем-то особенным, с маленькими мелочами, которые переносили с собой члены банды. Но, пожалуй, самым запоминающимся стал Шейди Бель благодаря лихо скачущему сюжету. И видит бог [spoiler]смерть Кирана[/spoiler] ударила под дых сильнее остальных на тот момент, потому что казалось, что тот наконец становился частью команды, пусть еще и без полноценного доверия остальных. И то, КАКИМ образом это презентовали в игре - это просто немыслимо в своей жестокости.
Вообще, многие персонажи прошли достаточно интересный путь от их первоначального образа в начале игры до того, кем они стали (или не стали, фить-ха) в финале. Малышка Тилли, заново приученная к жестокости окружающего мира, Датч, который раскрывался как пион каждой новой стороной, Джон Марстон, прирученный, как шелудивый пёс.
[i]И о мой бог, Сейди Адлер.[/i]
От перепуганной женщины, вмиг лишившейся привычной жизни, мужа, дома, к невероятно талантливой ганслингерше, которая вполне успешно подрабатывала охотником за головами, храня в сердце безмерное желание мести.
Сам Артур Морган предстаёт как целеустремлённый разбойник, со своими тараканами в голове и тайнами за душой. Он понимает и принимает свой образ жизни, глубоко внутри возможно понимает, что это всё ещё неправедно и неправильно, но иначе не может или не знает как. И всё сильнее это проявляется ближе к концу игры после ключевого момента, когда уже понятно - осталось недолго. Он не становится праведником, но отбрасывает целеустремлённость как у Датча, когда раньше они могли ходить по головам и никто им и слова не говорил. Дает шанс на нормальную жизнь вдове Даунс, сам снедаемый чем-то вроде вины.
Твердит из раза в раз, что он не добр и не благороден.

Это полноценная, порой неспешная, порой очень захватывающая история о людях поступающих неправильно и опасно, ради своего блага, выцарапывая себе возможность начать жить заново.
И как говорят? "Сотрите мне память, чтобы я мог испытать это снова".

Red Dead Redemption 2 is one of the best examples of what an open-world game can be when every detail actually matters. The world feels alive in a way that goes beyond just size—it reacts, changes, and moves even when you’re not paying attention to it.

Just riding through the map can turn into an experience on its own. One minute you’re crossing quiet mountains or muddy trails, and the next you’re stumbling into some random encounter that feels like it could’ve been scripted for a movie. The game is packed with those small, unscripted moments that make the world feel real instead of just big.

What really sets it apart is how much care went into everything, from the way characters talk and move to how towns and wildlife behave over time. It’s slow in a deliberate way, but that pace is what makes the world feel grounded and believable instead of rushed.

At its core, it’s not just about having a huge map—it’s about making that map feel worth exploring.

This game is an emotional masterpiece with stunning visuals, unforgettable characters, and an incredibly immersive open world. Its slow pace won’t suit everyone, but the storytelling makes it unforgettable.

[b]Initial Thoughts:[/b] This review contains ZERO spoilers. Red Dead Redemption 2 is a massive, epic Western that represents the absolute pinnacle of Rockstar’s ability to tell a tale for the ages. It is truly one of the most remarkable story-driven journeys any gamer will ever embark on, but I would be lying if I said it doesn’t require a lot of patience to tolerate the technical issues that pop up their ugly heads far too often. My first major issue to address is the Rockstar Launcher. Where does Rockstar get off telling you when you can and can’t play a game you purchased just because their launcher doesn’t want to work properly? It is a total headache and a ridiculous thing to have to deal with. The game is also prone to occasional crashes and bizarre engine glitches. I stream my games to my friends, and I was riddled with FPS issues at first; the stream would completely turn into a slideshow until I realized there is some kind of error between windowed full-screen and regular full-screen mode. Once I messed with that for a while, everything stabilized and the performance was great. The game's settings are extremely finicky and can be unoptimized if you don’t select the correct options. Mind you, I’m playing on an RTX 5070 Ti, with 32GB of 6000MHz RAM and an i7-265K; it makes no sense that there would be any performance-related issues due to hardware, and of course, there isn’t once you get everything dialed in correctly. This game just likes to fight you. Yet, despite the technical issues and messy mouse-and-keyboard controls, the greatness of this game’s story forces you to forgive most, if not all, of its issues. I haven’t played the multiplayer, so I won’t speak on that at all.

[b]Story:[/b] The storyline and characters are pure art and deserve a 10 out of 10. It is fun, fresh, sad, dramatic, and deeply rooted in classic Western themes while offering something entirely unique. The pacing keeps you hooked, and just when you think you have the game's rhythm figured out, it completely flips the script on you. Chapter 5, in particular, was an unexpected and incredible shift that really made the world feel larger and more alive than I thought it was. I genuinely didn’t see any of that coming. This game is a masterclass in narrative tension. Outcomes change heavily depending on how you play and the choices you make; for example, acts of good or bad change Arthur’s demeanor and how he interacts with the world. It also brings different people in and out of the story, and alters cutscenes and who appears in them. Because of this, the game provides a massive amount of replayability.

[b]Characters:[/b] The characters are so full of life and actually matter to the world. Very few games have ever capitalized on a cast to the degree this one does. You simply grow to care about the gang. When the storyline gets dark, it genuinely hurts; it actually made me feel sick to my stomach sometimes, in the way a really good dramatic movie does. When characters die or exit the scene, it doesn’t just simply advance the plot; it makes you angry. Sometimes this leads into a powerful, hate-fueled gunfight which just ingratiates you even more into the story and the gang. By the time you reach the conclusion of the main portion of the game, the fates of these characters left me feeling both sad and happy. It was a great conclusion, and it honestly made me cry. It’s so epic, and I promise you the emotional investment here is massive and worth your time.

[b]Gameplay & Mechanics:[/b] Surprise, surprise, it’s an open-world action-adventure game. The game offers both third- and first-person perspectives; both are equally fun, and I found myself switching between them often.

You manage a gang camp and get to collect money to improve it, which is a satisfying loop that I really enjoyed. The game does a good job of giving you options to make money, whether you want to buy and sell horses and wagons, rob trains and stagecoaches, steal and sell peoples personnel belongings, or tackle various side missions. You’ll use this money to upgrade said camp, buy new supplies, horses, weapons, clothes, and other things. The game also includes classic tabletop games like blackjack, poker, and dominoes. You can make money off these too, but they are mainly there for fun and the love of the game. I do think Rockstar really could have implemented a better skip feature for the post-hand animations to speed up the transitions; it gets old watching the same animations over and over, and it breaks any flow you may be building.

Once you get a feel for the character movement and get your horse to trust you, the gameplay becomes a lot less clumsy and a lot more controlled. The Dead Eye mode is back from RDR1 and works well. You’ll need it in a lot of scenarios, so ensure you are keeping plenty of meds on hand to keep your Dead Eye bar full.

The game uses momentum-based movement, which I thought felt sluggish and heavy; I absolutely hate sliding around everywhere I go or my horse deciding she’s not going to jump over the fence and instead run headfirst into it. But take that with a grain of salt; that’s just my opinion. I understand that’s a major point of contention, and the likely consensus of players probably enjoy the movement, but I don't, and I want to warn everyone that this is how the game works.

Combat also carries some irritating design choices. If you don't land a headshot, enemies can feel like they are taking entirely too many rounds to kill. I will admit, this issue became less of a bother to me the more I played and learned to understand ammo and weapon choices for different scenarios. You have to recognize that this game was heavily inspired by old spaghetti westerns, and a lot of these design choices are meant to complement that style. But when your character randomly starts spinning in circles, or your horse decides the 'W' key suddenly means reverse, it gets old fast. You get real sick and tired of this game playing Uno-reverse cards on your ass, and it always seems to happen at the absolute worst moments.

Worse still is the game's obsession with stripping away player authority. The system forces your character to auto-holster long guns onto your horse. If you forget to manually pull them back out before dismounting, you are left stranded without your rifle when bushwhackers inevitably ambush you at the worst possible moments. The game also loves to force your hand during missions. For example, it completely locks out your stealth weapons in certain scenarios just to push the narrative the way it wants. Why on earth would I not want to be quiet at a federal prison where there are hundreds of guards? It makes zero sense and ruins freedom of choice. Again though, it's all done to push an incredible story, so I forgive them lol.

[b]Graphics & Sound:[/b] The game is gorgeous; it is truly one of the most beautiful visual achievements in the history of video games. The world feels alive, organic, and staggeringly vast. Seriously, stop and look at the clouds... LOOK AT THEM! From the ambient wildlife to the weather effects, the atmosphere is completely immersive, though the finicky waypoint system can occasionally pull you out of the experience when trying to navigate the terrain.

[b]Final Thoughts:[/b] Red Dead Redemption 2 is a game any self-respecting gamer must play through at least once. It is a stunning, emotionally exhausting narrative achievement that stays with you long after it's over. You won’t regret or forget this one.

Red Dead Redemption 2 is nothing short of a masterpiece. From the moment you step into the vast, meticulously crafted world of the American frontier, the game pulls you in with its stunning visuals, dynamic weather, and living ecosystem. Every town, forest, and mountain feels alive, and the attention to detail is extraordinary—from the way horses react to terrain to the conversations of NPCs going about their daily lives.

The story is deep, emotional, and unforgettable. Following Arthur Morgan and the Van der Linde gang, you experience loyalty, betrayal, and the struggle to survive in a changing world. The characters are rich and complex, each with their own motivations, and the voice acting and cinematic cutscenes make you truly care about their journey.

Gameplay is equally impressive. The combination of exploration, hunting, combat, and side activities like fishing or poker ensures that no two days in the game feel the same. The honor system adds meaningful choices, shaping how the world reacts to you.

Whether it’s riding across the mountains at sunrise, engaging in a high-stakes shootout, or simply watching the wildlife roam, Red Dead Redemption 2 delivers an immersive experience that is hard to forget. For fans of story-driven open-world games, this is an absolute must-play.

Battlefield 2042 had one of the roughest launches in the series, but after a lot of updates, it’s honestly in a much better state now.
At its core, the game still delivers those classic Battlefield moments where absolute chaos happens everywhere at once - tanks exploding, helicopters crashing into buildings, entire squads pushing objectives together, and random nonsense somehow turning into cinematic moments.

The gunplay feels solid, movement is smooth, and large-scale battles can be genuinely fun once everything clicks. Portal mode is also a great addition because it lets players revisit older Battlefield content and create some completely ridiculous custom game modes.

That said, the game still carries a lot of problems that stop it from reaching the same level as older Battlefield titles like Battlefield 3, 4, or 1. The specialist system remains controversial, some maps still feel too open, and the overall atmosphere lacks the gritty military vibe that older games had.

The good:
- Large-scale battles can be incredibly fun
- Gunplay feels satisfying
- Movement and traversal are smooth
- Battlefield chaos is still entertaining
- Portal mode is a great feature
- Vehicle combat can be exciting
- Much improved compared to launch
- Weather events look impressive
- Crossplay support is useful
- Some maps and modes are genuinely fun now

The bad:
- Launch damaged the game’s reputation badly
- Specialist system feels less immersive than classic classes
- Some maps feel too empty and oversized
- Teamplay feels weaker than older Battlefield games
- Bugs and server issues still happen
- Vehicle balance can be frustrating
- Atmosphere lacks the gritty feel of previous games
- Destruction feels downgraded compared to older titles
- UI and menus are awkward
- Still doesn’t fully feel like classic Battlefield

Overall, Battlefield 2042 went from being a disaster to being a decent multiplayer shooter. It’s much more enjoyable now than it was at launch, but even after all the improvements, it still feels like a weaker Battlefield game compared to the series at its peak.

It's a great game, you can finish it in a single dedicated Sunday. Gathering resources, exploring islands, and the co-op mode make it especially fun to play with friends. I highly recommend giving it a try.

A truly miserable experience.

The "open world survival craft" genre has become my favourite type of game, and Raft was on my radar for a long time. It was well-reviewed and I was saving it for a rainy day based on the assumption that it would be good. Unfortunately, it's not good.

Everything in the game seems unfinished. The movement is imprecise and janky. So is the combat, which the game expects you to participate in. None of it feels pleasant. Everything you do feels about 30% too slow -- from the walking and sprinting speeds, to the time between tool swings, to the time it takes between eating a food item and that item disappearing from your inventory and allowing a new item to be picked up in the slot it previously occupied. All of it will make you feel like you're playing through brain damage.

The crafting/harvesting/survival mechnics are not tuned well. You'll be running in repetitive ellipses on your raft attending to your tedious survival chores without a moment to breathe and take in the vibes. Your food and water meters deplete too quickly, and nutritional items replenish you too little.

The exploration of islands is very disappointing, and they're homogeneous slop piles full of graphical assets that look like you should be able to harvest or interact with them, but which you cannot, full of hostile creatures that are annoying to fight. Islands seem to spawn from a small roster of pre-gens, but they're universally boring.  The bespoke islands that are part of the story aren't much better, 

The shark who follows you all game is one of the most annoying mechanics ever devised. It will bite your raft every 5 minutes, and you either ward it off or let it destroy one (1) tile of raft. Either way you pay a slow, constant material tax for repairing your raft's shark-inflicted wounds. There's nothing interesting about interacting with the shark directly, whether you're trying to kill it, avoid it, or escape it, because your interaction is mediated through the game's sloppy, slow movement and combat systems. There aren't really any interesting items or equipment to alter the sharks behaviour (other than a bait item to distract it). The shark is on a hard respawn timer, so if you want to drop everything you're doing to kill it, you will earn yourself 3 minutes of peace. It could've been such an interesting nemesis system, that changes as the story advances and you can craft more complex gear, but instead your sharky foe is insanely undercooked like the rest of the game.

In terms of good things about Raft... well, as far as I'm aware, it was the first survival crafter to make your base a mobile vehicle, and that's a good gimmick that other games have since copied. Props for the idea. The music is also good, and there's at least one track that's an all-time jaunty wistful adventurin' banger.

I suspect the high review average for Raft is compromised by all the co-op players. A robust co-op mode can quite literally turn a bad mechanic into a fun time, as you groan and gripe about it with your buds. I can see the co-op experience being preferable to solo, but I can also see co-op becoming boring a lot quicker, once surviving stops being difficult.

Raft is like a C-tier or D-tier entry in the genre, and not worth playing until you've exhausted all the better games that are its peers. Even after that,

TLDR: Fun game if you don't expect much and you have company to play with it. Bad game if you look too hard at it. I looked too hard at it, so I didn't like it and I’m mostly just listing all the major reasons I could think of.

But, as people have mentioned, it's very buggy. Most of the time, it's fine and you can ignore the bugs since they're kinda harmless. But, if you do any fighting or do the story quests, it gets incredibly frustrating and the inventory lost is very punishing (losing "2/3 of your inventory" when it's actually more like everything and your morgage). Hit boxes are weird and enemies tend to hit you way more than they can hit you. Like, hitting you through walls while you can't kind of janky. So, you just end up avoiding fights entirely. 

Quick mentions, the game can be lackluster if you look at the seams too hard. The islands you find around are very repetitive and predictable after the first 2-3 encounters, having the same rewards n the same places. And I found myself ending up with a bunch of things in surplus you quickly have no purpose for that you either must throw away (fearing they will become relevant in the future again) or increase your raft size and then add chests for just for them. The eating and drinking mechanics are annoying if you don't always carry a lot of water and food with you since your character has the faster metabolism known to mankind. The nighttime sucks always and the story feels very chaotic (maybe because they released the chapters at different times and experimented with stuff?). 

Speaking of the story (no spoilers), it was original and it had good puzzles, I'll give it that. But I realized I only enjoyed them when I already knew how they went from vague memories of Markiplier playing it. But, if you have no idea, I find that the puzzles are really convoluted and weird, which you'd think would add charm to the game with the cartoony aesthetic. Instead, it just makes you sit there confused and kind of annoyed because you spent thirty minutes to an hour going around the location looking for the one thing you need and you end up just looking up online. The chapters felt connected in the weirdest way, making it hard to care or simply get attached to it.
 
 I'll be real with you guys; at the end, there was no fun. I just wanted it to end. I wanted to be proven that the game did get better in the late game when I saw the quality being mediocre. It was just so much useless back and forth for the sake of making you waste time. They did parkour segments, but the character physics isn’t made for it, and the hitbox is still buggy. They mixed puzzles with fighting without any warning. They added weird temporary mechanics. There’s a lot of wasted potential and empty zones. All that for one of the most unsatisfying endings I've seen. Like, yeah, there's a cutscene. Yet I’ve had single screens of texts be so much more satisfying than that. 

Anyway, I say all this negative, but the core game is ok. If you want a cheap game to play with friends that has lots of shenanigan potential, I’d imagine this would be real fun. But, as someone who wanted to play a good survival game on my own, it wasn’t it.

It's good but I would've liked more rafting in this raft game.

The game starts out pretty rough, you're in a constant struggle to keep your bars full, crops and raft protected and gathering resources to actually progress in the game. It's a frustrating part of the game because there's no down time and progress is slow.
Once you get over that hump, you reinforce the raft and start to get some down time and agency the game has a great upswing. Building your ship at your leisure and exploring some islands.
*But* This is the point where the game decides the raft is no longer the focal point of the game. It's just the place you stand for 10 mins as you travel between story islands. Story islands you slowly have to walk around, doing simple puzzles and jabbing animals with a spear. So, pretty dull and not exactly leaning into the whole 'raft' gimmick.

Usually a game, advances by giving new challenges to overcome as you progress, no. The most challenging and engaging part of this game is towards the start, and then it just drifts to sleep leaving you having prepared for a challenge that never comes.

Just participated in the Reforged Playtest. I wanted to share some feedback and issues I noticed.

Account transfer / missing items
- My Progetto 46 legendary camo was not transferred.
- My 21 Felice charms were converted into 21 WZ-120-1G FT charms.
- None of my Reforged Rings currency was transferred to the new account, even though this currency was created specifically for this event.
- The tank restoration tokens in the Inventory say "May contain ...", as if they were standard lootboxes. This doesn't make sense: restoration gives you back the tank 100% of the time.
- The hotkey layout was not transferred, now I have to do it myself again.

Controls / usability
- I play on a MacBook using the touchpad, and scrolling through horizontal menus is now impossible.
- I did not have this issue in the legacy client.
- In battles, the new font has really bad readability due to black outline and general flat font type. In general, it seems cheap and unrefined. When I made my own games with ChatGPT, the font looked like that. 
- I am a power player; I care about seeing the results quickly and going into the next battle. So I find the increased animation time and density after battle extremely annoying.
- The player profile layout in the Results page is stupid. Most of the page space is empty, there is a little bit of information on the left, and a little at the bottom.
- The mini-map is now too small and less comfortable to use. The update you made before, when the player icons would move smoothly across the mini-map, now seems to be reverted, because the icons move in a discrete, jumpy way again.
- In Daily Missions, it's now harder to see which missions are complete, and which ones are not, because you reduced the highlighting contrast. The unlocked containers are not highlighted at all, only the progress bar moves.
- It's no longer clear which repair ammunition to use, because all indicators are in the same row.
- In general, the timer on top of the ammunition looks too large, again creating the feeling like it's an alpha version of the game. But isn't it less than month before the launch?
- Reminder: I play on a Macbook with a touchpad. In the new client, I cannot even select the quick commands from the Wheel, because it instantly shoots the gun.
- Before the battle, it seems that the Booster are ON by default, because of the bright colorful gems that indicate their rarity. 

Performance
- I have regular micro-freezes (like 2 per minute), even though the FPS tracker shows 60. Moreover, this happens, even though the game is set to Performance mode by default! So, the legacy client looked better (not as blurry), had no performance issues, and this one looks worse AND I have freezes on top?
- Even if the battles are in the Performance mode, I definitely did not expect to see a worse graphics quality IN THE HANGAR.
- There is now a longer latency after clicking on the player's card in the Results page to see their profile.
- The tank rotation animation when switching between after-battle tabs is now lagging (not as smooth as in the legacy client).

Tech tree / Tank upgrades
- The notifications in the top right corner every time I purchase a piece of equipment are completely useless. Yes, I know I just paid some silver, you don't have to inform me about the change of state in my Storage.
- I also don't understand whether I'm supposed to buy the tanks in order or not. If I had Celeno unlocked, did I do the right thing when I purchased it? Or should I have somehow poured XP into the tree to purchase an Atlas directly? This is not clear.
- It says that my new Celeno has a Legendary Classic camo applied, but the tank is just default blue. So where is the camouflage?

Guides / information clarity
- The Update Guide shows the different content sections, but the pages themselves appear empty and do not display any actual content.
- It is unclear how sets work and how collectible containers should be used once a set is complete. Some of my sets (that are based on classic tech trees) are marked Complete, even though there are non-purchased Collectible tanks in them. At the same time, some sets are impossible to complete without the collectible?
- The sets page / shop / gallery shows many colorful thumbnails, but it does not clearly explain why each set would be useful or valuable to me.

Example:
- I saw the Adaptive Warfare set.
- The thumbnail looked nice, and I recognized the T95E6, which I already have.
- After opening the set, I saw three additional tanks that I am not interested in.
- In this case, it is unclear what value I get from the set or what action I am expected to take.

Appearance / camouflage
- In the Hangar, under the Appearance section, the regular camo is shown even when a legendary camo is applied to most of my tanks.
- For tanks that already have the same camo applied across all maps, it would be helpful if that camo were auto-selected in the camo settings menu.
- I have around 60-80 Style items in my Inventory, but there are no available actions to perform with them. In the legacy client, it was possible to exchange them, but now I can only view them. What is the point of this?
- Some of the styles are not applied upon viewing them. For example, a Legendary Style for Taygete did not produce any visual change when in View mode.

Overall, several parts of the new client feel unclear or difficult to use, especially around item transfers, collectibles, sets, and appearance settings. More explanation and better UI feedback would make the experience much easier to understand.

***

I've spent alot of time playing counter-strike in local internet cafes 20 years ago. Right now its all about cs2 but its nice to have the old cs for nostalgia.

CounterStrike is a legendary game. One of the most influential multiplayer shooters ever made and a huge part of PC gaming history.

The gunplay was brilliant, the maps became soo iconic, and for years this was "the" game. Internet cafés, LAN parties, clan wars... an entire era of gaming lives inside Counter Strike.

The problem is that I'm reviewing it today, 27 years after, not in 1999.

Nowadays, finding a decent server feels harder than voting in north korea. Half the servers are packed with  hentai mods, the other half have hackers flying around the map like total m*rrons, and when you finally find a normal match, there's a decent chance somebody starts acting like you personally ruined their life, killed their family, killed their pet, just because you missed a shot or couldn't set the bomb.

The game itself is still good. That's the frustrating part. Under all the nonsense, you can still see why it became a phenomenon.

But the experience around it has aged terribly.

I respect it a lot. I had great memories with it. It's an all time classic, always will be.

I just can't recommend people jump into it in 2026 expecting to experience what made it special, unfortunately thoses memories are gone forever.

A look into tactical first-person multiplayer shooter history. This release was the first commercial one after many versions of a free to download mod (last version was 1.6) which became the most played and favoured shooter globally, strongly pushing the development of e-leagues in the early 2000s. At the time of its release in 2003, many players lamented the convoluted purchase menu, team commands, deliberate block of scripts for equipment purchases and error-producing copyright protection. The issues were solved and it continued to be the standard for fast-paced competitive tactical shooter tournaments for a few more years.

Even after 18 years, Portal still feels surprisingly fresh. The gameplay is simple to understand but incredibly satisfying, and using the portal gun to solve increasingly clever test chambers never gets old. Some of the puzzles can be frustrating at times, but figuring them out always feels rewarding.

I expected the achievements to be much harder to complete, but they ended up being really enjoyable with just the right amount of challenge. The bonus challenge levels are also a lot of fun and add some nice replay value.

What really makes the game stand out is GLaDOS. Her dark humour and constant commentary make an already great puzzle game even more memorable. Short, sweet, perfectly paced, and without a second of wasted time.

In 2007, it was a puzzle game.
Today, it is a time machine.

Replaying Portal feels like visiting an old home.
The sterile white walls still hum.They hold an eerie, familiar loneliness.

GLaDOS’s banter hits differently now.
She feels less like a villain.
She feels like an old friend.
A friend keeping you company in the dark.

The radio music still brings a smile.
The companion cube still breaks hearts.

They rarely make masterpieces like this anymore.
If you were there, you know.
If you weren't, grab a portal gun.
It is time to go home.

Portal is an elegantly designed puzzler with the now iconic titular portal mechanic. The game does a fantastic job of explaining the main mechanic and slowly ramping up the complexity of each puzzle. The introduction is so well done that Portal also works as a good introduction to FPS games in general.

The game is fairly short, however I found that to be a strength compared to the second Portal game. I found the length and pacing to be just right, while Portal 2 felt like a bit of a drag at times. The mix of aesthetic, comedy and environmental storytelling all resulted in a very unique vibe that has not been recreated quite like that ever since.

I picked up Portal way back in 2010 as part of the Orange Box goodness (or whatever bundle it was in at the time), and even though my stats show I only knocked out the core story achievements (Lab Rat, Fratricide, Partygoer, and Heartbreaker), it left a massive impression.
This game is pure genius in the smallest package possible. The moment you get the portal gun, everything clicks. The puzzles start simple and ramp up beautifully, teaching you mechanics without ever feeling like a tutorial. By the end, you’re solving rooms that would have seemed impossible an hour earlier. GLaDOS is one of the best video game characters ever—witty, sinister, and hilariously passive-aggressive. The cake is a lie, but the fun is very real.
My playthrough was straightforward: I beat the main campaign and called it there. No advanced maps, no challenge medals, no turret-killing shenanigans or 30,000-foot falls for me. But honestly? The base game is so strong that it didn’t matter. It’s short, sweet, and replayable. The physics, the humor, the “eureka!” moments—Portal nails every single one.
If you’re on the fence, just buy it. Even in 2026 this game holds up perfectly. Short enough for a weekend, memorable enough to stick with you for years. Now if you’ll excuse me, I need to go stare at a companion cube again.

I come back to Portal twice a year or so to replay it. It's never boring to revisit it, and I'll never forget my first playthrough. It's not a long game, but what it does in the short runtime is incredible.

I [b] genuinely [/b] tried to give this game a fair chance.

I grew up with The Sims 2 and The Sims 3, so I already knew about most of the criticism surrounding this game when it released back in 2014, but I still wanted to experience it for myself before judging it. After finally putting time into it years later, I understand exactly why so many longtime players feel disappointed.

The Sims 4 is probably the most aesthetically polished game in the franchise, and that is unfortunately where most of the praise ends for me.

There ARE aspects of the game that initially look appealing. The graphics are clean, the art style aged decently, and the Create-A-Sim system is accessible enough for casual players to immediately have fun with.

But the deeper you get into the game, the more restrictive everything starts to feel.

Even the build mode people constantly praise begins falling apart the second you want to create something beyond the game’s carefully limited systems. Suddenly you need cheats, debug menus, TOOL mod, Better BuildBuy, custom content, and twenty browser tabs open just to make the game feel flexible enough to properly express creativity.

And that becomes the core issue with The Sims 4 as a whole: so much of the experience depends on outside labor from the community instead of the actual game itself.

At some point you realize most of the best Sims 4 content is not even made by EA. The community fixes bugs EA ignores for years. Modders add more meaningful gameplay depth than official expansions. Custom content creators carry the game’s aesthetic variety almost entirely on their backs.

A life simulation game should not require this much external effort just to feel complete, immersive, or creatively satisfying.

Underneath the pretty graphics and smooth build mode is a life simulation game that feels incredibly shallow compared to its predecessors. The Sims 2 had insane attention to detail, personality, charm, and little interactions that made sims feel alive. The Sims 3 had an open world, story progression, unpredictability, and actual depth to gameplay. Sims had individuality. Households around town evolved on their own. Things felt chaotic in a fun way.

The Sims 4 somehow feels emptier than games that came out over a decade before it.

The emotion system that was marketed as revolutionary quickly becomes repetitive and surface-level. Traits barely matter because most sims react the same way to everything. Conversations repeat endlessly. Gameplay loops become stale extremely fast. The game feels less like a life simulator and more like a virtual dollhouse constantly interrupted by loading screens.

And then there’s the monetization.

Calling this game “free to play” is honestly laughable when the actual full experience costs an absurd amount of money. Hundreds upon hundreds of dollars worth of DLC, expansion packs, kits, stuff packs, and micro-content — and somehow the game STILL feels incomplete. Features that absolutely should have been base-game content are sold back to players piece by piece.

What makes it worse is that EA keeps pumping out new packs while bugs that have existed for literal years remain unfixed. Sims stop responding. Saves corrupt. Events break. Careers glitch. Pathfinding randomly implodes. Sometimes entire saves become unusable even without mods installed.

And yet somehow there is always another $40 expansion ready to release.

At some point the community itself became responsible for keeping this game playable. Players rely heavily on mods not only to improve gameplay, but sometimes to outright fix issues EA never addresses. Mod creators have genuinely added more meaningful depth to this game than many official packs have.

But even that becomes exhausting.

Once you start looking for mods or custom content, you suddenly have to dig through dozens of sketchy websites, ad-ridden pages, broken links, subscription-gated downloads, early-access paywalls, Discord servers, and random Patreon pages just to make the game feel complete or personalized. Want a different aesthetic? Better gameplay? More realistic interactions? Functional systems? Prepare to spend hours mod-hunting and risk downloading malware because the vanilla experience barely offers enough depth to stay engaging on its own.

And despite all this, the game constantly throws advertisements and promotional popups at you trying to sell even more content. Nothing kills immersion faster than opening your supposedly relaxing life simulation game and immediately getting blasted with ads for another overpriced DLC.

The saddest part is that The Sims franchise used to feel innovative. It used to feel creative. It used to feel like a genuine passion project centered around storytelling and simulation. The Sims 4 feels corporate. It feels engineered around monetization first and gameplay second.

First red flag for this game was having to download the EA app to even play it. Then, after booting it up, got four different pop-ups before even getting to press a single button on the main menu. 

Wanted to overall play this game because I enjoy building and decorating, and wanted to do so in a certain way involving mods. However, a ton of the mods to do so are locked behind paywalls or on websites that requires subscriptions, payments, and sign-ups to even view said mods. Which, ultimately kills the whole vibe and desire to play this game in the first place. Paying around $10+ per mod/custom creation just to give the game a early 2000's grudge theme -- or any theme other than base-game is awful, you shouldn't have to make an account or pay money or to even risk downloading malware just for something so simple. It's game changing, but not THAT game changing, people.

Worst part is; I've played this game mod-less in the past. It can be interesting for some time, but loses interest very quickly. Lots of content behind paywalls for in-game currency, not a lot to work with, tons of pop ups, and anything you can use is boring or repetitive. Which puts you in a position: buy stuff with real money (which there's a lot of stuff with too high of a price tag on each) or deal with what you got (which isn't anything impressive).

I don't like to immediately judge a game off the bat, but I knew everything about this game when it came out in 2014, and it failed horribly when I gave it a chance twelve years later. 4/10 for the possibility to play it, and a 0/10 for the paywalls and pop-ups.

since the early 2010s, i’ve been part of this scene. it started with playing the sims freeplay up to level 30 on my dad's samsung tab 2. i was obsessed with the time-consuming limited-time event rewards and packing my houses full of them. when i found out a full pc version existed, i spent ages looking for a way to play it for free, but had no luck and stuck to freeplay. eventually, as a kid with no pocket money, i downloaded a pirated copy just to taste what it was like. 

this game honestly helped shape who i am. it made me swallow my pride, bring my head down from the clouds, and finally come out of my shell. as someone who deals with severe anxiety, it pushed me to socialise and express myself in ways i couldn't in the real world. it also completely birthed my deep investment into the simulation genre and its history. 

growing up, i didn't actually play with physical dollhouses, but i'd see my friends with them or spot them in tv ads and absolutely drool over wanting to build one. for years as a little kid, i would play with building blocks shaped like houses and build house fortresses out of cushions. i constantly dreamt of playing pretend with a house. i even bunked a class in grade 5 just to roam the school grounds under a palm tree, improvising a home in my head. this game just ticked all my boxes. it became the ultimate digital sandbox of that childhood dream, letting me build, design, and control everything. 

the soundtrack was absolutely iconic and kept me hooked for hours. on top of that, modding is incredibly seamless. i even made a small mod myself back in 2021 and was amazed by how accessible the tools were.

the hours you see on my steam profile (around 11 hours at the time of writing) don't show the real picture. i've been playing this game day in, day out since 2017, letting my imagination run completely wild. 

the only reason this game went from holding a "special place" in my heart to holding a "special place in the past" is corporate greed. when a game requires nearly $1,000 usd in dlcs just to feel complete, it completely kills the enthusiasm. ea literally milked the player base dry before finally making the base game free-to-play. while i've ditched piracy now that i can support games properly, companies that corporate-greed their products this hard almost invite it. the constant churning out of low-effort dlcs has caused my enthusiasm to decline, but i still hold onto a bit of hope that they can turn things around and bring back the magic this franchise used to have.

Don’t Starve Together is technically built for co-op, but somehow I still lost hours playing it by myself.

There is something weirdly addictive about being dropped into the wilderness with nothing, then slowly running around collecting resources, crafting tools, building a base, and trying not to die because you made one terrible decision at sunset.

The art style is such a big part of the charm. It is creepy, cute, strange, and a little storybook-nightmare in the best way. Everything feels slightly hostile, even when it looks adorable. You are not just surviving nature. You are surviving nature if nature had a sketchy little grin and absolutely wanted you humbled.

I liked the loop of gathering, building, exploring, and slowly figuring things out. Even solo, it was easy to sink into. One minute I was just collecting sticks and grass, and the next minute I had a whole little setup going and was suddenly emotionally attached to not losing it.

It can be punishing, and the game absolutely does not care if you are having a peaceful time. Hunger, darkness, monsters, weather, sanity, bad planning, all of it can ruin your life very quickly.

But that is also what makes it fun.

Don’t Starve Together is charming, stressful, weird, and surprisingly easy to lose hours in, even without anyone else there to help cause problems.

Dying Light is easily one of the best zombie survival games ever made. The parkour system feels smooth and satisfying, making movement around the city incredibly fun. Combat is brutal, progression is rewarding, and every weapon feels impactful.

What truly sets the game apart is its day and night cycle. During the day, you can explore and scavenge with confidence, but once the sun goes down, the game becomes a completely different experience. The tension, atmosphere, and sense of danger at night are unmatched.

The story is decent, but the gameplay is what keeps you hooked for dozens of hours. Whether playing solo or with friends, Dying Light delivers an unforgettable zombie survival experience.

Even after all these years, it remains one of the strongest entries in the zombie genre.

The best zombie game out there. The parkour is elite, the combat is also amazing. The story is little bit predictable in some parts and the main villain is a little bit cartoonish, but nonetheless definitely worth buying it.

Dying Light with it's following dlc "The Following" (which I honestly liked a lot better than base game itself, where they even introduced a drivable/modifyable buggy) seems to really hold the title of the "ultimate zombie game" out there.

The few minor cons would be:
- Huge lag spikes in certain town-ish spots with lots of meaty zombies
- Terrain issues where, when u grapple something, you can sorta feel like it goes out of immersion due to impossible grabs and throws
- A lot of times, at least for me (if it weren't for my veteran friends assists), you could easily get lost on where to go or what to do, because objectives sometimes are faulty and not always intuitive

If I give base game a solid 8/10, then "The Following" dlc easily tops it off with better rating, where as of now, I give that - 9.5/10.

Co-op was definitely super fun to experience in this game however, and I hope other game studios follow it's similar premise/formula (perhaps even with slight tweaks of their own) to make more titles just like this one.

Dying Light really strikes a great balance with Action and Horror elements while also giving the player unprecedented movement abilities. What would likely be a boring open world game is massively expanded with the addition of the Day Night Cycle (and its effects on the world) and the parkour system.

Story wise I don't think there is much special here, honestly I found a few of the side quests to be the best content. One that is really memorable involves the search for insulin for a young child, which leads you on a chase across the city searching for it. When I did this it was getting dark in the game but I felt given the circumstances resting would not make sense. This created a race against the clock for me to find it and beat the quest before the volatiles came out. That is what Dying Light does best.

Ultimately, great game and goes on sale quite a lot so grab it when you can.

7 Days to Die has completely destroyed the survival fantasy I used to have about myself. I thought if society collapsed, I would be resourceful, tough, maybe even the respected leader of a small group of survivors. Turns out I would spend the apocalypse rummaging through kitchen cabinets for canned chili, eating boiled meat in a shack made of crooked wooden blocks, and getting mauled to death because I forgot to reload my pipe shotgun. Every seven days, the game gives me plenty of warning that an army of zombies is coming, and every seven days I somehow act shocked when they rip through my “impenetrable fortress,” which is usually just a refurbished gas station held together by cobblestone, panic, and one door I forgot to upgrade. I do not survive the blood moon. I simply inconvenience it for a few minutes before sprinting into the darkness wearing plant fiber pants and regret.

The humiliation does not stop when the zombies leave. I have broken into abandoned houses with the confidence of a trained scavenger, only to immediately step on broken glass, fall through the ceiling, wake up six sleepers, and die next to a toilet I was trying to search for a pistol. I collect garbage like it is treasure, I treat a working wrench like a religious artifact, and I have been emotionally outsmarted by zombies who cannot even use doors. The game says I have seven days to prepare, but what it really means is I have seven days to gather resources, build a base, make a plan, ignore that plan entirely, and die somewhere stupid while carrying everything valuable I own. Rated 3 half-eaten cans of sham out of 5 — I would not last one week in the apocalypse, but I will absolutely continue pretending I might.

Other than the name suggests (e.g. previously unreleased multiplayer maps), this game is a late and half-baked attempt of the developers to add a single player campaign to the visually slightly enhanced Counter-Strike 1.6 first-person tactical shooter setting. It's basically just a series of individual missions in different regions where the player tries to fight himself through hordes of hostiles. Compared to other shooters, NPC enemies were already lacking behind drastically at the time of the original release in 2004, the same year Half-Life 2 was available to the public with a much more progressive game engine overall. Interestingly enough, it's also the same year when CS: Source was published, although the latter had massive performance and mechanics issues at the time (e.g. hitbox inaccuracy, hit delays, ping spikes, etc.), hence being viewed as botched successor for most gamers, but even more so for professional e-league players, thus leading to big parts of the community sticking to the original release and engine until "Global Offensive" was published years later.

I am a dedicated eFootball player and I am writing because the current state of the game is simply unacceptable.

Here are the problems I face regularly:

— A player I own rated 105 gets chased down and dispossessed by an opponent rated 86. How does this make any sense?

— My passes become inaccurate at the worst possible moments, even with top-rated players who have excellent passing stats. This is not a skill issue — it is inconsistent and clearly not stat-based.

— Before big matches, my players are almost always given weak condition ratings while my opponents get maximum condition. I did not spend time building my squad to have the game undermine it before the match even starts.

— Opponent players regularly perform beyond their ratings — faster, more aggressive, and nearly impossible to get past — especially when I am winning.

This pattern is consistent, widely reported by the community, and points to an artificial system designed to manipulate match outcomes rather than let skill decide games.

Player ratings need to mean something. Conditions need to be fair. And matches need to be decided by skill, not by whatever hidden system you have running in the background.

Please fix this. Your players deserve better.

A scripted game treats you like a "voyeur", someone who obtains gratification from observing, rather than engaging in the action. You are unsuspectingly watching a movie, a "football" match which the game tricks you into believing that your inputs have substantial effect on. Konami has no interest in giving you any amount of control over gameplay, or giving you options to create diverse tactics. Rather they make you gamble for hardwired players and managers that reduce what can happen in the game, that solve the problems they have implanted on free players. This automation is further deepened by the momentum mechanics where the most likely winner is decided by certain conditions other than player inputs. The game will leave strikers completely unmarked in the penalty area when it is your time to score, make you miss obvious chances with top finishers when it is not. Momentum will make the AI score bicycle kick goals over tallest toughest defenders, unstoppable passing surges where the ball smoothly travels through your matching up defenders and if all else fails, gives a penalty in a clean possession gain. Dont spend a penny on this game. It isnt worth it.

Street Warriors Online, developed and published by Crazy Rocks Studios, is an online multiplayer brawler that aims to capture the intensity of street-level combat within a competitive, team-based environment. Instead of relying on fantasy abilities or ranged weapons, it builds its identity around close-quarters fighting, where punches, kicks, and environmental interactions take center stage. The concept is immediately appealing—large groups of players clashing in chaotic urban arenas—but the experience ultimately depends on how well its systems support that ambition.

The combat system is designed to feel direct and physical, emphasizing timing, positioning, and awareness over complex ability rotations. Players can perform a range of attacks, including basic strikes, sprint-based moves, and heavier actions, while also blocking or countering incoming blows. This creates the foundation for a system that rewards player skill rather than automation, encouraging careful engagement rather than reckless button-mashing. When the mechanics work as intended, fights can feel dynamic and reactive, with players weaving between offense and defense in quick succession. However, the consistency of this system can vary, and at times the combat lacks the fluidity needed to fully deliver on its promise.

Matches take place across several multiplayer modes, ranging from straightforward team battles to objective-based formats that require coordination and map control. Larger matches, particularly those involving multiple players on each side, highlight the game’s chaotic energy, as fights break out across different parts of the map simultaneously. The inclusion of a clan system adds another layer of structure, giving players the opportunity to form groups and compete collectively. In theory, these elements create a strong social framework, but their effectiveness is closely tied to the availability of active players.

Progression is handled through a combination of leveling, stat allocation, and equipment customization. As players advance, they can improve their character’s attributes, unlock new moves, and equip items that influence combat performance. This system provides a sense of growth and long-term investment, but it also introduces balance concerns. Players with more experience or better gear can gain noticeable advantages, which may make early matches feel uneven for newcomers. While customization options are extensive, allowing players to personalize their appearance, the impact of progression on gameplay can sometimes overshadow the skill-based foundation the game aims to establish.

One of the more interesting aspects of Street Warriors Online is its use of environmental interaction. Many arenas feature objects that can be picked up and used during combat, adding an element of unpredictability to encounters. These improvised weapons can turn the tide of a fight or create unexpected moments of advantage, reinforcing the game’s emphasis on adaptability. However, this feature, like others, is not always fully refined, and its impact can feel inconsistent depending on the situation.

Presentation is where the game shows its limitations most clearly. Visually, it lacks the polish and detail expected from more modern titles, with environments and character models that feel dated. Animations, particularly during combat, can appear stiff, which detracts from the sense of impact that is crucial for a brawler. Sound design does little to compensate, offering minimal enhancement to the overall atmosphere. While these issues do not make the game unplayable, they contribute to an experience that feels less immersive than it could be.

A more significant challenge lies in player activity. As a multiplayer-focused game, its core experience depends on having a consistent player base. In practice, this can be difficult to maintain, and finding matches may not always be reliable. Without active participation, many of the game’s systems—team modes, clan interactions, and competitive elements—lose their relevance, leaving the experience feeling incomplete. This reliance on player engagement highlights one of the risks of online-only design, particularly for smaller titles.

Ultimately, Street Warriors Online is a game built on a solid concept that struggles to fully realize its potential. Its focus on melee combat and team-based encounters offers a refreshing alternative to more conventional multiplayer games, and there are moments where its systems come together to create genuinely engaging fights. However, technical limitations, balance concerns, and inconsistent player activity prevent it from reaching a higher level of polish and sustainability. It remains a niche experience that may appeal to players looking for something different, but it falls short of delivering a consistently compelling online brawler.

I can't come to a term that I will leave negative review for this game. It pains me so much that this game got twisted into something that was not meant to be. Game started as hardcore, gothic horror, extraction shooter set in 1895. When I say this I mean a REAL hardcore game. It used to be very hard and very bone chilling to play it. Now is just another casual arcade fast shooter game and only thing that remains of what it used to be is name "Hunt Showdown" in title.

Old gold era:

There was no hand holding, boss arena was scariest place on map not because of boss, but because of players. Back then you did not know if someone was waiting for you to kill you and your teammates. You had to play slower thus why game match used to be 1 hour long. Nights used to be pitch black and frequent, it was a living nightmare to play night maps but that was a point! You always had this coldness in your body when you were walking on open or in swamps during the night. You would never know what lurks behind next tree or shack. Even regular grunts used to be scary enemy that could end you if you are not focused. From the start of match till the very end when you reach the exit, you were always under pressure and fear even if you killed lots of enemy hunters. There was just no way of knowing if you are 100% alone on the map. Not one time my heart rate dropped under 100 bpm and adrenaline rush was constant. THIS is why I bought this game so long ago, THAT is why back then all that I could do is scream "SHUT UP CRYTEK AND TAKE MY MONEY" after seeing this game and how it plays.


Bounty was a must go in every match every time. Why? Because game used to be hard so getting that sweet booty of bounty was important and if fact it was the only way to score big cash from the match (unless you got lucky and looted 1000$ from dead enemy but back then that was super rare). If you lose you felt it on your stash, if bounty escaped you still felt it on your stash, hunters were way, WAY more valuable, so backing away from the fight and leaving was not ashamed. In fact, many times, in old era of HS, players would say " Nah I want to keep this hunter and gear! We have no ammo, no equipment to fight and survive! We need to dip!". So you leave the map and actually LIVE TO FIGHT ANOTHER DAY! 


Every situation you got yourself into required a careful thinking and tactical planning. For example instead of screaming "That fu*king camper sniper! That is not FAIR! COWARDS! THEY ARE AFRAID TO COME AND SIT ON MY SHOTGUN! WHA ,WHA, WHA  PAPA CRYTEK NERF THEM!" you needed a calm mind, careful thinking and patience . "Fu*king snipers! Now how can I deal with this and win? How can I draw them out and win?" is how the game was played. Never in my 1800+ hours have I cried about campers or even cheaters to be honest. Most of fights were normal outcomes where I could see my errors in playing and only thing I could say in the end is "Dam it I lost! I know that I could deal with that much better but I lost because I was not thinking!". 


This is how Hunt should have stayed. A hardcore game in which you would be afraid, ultra focused, careful and pouring sweat from just being in game and not a CSGO type sweater where you mindlessly run around and gun down people.

Middle era:

Game started to be easier and faster. Boss whisper and red HUD mechanic, night maps became brighter and rarer, grunt started to be more of nuisance then actual threat but still they were dangerous. Traits were redesigned, some for good some for worse and new ones were added that made game more faster, more casual and less what game ground was. Match was cut to 45 minutes because of fast pace that we were getting in, 

Developers started to hand hold many players because they cried like babies and instead of actually learning how to deal with Hardcore game, they wanted papa Crytek to "fix" their lack of skills and ability to learn, by making more and more things easier for them. 

Still game was in somewhat perfect balance between old hardcore and what it is now so it was still ok in my book. Yes, even though I hated this changes that made game easier, I still loved the game with all my heart.

New era (post 1896):

This is where everything went down hill for this game. It lost its own soul and is no longer game that was supposed to be. Game became ultra fast, bounty became last thing that people would go to, everything is about shooting other players. 

First thing that you hear when match starts is this "Let's rush middle and see where other players are" , "Let's see if they are close so we can go fu*k them up" , " Who cares about bounty? I want to go and shoot people!". Hunt turned it self into Counter Strike. 

Economy became broken because before you had to go and get bounty for the money. Now with events, traits and all other mechanics you can get 3 times more money just buy killing players, looting cash registers and cash sacks. Losing hunter was not big deal anymore, because you can just get another one and go to event points to farm XP and speedrun them to level 50 with best traits you can get with no effort at all.

Events became life support for game but unfortunately that is not good. When there are no events, game becomes deserted with big portion of players leaving the game. And why wouldn't they? Playing outside of events is not profitable and thus game becomes little bit harder. But that is too much for them and so they leave and come back when there is another event.

Traits and cards became legal cheats in game that doesn't just hold hands of players but carries them. Cards that grant you wall hacks, lets you see through eyes of enemy player (this also gives you ability to see if you are last person on map), pinpoint direction of enemy players, fast heal, fast grunt killing (grunts became boring chore), fast everything. Almost everything that you do now leaves trace behind you for others to track you down easily. Exit points can now be blocked with these cards to make easier for pursuers (when pursuing actually happens), clown looking hunters that have nothing to do with Hunt world (Ghostface for example), UI has been reworked for worse in favor of consoles and it keeps getting worse.

Final words:

Game went from something hard, slow, actual skill required and unique masterpiece to another boring casual, ultra fast, sweater, shooter game like COD, CSGO and Battlefield, where you don't have to use more than 1 braincell to play it. It is like if DayZ started to turn into Fortnite. This is not Hunt Showdown that I love and with that I, with my 1800+hours, retire from this game, maybe forever. I can only hope, but it will never go back to what it used to be. Game got destroyed by "loud minority" while "silent majority" was fine and had fun in game.

For all players who came here post 1896, all I can say is hope you enjoy this now casual arcade of the game for you would never last even a minute in old era, you tourists. But that's ok. We all have our own taste when it comes to games. Some players like to play games in which all you do is sprint, jump and shoot other players around the maps, using every game mechanic that makes it super easy, without requiring any skills or brain whatsoever. So if you are that kind of player, you will love this game.

But for us who don't like game to hold our hands and just love the thrill of using our brain while playing tactically, building skills, outsmarting the enemy, learning how to deal with game hardness of its own nature and enjoying the adrenaline rush that this game used to provide, then this version of game is no longer for us. We can only move on and let new kids play with their new baby sucker we call "Hunt Showdown 1896".

Goodbye Hunt Showdown, thank you for what you were long time ago but now you are just shadow of your former self. You are no longer worthy to be on my list of best game ever. See you in HUNGER!

I joined this game at the tail end of Serpent's Moon, just as the final installment of the Moon trilogy, Devil's Moon, was about to launch. 

Back then, in 2022, the biggest issue was the Avtomat. The developers had decided to release a 1-slot variant of the Sparks rifle, the Sparks Pistol, which still held the ammo properties of the regular Sparks; 20 bullets spare with the ability to have 2 ammo types to change between. The developers for some reason, didn't think people would bring Avtomats with Dual Sparks Pistols to have 40 reserve ammo on a burst fire assault rifle in a game set in 1890s Louisana. 

This was a massive meta balancing issue, where the best players would bring an Avtomat, Dual Sparks (with the Quartermaster trait) and two Ammo boxes, to essentially have more ammo than there was AI on the map. You could imagine how unfun the fights were then.

So, the developers did a massive meta shift to undo the mistake THEY made; they changed it so Base Ammos (Compact, Medium, Long, Shell) would get a fixed rate back from ammo boxes (8/6/2/2), and made it so you would have diminishing returns ammo stacking dual pistols. Which, severely nerfed several guns and combos. 

Why am I talking about this? Because throughout the years the developers have constantly created massive problems for themselves and then blew their own foot off trying to fix it.
Gunrunner, Explosive Crossbow, 1896/2.0 update changing penetration, I can go on.
Instead I will focus on this specific update, The Road to Hell.

They say it's paved with "good intentions', but I don't buy it, I don't think theres a single well meaning change in this entire update and it's Cryteks swan song to throw out before the game plummets to Pre-Post Malone numbers for the 6th time this year.

In this update they have done a MASSIVE overhaul to the Loadout and weapon system, instead of weapons being grouped by size (Small, Medium, Large), they now take up "points" in your "capacity"

Small pistols are 1 point, stocked pistols and certain (I'll get to it later) revolvers are 2, Carbines are 3, Rifles and Shotguns are 4 and "We have given up balancing these weapons" are 5.

This on it's own wouldn't be bad, right? Well, the decision making in assigning these arbitrary point values to weapons makes no sense. Take for instance sawed-off shotguns, which prior to this update you needed a hefty trait called Quartermaster, that would allow you to bring a Full Sized Rifle and a Chopped off Shotgun (and vice versa). 
Well, now all sawed off shotguns are 2 points. Meaning you can bring what would be a full sized rifle before (Berthier, Officer Carbine, etc) and a medium slot shotgun (anything from a double barrel, to the Levering-capable 1887 handcannon, to the ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ pump-action Specter 1882).

So, the problem was Rifle + Shotgun = easy win; what do the developers do? Make it not require the trait it would've beforehand. 

That's just one nitpick, for some God-forsaken reason they've decided to lump the UPPERCUT into a 2-point weapon. The Uppercut has been a mainstay of the game since it's release and has been nerfed more times than Overwatch's Brig. The gun is a shallow shadow of its former self, and it's direct upgrade, the LeMat Haymaker (a long-ammo revolver with an underbarrel shotgun capable of using slugs) is ALSO a 2-point weapon. It is also CHEAPER than the Uppercut. There is functionally no reason to bring the Uppercut unless you are nostalgic or you have a skin for it. Ammo stacking with the big 4 (Mosin, Lebel, Krag, Berthier) is no longer possible, and the Uppercut and Haymaker both have FMJ ammo, so if you were to bring a consistent, long ammo backup hand cannon, you'd have to WILLINGLY nerf yourself to bring a gun that has maybe an above-average use rate.

On top of that, they have given up on balancing ammo entirely, instead of REVERTING the changes they made 3 years ago where Bleed goes to Heavy after half a second from a Medium ammo bullet across the map, they just took the ammo away from purchase. You'd have to scavenge to get Bleed on *ANY* weapon that supported it, including the Springfield 1866 and Drilling, which severely handicaps those weapons' capability as a support style archetype. If Bleed was kept on certain guns, and not given to half the armory in 1896 (Dolch, Centennial, Officer, Springfield, Drilling, Maynard, Conversion, Scottfield are all weapons that have Bleed ammo) then maybe it wouldn't've been so oppressive and overrepresented in the game. 

And to top it all off, the Mako 1895 Carbine (they erased the word "carbine" from it in the 1896 update) has been gutted this patch. A rifle which has failed to fill a niche since it's inception; a rifle meant to be "jack-of-all-trades, master of none" of the long ammo category, getting overshadowed by it's DIRECT COMPETITOR, the Centennial, at every step of the way. They buffed its fire rate and reload speed so it could ACTUALLY be usable against 87% of the rifle roster, and the joy of its existence lasted a whole 2 months before it got severly nerfed. Out of 2  years, the gun was good for 2 months and even that was deemed to much.
The patch notes state: "We have reduced the Mako's 540m/s velocity down to 440m/s as historically accurate due to black powder cartridges."
We have lost the plot and have started War Thunder balancing.
The gun needed High Velocity ammo to be able to compete against the mainstays like Lebel and Mosin and Krag and Berthier because they're FASTER weapons with BETTER AMMO ECONOMY and MORE DAMAGE, not an absolute shotgun kneecapping for "historical accuracy"

If a developer is reading this review (which I doubt) I urge you to load any Mako model you have on hand or in game and read the inscription STAMPED on the barrel assembly reading "SPECIAL SMOKELESS STEEL" as the model of the gun you are using in the game is the 1896 SBR model made to use the new smokeless 45-70 government USED IN THE EXACT SPRINGFIELD YOU ADDED THIS UPDATE.

If you don't want the Mako to be good then just say so, don't go round about and pretend you're conserving "historical accuracy" when Sparks, an actual black powder rifle, kept its 533m/s, the Haymaker, a black powder REVOLVER kept its 540m/s... I can go on.

This update is a massive disconnect of the developers from the playerbase, the same playerbase that actually plays the game instead of leaving it for months unopened and then come back to buy a skin or two. You can nerf Spitzer, you can nerf Long Ammo, you can do meta shifts, it's a part of the live-service model, but for Heaven's sake why do it in such a way where NOBODY wins. Not me, not the 6-stars that won't be affected by this, not the 3-4 stars who play this game casually with their friends and not the 5 stars whose favorite loadouts just got removed because the developers have given up balancing their game.

Please, overview some of the changes you've decided in this update. 
Put the Mako back to 540
Swap the Sparks Pistol and Uppercut's points values (Uppercut Precision SHOULD be a 2 point, at 3 it competes with the Dolch Precision, an infinitely superior weapon)
Either reduce Berthier's damage or fire rate
Make the repeating sawed-off shotguns 3 slot
Remove bleed from the problematic weapons entirely (Maynard, Centennial, Officer, Dolch)

The game has potential to thrive, to reach the audience you so desperately need to keep it going, but you can't get there forcing metas that aren't going to be healthy for the game. 
I hate to leave a negative review on a game I've spent so much time, money, had so much fun, met so many people that are dear to me on, but I feel like I have to.
I'll change this review if they end up doing so, but on their track record I fear that'll be quite some time.
