The game is very hard early on when you still don't know the basics, so prepare to die a lot until you learn how to play. Whoever treats it as an action game will suffer and get frustrated.

While it's a FromSoftware game, it feels different from the other titles, doesn't play the same, and only shares a few mechanics with the Souls games.

Graphics are still good in 2026, with very nice scenery, and while it's kind of an open world, it's small enough that it doesn't even have a map.

Bosses and mini bosses are well crafted, each one challenges you to apply what you learned, and some bosses act like a skill check, you shall not pass until you learn how to play this game. Most importantly, there is no difficulty setting, the only way forward is to get good, and once you do, you'll appreciate the time spent mastering your skills. In the end, you'll reach a level where the fights feel like a rhythm game. Easily one of the best combat systems in gaming and among the very best when it comes to parrying.

I was never this satisfied after beating a boss in a game. Highly recommended for anyone patient enough to learn instead of just button mashing, this game is as memorable as it gets.

Sekiro is indisputably the best game FromSoftware has ever created in terms of mechanical combat. It delivers a hyper addictive gameplay loop that demands absolute, unadulterated skill. While many label it the hardest entry in the "Soulsborne" pantheon, it is also easily the most deeply rewarding to master.


The genius of Sekiro lies in its Posture System, which is vastly deeper, more intelligent, and frankly way less goofy looking than standard action games. In typical Souls games, your primary defense is the panic roll you spend half the boss fight repeatedly tumbling across the floor like an acrobat just to escape a hit.

Sekiro eliminates that entirely. You don't run, and you don't roll. You stand your ground, look a ten-foot-tall boss dead in the eye, and meet their blade face-to-face. Because perfect deflections deal posture damage, defense becomes an offensive mechanic. Every parry is a statement. It transforms the combat into a beautiful, high-stakes dance of clashing steel where you feel like an absolute anime legend slashing, hacking, and controlling the rhythm of the blade.


The narrative and setting get a massive plus. Unlike other FromSoft titles, the lore isn’t buried in cryptic item descriptions you don't need to read the underwear of a zombie soldier just to understand the plot. Sekiro delivers a clear, compelling, and emotionally driven story from start to finish. Because the narrative actually moves forward, the New Game Plus (NG+) cycles stay fast and aggressive, never letting the momentum run dull.


9/10 strongly recommends

The problem with the game is that the combat feels off. It may have to do with how fast the combat is mixed with a reloading system that requires a QTE for reloads, much like Synthetik's, but the game is fast-paced. CyberCorp is a stylish cyberpunk action RPG that combines twin-stick shooting with a loot-focused progression system, delivering a fast and energetic experience set in a futuristic world dominated by powerful corporations. Developed and published by Megame LLC, the game places players in the role of an operator who remotely controls combat units sent into dangerous urban warzones. This premise provides a fitting backdrop for a game built around intense firefights, equipment customization, and the constant pursuit of stronger gear.

The most impressive aspect of CyberCorp is its combat. Battles are quick, chaotic, and highly dependent on movement and positioning. Enemies attack aggressively, encouraging players to stay mobile while making full use of their weapons, abilities, and equipment bonuses. The controls feel responsive, and the isometric perspective offers a clear view of the battlefield, allowing players to react quickly to incoming threats. Every encounter becomes an opportunity to experiment with different tactics, whether focusing on raw damage output, defensive survivability, or specialized status effects.

Progression is deeply tied to the game's loot system, which serves as the core motivation for long-term play. Missions reward players with new weapons, armor pieces, modifications, and upgrades that can dramatically alter performance. The variety of gear encourages experimentation, and discovering combinations that work together effectively is one of the most satisfying parts of the experience. Rather than simply increasing numbers, many items contribute to unique build possibilities that can significantly change how combat feels. This constant cycle of collecting, upgrading, and optimizing creates an addictive gameplay loop that fans of action RPGs and looter shooters will immediately recognize.

CyberCorp's cyberpunk setting also contributes greatly to its appeal. Neon lights, futuristic architecture, advanced technology, and corporate-controlled warfare create an atmosphere that feels authentic to the genre. The environments may not always be groundbreaking in design, but they consistently reinforce the game's dystopian themes. The visual presentation balances style and functionality well, ensuring that combat remains readable even when the screen fills with enemies, effects, and projectiles.

Cooperative multiplayer adds another layer of enjoyment. Playing alongside friends makes missions more exciting and introduces opportunities for team-based strategies. Different builds can complement one another, allowing groups to tackle challenges more efficiently while enjoying the satisfaction of shared progression. The cooperative component feels like a natural extension of the game's design rather than an afterthought, and it significantly increases replay value for those who enjoy multiplayer experiences. Fortunately, solo players are not neglected, as the game remains fully playable without requiring a team.

Despite its strengths, CyberCorp does have some limitations. Mission objectives can begin to feel repetitive after extended play sessions, particularly for players focused on grinding for the best equipment. The game's indie origins are also noticeable in certain areas, including content variety and overall scale. While the core gameplay remains engaging, some players may wish for a larger selection of environments, enemies, and activities to support the lengthy progression system. These shortcomings do not undermine the experience, but they do prevent the game from reaching the same level as the biggest names in the genre.

What ultimately makes CyberCorp successful is its understanding of what makes loot-driven action games enjoyable. It delivers satisfying combat, meaningful character progression, extensive build experimentation, and a visually appealing cyberpunk setting. The game does not attempt to reinvent the genre, but instead focuses on refining familiar mechanics into a cohesive and entertaining package. Players who enjoy top-down shooters, cooperative gameplay, and the thrill of hunting for powerful equipment will likely find plenty of reasons to keep returning.

Half-Life 2 is one of my favorite games of all time, and also a perfect example of a truly generational game. It didn’t just improve the FPS genre—it changed how games could tell stories and interact with the player.

What still makes it stand out is how immersive it feels without ever taking control away from you. The story happens around you while you’re playing, which keeps everything flowing naturally instead of breaking it up with constant cutscenes. It pulls you into the world and just lets you exist in it.

The gravity gun alone is a huge part of why it’s so memorable. It turns the environment into something you can actually use in creative ways, not just something you move through. That idea alone influenced so many games after it.

Even today, the pacing feels sharp and intentional. Every section introduces something new and then moves on before it gets old, which keeps the whole experience feeling fresh from start to finish. It’s the kind of game that didn’t just age well—it helped shape what came after it.

If you asked me what is the first game you should get on steam, I would tell you Half-Life 2. A timeless classic, that encapsulates you in it's world as soon as you step off the train. I've been playing HL2 since The Orange Box on the Xbox 360, and It only gets better with the updates to it on PC.

I didn’t really understand why this game was so famous before playing it. Not in a bad way, I just thought it would be a bit boring, since it’s a little old by now, and I kinda suck at FPS games. But now, after having finished it and both Episodes (and playing Half-Life 1), I’m a fan lol.

HL2 was revolutionary for 2004, just like HL1 was for 1998. However, HL1 is much harder to get into, in my opinion. HL2, on the other hand, feels much newer. It still holds up surprisingly well even in 2026. It is fun to play. You can mess around with almost everything, you basically choose how to fight, and the characters are unique enough to make you care about them. You remember them from Black Mesa, and if you do not, you get to know them well enough through their dialogue to care.

I also really like the graphics. I don’t really care whether things look “4K Ultra HD” or whatever. I love how realistic the faces are, how they actually move and show expressions while other characters are talking, how they can hold things for you to grab, and even hug each other. It's beautiful!!!

Really, it’s so impressive that they managed to surpass HL1. Like, how do you surpass Half-Life? And you could say Half-Life 2 somehow did.

Of course, like anything else, it has some minor flaws here and there. I think the pacing of the main story can feel a bit stretched at times, and I wish there had been more use of the Gravity Gun (aside from ravenholm, we don’t go to ravenholm) - in puzzles, for example. I also would have liked more character interactions. However, I think the Episodes fixed many of these issues. They feel like they contain everything Valve couldn’t fit into the original release. The pacing is better, and you finally feel less alone. You also get some delicious lore about the Half-Life universe and its characters.

It’s a bit sad that there’s no Episode Three or Half-Life 3. Yes, I know about HL: Alyx, and once again it’s wonderful how Valve keeps surpassing itself, but I personally can’t afford a VR headset, haha. Maybe one day.

Anyway, I recommend the game. Play it however and whenever you want!

Also. The player character, Gordon Freeman, is cool as hell. You could say he's attractive.

“A masterpiece that still defines what a story-driven shooter should be.”

Half-Life 2 isn’t just a great game—it’s one of the most influential titles ever made. Even years later, it still feels surprisingly modern in how it tells its story and builds its world.

You step into the shoes of Gordon Freeman in a dystopian future controlled by the Combine, and from the very beginning, the game immerses you without relying on traditional cutscenes. Everything happens in real time, making the experience feel natural and uninterrupted.

The physics system was groundbreaking at the time, and honestly, it still holds up. The Gravity Gun alone creates some of the most creative and satisfying gameplay moments you’ll find in any shooter. Puzzles, combat, and exploration all blend together seamlessly.

What really stands out is the atmosphere. From the eerie silence of abandoned towns to the tension of Ravenholm, the game constantly keeps you engaged without needing over-the-top action every second.

Of course, it shows its age in some areas—animations and certain mechanics can feel a bit dated—but the core experience remains incredibly strong.

Pros:

Innovative storytelling without cutscenes
Iconic physics-based gameplay
Memorable environments and atmosphere
Still feels unique compared to modern shooters

Cons:

Some mechanics and visuals are dated
Occasional pacing slowdowns

Verdict:
If you care about video game history or just want to play a truly great shooter, this is essential. There’s a reason people still talk about it today.

I've been putting off writing a review for Grim Dawn until I put at least 500 hours into the game, but I finally grew impatient and decided I'll write up my thoughts, even before the final expansion releases.

The short version is this: if you like ARPGs, Grim Dawn is a must play and I strongly recommend it. That being said, let me elaborate a bit.

- Expansions
This review is written under the premise you buy the game with all its expansions. The base game is good, great even, but it is lacking compared to the full experience expansions provide. They're absolutely worth their money. That being said, the base game is often on sale for very cheap prices, so you can try it out and purchase the expansions at any time later.

There is also a third expansion coming soon, which will extend both the campaign and the endgame even further.

- Condition of game maintenance
Grim Dawn is an older game, having been released in 2016. The game has, however, been receiving regular updates and has received multiple expansions, with the third and final expansion coming soon (hopefully in the next few months). Thanks to these consistent updates, the quality of life is on par with more modern releases, including a wonderful UI overhaul coming in the next version. QoL features are not locked behind the expansions.

There is also a number of mods available on the Steam Workshop, including a recreation of Diablo 2, or Grimarillion, which adds classes from other games like Diablo 3 or Titan Quest.

- Graphics and sound
Graphically GD is alright. It's a 10 year old game, so don't expect miracles, but with continued improvements to the engine the game doesn't look too dated at all. It's not the shiniest, prettiest ARPG out there, but it's still a solid looking game. Sound is alright. Attacks sound punchy (as they should), music is decent and there are some quite memorable tracks. 

- Masteries
GD is sort of a spiritual successor to Titan Quest, which pioneered the dual mastery system. Instead of picking a single class, GD lets you pick two masteries, each of which have their own skill tree, to create a unique class.

For example if you pick the Demolitionist mastery with Occultist, you end up with a Pyromancer.

This means that with 9 different masteries and many synergies between all of them there are probably hundreds of strong builds you can try. You have great freedom in how you play - want to be a dual gun wielding Reaper (Nightblade + Necromancer)? Go right ahead. It's all here - melee, retaliation (thorns), dual wielding, 2h weapons, guns, shields, spells, pets, skeletons, DoTs and way more.

Thanks to damage type conversions and powerful legendary items, an experienced player can create synergies even where there don't seem to be any at first sight, greatly adding to the game's depth. The theorycrafting potential is nearly endless.

- Devotions
While exploring the world you will run into devotion shrines, repairing them awards you devotion points which you can spend in the constellation tree. This tree mostly includes passive boosts, offensive or defensive, but certain devotions also offer additional skills, which trigger under certain circumstances.

This also encourages exploration of the game's hand-crafted world.

- Gear
Gear in Grim Dawn is great.

Items can grant you additional skills, some of which are very powerful. Damage conversions can help make (nearly) any synergy viable, and target-farmable Monster Infrequent drops can fill in holes in your build. There are some great sets, but it's not like D3 where you have to build around a set. I've made powerful builds that didn't utilize a set at all. There is a lot to farm, and at times a legendary drop immediately gives me a new build idea I rush to try out.

- Campaign
Grim Dawn is very focused on the campaign, and I think it works really well. The main quests are fine, but there are many smaller sidequests scattered around. Even after hundreds of hours I still find myself discovering new secret areas and finding small (and sometimes big!) sidequests I missed. The campaign is long and varied and offers lots of optional content, and the game's world is very enjoyable to explore.

You will probably have to play the campaign at least twice - once on normal difficulty and once on ultimate. This isn't such a huge deal, as on subsequent playthroughs you can rush through normal, get started on leveling, and then hop into ultimate for the proper (imo) experience. 

- Endgame
The endgame here isn't as fleshed out as others in the genre, but it's solid. There is the shattered realm, a place to test your build's capability as you fight your way through multiple floors of a randomly generated dungeon on a timer. Skeleton key dungeons offer roguelike-ish dungeons that reset upon your death, and Crucible is a wave-based game mode completely separate from the main game, though I don't have much experience with it.

There are also multiple superbosses, some of which are very very strong and require a minmaxed build to defeat. They are also usually hidden behind a sequence of secret sidequests, creating a buildup to the intense battle.

- Overall
It's a really, really good game. Perhaps my favorite game. GD can be as challenging or laid back as you want it. There's hundreds of hours worth of content to dig through and hundreds of builds to try. The game may be a little dated in certain aspects (combat feel, graphical fidelity) but if you can move past that it's one of the best gaming experiences out there.

The inventory and skills windows are also terrible. When swapping items in and out of inventory when checking how they affect your stats, they don't swap to the same place, so you have to go and find the item again to put it back on your character. There is no button to swap items into your side stash, so manually clicking and moving over individual crafting items is a chore. Why don't crafting items  have their own inventory anyway? 

The skills have useless explanations. Are spells of similar type grouped physically together? Ha, no, they're just scattered randomly on the page because who needs usability design. Half the text is dedicated to some backstory of the spell inventor, instead of explaining what the spell actually DOES. Does the spell affect your weapon or not? Do you have to cast it or is it passive? WHO KNOWS. Go read two wikis and still be confused so make a reddit post to find out. The game sure won't tell you. Or show you. None of the status indicators of ailments have any text on the screen. Is that green stuff poison or acid? Who knows.

I've played this game on and off for a long time. But it's only now that I feel compelled to actually critique it. Because I'm probably going to uninstall it, and send it to the hidden section of my library.

Let me be frank. If you are going to build American-style stroad cities that are primarily suburbs. It might be an ok experience. 

But if your goal is to build an actual competent city with density and mass transit....do yourself a favor and save your money. So much of this game is locked behind DLC. I don't have much to say there that has not already been said. 

But it seems as though this game was originally developed with the core assumption that the disaster that is American urbanism is the ideal. Given the developers were European I am not sure why they ever thought that was a good idea to build a city-planning game around. Maybe they never actually visited the United States in person and only ever consumed movies that were romantic about Suburbia or whatever. The point is that this game rapidly breaks and glitches if you actually set out to design a real city. 

 This game being written on Unity was already a red flag. The baked in assumptions are nails in the coffin.

Unless you use a bunch of mods to force changes to AI behavior, remove limits on network and road shapes (they break constantly otherwise), and more. This game is going to raise your blood pressure to dangerous levels. Perfectly viable bus or metro routes get a mysterious silent refusal that can only be overcome with Network Anarchy. Death waves have no clear cause. And even when traffic is not the bottleneck, buildings will get saturated with garbage and nothing will change. Which is especially infuriating when the only solutions to bulldoze or reset end up nuking the progress in a University or completely chowdering a transit system's lines. Just naming a few. This game's dysfunctions are many.

When playing I felt like I was rarely struggling with actual engineering, physics, and logistical issues. But instead I feel like I am negotiating a minefield laid by poor development.

It's a bummer that there really is no competition in the City Planner genre of simulation. And it shows with the disaster that is this game's successor. 

Overall, this game might be fun for a time before its inherent dysfunction catches up with a vengeance. Then it's just a quagmire of bugfixing, glitches, and performance problems.

 Not only was the original development team incompetent. But they chose to build this game with an engine that at best is a jack of all that does nothing particularly well. But more commonly flatlines in subtle, infuriating ways because it simply cannot scale reliably beyond a toy demo.

No, I won't be absolving the Colossal Order devs here. The fairy godmother did not write the code, nor design the baked in systems. The development team is equally as complicit in this game's dysfunction as Paradox Interactive. A bad publisher is merely a force multiplier. 

4/10: Would not recommend beyond an occasional curiosity play. If and when a proper city-planner game comes along the Skylines games need to be discarded by all.

A basically good sandbox in which you can do and try many things. 
For me, it just became boring at some point because it simply lacks a good story, 
but this game doesn't even want to have one. 
Therefore, it is simply a good sandbox, with cool fights and battles.

The idea behind the game is great, and it feels strong at first but it becomes clear that it is still unfinished, unevenly developed, and stretched too thin, still 6 years after early access release.

Each sandbox playthrough starts with a heavy amount of grinding before the game properly opens up. Early progression relies on a very limited set of repetitive quests and constant small-scale fights against bandits. These encounters offer minimal rewards, often barely covering losses. For example, defeating a bandit group might yield a couple hundred gold and some low-value loot, while losing a single troop or piece of equipment can cost just as much or more. For perspective a war horse for one single soldier usually costs around 1,000 gold.

Progression from a new character to a functional mercenary or vassal leader requires an excessive amount of grinding. Even activities like tournaments quickly become repetitive and are required in large numbers to reach the first clan tier, which would get you a semi decent war band.

Some systems like stealth, smithing, and the war sails naval dlc could have been added later after fixing the core issues. Instead of strengthening the foundation they add additional layers on top of systems that already feel underdeveloped. Politics, diplomacy, managing AI armies are very weak areas of the game. 

Mods are often suggested as a fix but in practice they don't fully resolve the underlying issues. There are too many interconnected systems needing adjustment, and setting up a stable modded experience can itself be time-consuming, inconsistent, frustrating, and at the end a bit of a waste of time.

The game ultimately contains a lot of shallow systems that lose depth quickly. The potential is definitely there, especially in combat, sieges, tournaments, but it needs bug fixing, balancing, diplomacy. If the foundation were more polished and focused, the game could be outstanding and expansions like stealth, smithing, or naval combat could be meaningful additions.

After dozens of hours playing Bannerlord, I can honestly say that this game frustrates me more than it entertains me.

The core idea is fantastic, and the battles are some of the best I’ve ever played. Unfortunately, the AI and diplomacy completely ruin the experience. Kingdoms will happily fight three or four wars at the same time, refuse to make peace even when they’re clearly losing, and make decisions that feel completely disconnected from reality. It often feels like the entire map exists only to gang up on the player.

Tracking down lords for quests is another nightmare. The encyclopedia tells you where they were “last seen,” but by the time you get there they’ve somehow crossed half the continent without being spotted. Instead of enjoying the game, you end up chasing NPCs around the map for days.

The economy and kingdom management systems are also frustrating. Cities constantly run out of food, the AI struggles to defend its own territory, and as a vassal you often have to watch your kingdom make one terrible decision after another while you can do very little to stop it.

Bannerlord has incredible potential and moments of genuine brilliance, but too often it feels like you’re fighting the game’s systems rather than your enemies. After all these years of development, I expected diplomacy and AI behavior to be much more polished.

I wanted to love this game. Instead, it left me feeling exhausted and annoyed far more often than excited.

Not recommended.

I have been replaying every "Civilization" game recently - and what do you know, this Fifth one seems to be the best in the series to me currently! 
Of course, the very first "Sid Meier's Civilization" from 1991  is still the most memorable, and "Civilization II" has its own merits as well, like FMV and CD Audio. But "Civilzation V" is the only one you want to replay again and again - the graphics here are not as modern as in newer games, but it is still has not aged yet (contrary to "Civilization IV" and especially "Civilization III"), and the gameplay is much more simple, elegant, and logical, than in "Civilization VI" (I have not tried VII - that one looks like the worst of all).
So if you want to pick just one game of the series, try this one!

Even years after its release, Civilization V remains the absolute gold standard of the 4X genre.

While newer entries get bogged down in tedious district micromanagement and cartoony visuals, Civ 5 nails the perfect balance of deep grand strategy and accessible fun. It is the only game in the franchise where "playing tall" optimized empire of just 3 to 4 massive cities—is a perfectly viable and incredibly satisfying way to conquer the world.

The move to a hexagonal grid and the "one unit per tile" rule turned combat into a brilliant, tactical game of chess where terrain actually matters. Throw in a stunning, mature  aesthetic, a legendary orchestral soundtrack, and a flawless late-game Ideology system, and you have a masterpiece that simply refuses to age.

Sid Meier's Civilization V is one of those games that's really easy to get into, but not everyone will enjoy it. If you like strategy games and taking your time to think about your decisions, there's a good chance you'll love it. If you're looking for fast-paced action, this probably isn't for you.

Even though it's an older game, it still holds up surprisingly well in 2026. The gameplay is simple enough for beginners to understand, but there's plenty of depth once you start learning how everything works. Every match feels different thanks to the variety of civilizations, maps, and strategies you can try.

The biggest danger is the classic "just one more turn" effect. You'll tell yourself you're done for the night, then suddenly it's 2 AM and you're still trying to finish a war or build that one last wonder.

I've only played around 10 hours so far, but I've had a lot of fun with it. It may not be for everyone, but if you enjoy strategy games and don't mind slower-paced gameplay, Civilization V is definitely worth trying.

**Overall: Great game, but not for everyone.**

After finally passing 1,000 hours playtime for this, I figured I should write a review.

Civilization games function best when they allow sandbox gameplay to tell a story on its own. From the very beginnings of each game, you feel invested in your civilization and can understand how beliefs / religion can form from the natural habitat around you and the benefits it offers. Starting near floodplains can lead to you worshipping the rivers and marshes and the food they bring, starting in harsher climates such as the desert or tundra can strongly alter the culture of a country. You can develop long lasting rivalries with opposing civilizations and bare grudges for hundreds of years over past slights.

Gameplay wise each of the different win conditions is varied enough to allow you to choose a different path through each playthrough. Domination can be a slow grind towards the end as you finish off the last opponents, religion seems like a slightly more limited version of domination but does have some variety. Science victory is the least reliant on interaction with the AI and so feels like a good fallback option if other objectives haven't gone your way early. Culture wins are the most varied simply because of all the different ways of getting culture / tourism and the different mechanics that feed into it.

The last Civilization game I played a lot of was 2, so I initially approached the combat in 6 with the same brute force unit spam tactics. Fortunately things have moved on since then, you have to use the terrain and the rock/paper/scissors mechanics of the unit types to your advantage. Only forts seem to be largely useless.

One of the best things about this game is how history is brought to life within it. It was a joy to pick up on all the familiar names, from Mary Shelley's / Jane Austen's / Leo Tolstoy's books to the paintings of Andrei Rublev (as I was aware through the Andrei Tarkovsky film of his life). Even figures I became aware of such as Kristina of Sweden (depicted by Liv Ulman in The Abdication) and Eleanor of Aquitane (depicted by Katharine Hepburn in The Lion In Winter). The more you learn about the different cultures, the more you feel tied to them in the game.

If I had to voice one frustration with the game, it's that the AI doesn't play the game well enough so the difficulty levels are compensated by giving them extreme bonuses in terms of unit numbers at the start of the game. This creates a slightly skewed gameplay curve where the biggest challenge is surviving and playing catchup at the start and yet by about halfway through your victory can feel somewhat inevitable because the AI is incapable of building districts correctly and focusing on win conditions.

Overall I can't recommend the game and all the expansions enough. I played for a while without the addons and felt satisfied, but once I got them it was like I had an almost entirely new game to learn and enjoy.

One of the best strategy games ever made. The variety of strategies and play styles this game offers is incredible. 

The concept of starting as a small tribe, and leading your civilization through the ages all the way to the information/future era was always so cool. So many leaders and cultures to pick from, all with their own soundtracks and charming models. What I really enjoy is that to win the game, you do not need to defeat everyone in combat; you can win through culture, science, religion, and diplomacy as well, which always felt super unique and allows for a lot of freedom in how you want to play the game.

If you enjoy turn-based strategy games or just strategy in general, I highly recommend you give CIV 6 a try; it's my favorite entry from the Civilization series. In my opinion, it upgrades on everything CIV 5 did well. The district system is amazing, and thanks to the randomly generated maps, you always have to think about how to build your cities and districts in the most optimal places.

The only downsides I have for this are that the late game can feel a bit sluggish and slow, with not that much happening apart from clicking next turn to finish up some micro management. The multiplayer can also feel a bit annoying to set up, with frequent desyncs and connection dropping, missing the beautiful narration and some cut-scenes, such as wonder completion. 

I recommend buying a pack that includes the DLC since those add tons of content, especially Rise and Fall and Gathering Storm.

Civilization VI Review

Civilization VI is one of the best strategy games ever made, and easily one of the strongest entries in the Civ series.

It is the sort of game where you sit down for “one quick turn” and then suddenly it is 3am, your tea is cold, your family has forgotten what you look like, and Gandhi is somehow threatening you with nuclear weapons again.

Civ VI gets so much right. The cities feel alive, the districts add real planning, the leaders have personality, and every game tells its own weird little story.

One minute you are peacefully building libraries and trade routes.

The next, you are sending tanks into France because they settled one tile too close to your bananas 2,000 years ago.

That is proper Civilization.

With Civ VII, there are some genuinely good ideas, but for me it has strayed too far from what made the series so addictive in the first place. Anyone looking at where to copy, where not to copy, and what made Civilization work so well should spend serious time looking at Civ VI.

This is the one that understood the magic.

Deep strategy.
Massive replay value.
Endless “just one more turn” moments.
Thousands of hours lost to building wonders, managing cities, and pretending your wars are defensive.

Civ VI is not perfect, but it is brilliant.

10/10.

There is no way one review could describe the scope of this game, but I will try anyways. Widely recognized as one of the greatest 4X games of all time, Civ 6 has it all. I don't play a lot of video games (under an hour each day), but I have still played 200 hours of this. There is simply no other game that could possibly rival it. It is engaging throughout, easy to learn, and has plenty of challenges to keep you interested beyond your 100th hour.

As with any game, there are a few cons. The AI aren't the most challenging, and after you reach a certain skill level you will find that they are easy to beat at any difficulty. The game, for being 10 years old, is still at full price. While that's certainly justified, it could be a deterrent, except for the fact that it frequently goes on sale for under $10!

I'm a big 40k fan and I'm a big CRPG fan, esp those with a lot of narrative choices and such. This game was exactly what I was hoping, and expecting, and as such, I had an utter blast with it.

The story was a lot of fun with plenty of enjoyable twists and turns, the companions (base game at least) were fantastic. Some were better than others, but they all had a lot of character to them and they were an utter lovely bunch and extremely well written. I ended up misty eyed by the end LOL. 

The game very much understands the 40k setting. It understands, unlike Mr James Workshop, it's NOT about how badass and awesome space marines are. It understands it ISN'T about "le epic humanity." It understands the universe is a deeply insane, morbid, yet almost hilariously dark place to live in. It balances the line of "badass", "stupid as hell", "grimdark as hell", and "actually kind of nice" incredibly well. I would honestly go so far to say out of most 40k media I've read/played/watched, this gets it the most. It is extremely multifaceted in all the best ways, and understands it perfectly. I absolutely adore it for that, and I think if you're a 40k fan who gets frustrated by all the, y'know, nazis, in the community, you'll appreciate it. 

Now all that being said, it isn't a perfect game. There's some areas that were a bit iffy.

For starters, combat, your mileage will vary. I personally thought it was fine, a bit dense and confusing, but I'm an idiot and others haven't had that problem like I have. I thought it was fun, but had to turn it down to easy more than a few times. But I'm perfectly willing to say: Skill Issue. However... there's companions you recruit later on and MANY people have complained how they're built like absolute trash. And unless you have a godsend mod (more on that later) you have to lose very important resources to fix it, which is really rough.

Secondly, there's a lot of information thrown at you such as with the colony system, the trading stuff and reputation, and it can be a bit overwhelming. It certainly was for me. By the end of the game, I fumbled it really hard and felt really let down, because I barely got anything out of it. On a second playthrough I'll obviously do better, but that bummed me out a bit. But maybe it's on me for not being rogue trader-y enough, which I'll gladly accept if so.

Next... some of the companion quests were a bit lackluster compared to others? Some were super long, really in-depth, had a lot of great moments. Others felt... really short and a bit of a let down in comparison. I still enjoyed all the companions, but that felt pretty odd. And I'm not even going to touch on the "heretic" path. If you're expecting to be cunning and sneaky and evil, well... Put those hopes away. Iconoclast for the win anyway :P That'll make more sense if/when you play the game.


Next... the romances were... confusing. I thought I was going along for two, but they both never went anywhere and I'm really not sure where I went wrong. I 100% STARTED one but it didn't go anywhere. I know I'm not the only one, and it doesn't help in trying to call someone creepy, I apparently started a relationship with them?? Its a bit confusing, but hey, that's what guides are for.

Next, there were a few moments in gameplay that quite frankly, sucked ass. Such as a terrible designed stealth section in a game that is NOT built for stealth. Why do devs love this? Then another section that was a maze with INCREDIBLY long loading screens that were deeply frustrating to me and many other people. Some quests were still bugged me for, which was a let down too, and I know it's happened to others too. Not enough to ruin the whole experience, but a bit of a frustrating time on otherwise very great game. 

But... y'know what? Even with all of that, even with all my gripes, I absolutely loved my experience with this. Like I said: It was EXACTLY what I hoped for and expected. A solid, fun, well-written 40k crpg. And that's exactly what you get. It fills the niche I wanted it to. If you like 40k and you like CRPGs, you will 100% enjoy your time with this. I look forward to getting the DLCs at some point and doing a second playthrough. I also VERY much look forward to Dark Heresy, too.

Anyway, download toybox. Trust me. Just do it. You won't regret it. Incredibly useful mod to have.

Rogue Trader is one of the few CRPGs that actually understands what makes the setting compelling. The writing, factions, politics, fanaticism, and constant tension between duty and power all feel authentically 40K rather than a watered-down interpretation.

What impressed me most is that your decisions genuinely matter. Too many RPGs present "choices" that amount to different dialogue flavors before funneling you back onto the same path. Rogue Trader regularly allows your decisions to alter relationships, quests, outcomes, and even the fate of entire worlds and factions. Whether you lean toward the Imperium's dogmatic orthodoxy, pragmatic self-interest, or more radical approaches, the game often follows through on the consequences. It makes roleplaying your Lord Captain feel meaningful rather than cosmetic.

The companions are memorable, and their reactions to your choices feel grounded in their beliefs and backgrounds. The game also does a great job showing how difficult leadership would be in the 41st millennium, where every decision carries risks and there are rarely clean solutions.

Lore-wise, the dev did their homework. The game captures the scale, brutality, bureaucracy, and religious zealotry of the setting better than almost any 40K game I've played. It feels like it was made by people who genuinely appreciate the source material.

My only real criticism is that there are occasional modern "woke" elements and bits of dialogue that feel somewhat out of place with the setting. They're not dominant enough to ruin the experience, but they do stand out against an otherwise faithful depiction of Warhammer 40,000.

Overall, if you want a true RPG where choices have real weight and a Warhammer 40K experience that respects the lore, Rogue Trader is easy to recommend - especially now with all the expansions out.

It's just a perfect CRPG in space. This is what KotOR sequels should have been. Owlcat, please get a Star Wars license and make a game set in the Expanded Universe, away from disney sequel and high republic nonsense!

Owlcat knock it out of the park again, but also whack themselves in the face with the bat.  It's kind of admirable to be so consistent in both ambition and mistakes.

As with Pathfinder, the setting is nailed.  All the weird and bizarre quirks of 40k are explored in fine detail, with a great balance between grim dark grandness, over the top personalities, but also a human grounding.   Most media is hyper focused on Space Marines yelling battle cries at each other, but Rogue Trader manages to look past that and create probably the best setting in a 40k video game.

The combat and character building will be hit or miss depending on whether you enjoy the mechanical heft of the systems involved.  The level up system is still easily overwhelming, but there is satisfaction to be gained from figuring out how all the various and near endless mechanics interact with each other when the action does happen.  Having Argenta fire a heavy bolter into a crowd of enemies and see the crits and bodyparts fly never gets old.

There's a lot to this game.  So if you do play expect to have to look things up online, and honestly first time round if you're paralyzed by the levelling choices - just look up a guide and follow along.  While the game's difficulty is much less severe than Pathfinder, it's still worth trying to figure out what works.

If you can get your teeth sunk into it, it's an incredible game.  If you can't, you'll wonder what all the fuss was about.

Before beginning my very first playthrough of Rogue Trader I had only ever played one other Owlcat game (Pathfinder Kingmaker) And my knowledge overall on the 40K universe and lore was not the best so It was a intimidating thought going into this game at first. But Rogue Trader does a fantastic job at explaining the universe and themes without feeling overwhelming or slow.

I really found myself immersed into it all in only a couple of hours and playing it alongside my Girlfriend at the time made the experience even more enjoyable and fun. The cooperative elements of the campaign work well but there was some moments when the game only allows the host to take part in critical and key parts of the main story which did feel a bit underwhelming and takes away from the cooperative nature of it all. Combat encounters always felt intense and engaging and using voice comms to guide our party of six characters through disasters and threats always felt rewarding when you get outnumbered and have to use strategy and teamwork to live to fight another day.

So far I haven't even touched the DLC content but from talking to other friends about them I have heard positive things so I am very excited to do a second playthrough and get introduced to new questlines and companions and explore more of what Rogue Trader has to offer. Overall the amount of time I spent on this game should give a big indication on how much enjoyment and depth you can get out of Rogue Trader so I can highly recommend it if you are looking for a extremely well written adventure both solo or with friends!

Simply the best Warhammer game. If you are new to 40K Universe - go ahead and play this gem. 
This game serves as a great introduction to the Warhammer, because there are explanations for every term, every faction and why the world is so grim and dark.

I pre-purchased playing through the early development all the way through to full release. Stayed while the game continued to be refined, improved and starting over as DLCs landed. No regrets. It is hands down one of the most compelling CRPGs that I have played without being someone who is heavily into WH40K.

Owlcat really showed this to be a labour of love and continue with DLCs and patches while their next WH40K game is about to land. 

The artwork is fantastic, the companions feel like they matter, lore is as deep and vast as the universe it is set in, battle is satisfying. Feels well executed with meaningful dialogue and a whole range of options before you.

In the grim darkness of the far future, you find yourself as an heir to an ancient Rogue Trader bloodline. Although events beyond your control have placed you on the von Valancius throne and granted you a vast inheritance, you still have much to do to prove your worth in the wild west of the galaxy. 

There are rough edges in Rogue Trader. The game starts slow, and in my opinion, it doesn't do a perfect job of explaining its core mechanics. However, you gradually master them through trial and error. Don't be afraid to make mistakes with your character abilities as you can change them later on. On the other hand, companion interactions can drastically alter how that character’s story ends, even if you don't realize it at the time of your decision. Also, if you haven’t played any CRPGs other than Baldur's Gate 3 before, you might find the voice acting department a bit lacking. That being said, these are the only negative things I can say about the game. It is incredibly welcoming for newcomers to the Warhammer 40,000 universe. The characters, quests, and locations are engaging enough. I didn't encounter any game-breaking bugs, but I did get stuck a few times and had to look up guides online. The two DLCs I've played so far, Void Shadows and Lex Imperialis, were well worth the purchase, and I'm looking forward to the upcoming DLCs.

A massive CRPG in the warhammer 40k universe. It is a great blend of grimdark and humor, showing the horrors of the universe while playing its absurdities with a straight face. It's amazing and I loved every moment.

As a massive CRPG fan, I just cannot bring myself to like this game. Despite giving it a solid 16 hours of playtime, I find it incredibly boring and a massive chore to stay interested in.

The Lore is an Absolute Wall.
The primary issue is how aggressively lore-heavy it is. If you are already deeply immersed in the Warhammer 40k universe, this might be right up your alley. But for an outsider, the conversations don't feel natural; instead, it feels like you're just reading through a Warhammer wiki page for 10 minutes at a time.

Combat is a Tedious Slog
It doesn't help that the combat is an absolute slog. It follows the usual Owlcat formula of throwing endless "trash mobs" at the player, but here, those filler enemies feel entirely vapid. Because there are so few genuine choice dynamics in the battles, the encounters almost feel like they play themselves.

Disappointing Exploration
Worst of all is the exploration, which feels awful. I think back to Pathfinder: Kingmaker, where Owlcat gave us some truly great dungeons to dive into. Don't get me wrong, we aren't talking Hexen 2 or Wizardry levels of dungeon design, but those games still offered a sense of scale and a lot to enjoy. In Rogue Trader, everything feels incredibly small, confined, and boxy.

Ultimately, this one just isn't for me at all. If you aren't already a die-hard 40k fan willing to look past tedious combat and rigid level design, you might want to skip it.

BFV's campaign is pretty good, but also very disappointing. 

The stories are pretty good, even solid but inferior to BF1 in almost every sense. How they feel they could get away from a WWII game without a chapter in the Eastern Front showing the USSR/Third Reich is UNFATHOMABLE for me. That battle was the one that defined the war, and it's not even mentioned here. I can't even call this American propaganda, because the Americans are also not present… It just feels like a missed opportunity to portray WWII like this. I know the whole point of BF's War Stories is to shine a light on unknown stories from WWII, for example, the Tirailleur story that was VERY GOOD and a highlight of this campaign, but not a single story in the Eastern Front is insane. I am also very surprised to see The Last Tiger chapter, it was bold, depressing, bleak, and brutal. Probably the best from BFV and the most relevant.

I also feel that technically the game took a big downgrade, the game feels sluggish, the explosions don't register well, the destruction is less impressive, the game stutters even in cutscenes, and strangely, I feel the graphics and art style are worse than previous games.

Soundtrack, scenarios and weapon variety are still excellent. I also loved the new mechanics (Skying anyone?).

BFV is ok, it could be better, and seeing so many downgrades, both technically and narratively, clearly shows that this was rushed out of the door incomplete. Classic EA move.

Bought R.E.P.O. thinking it was a co-op horror game.
What I got instead was:
a piano that weighs approximately the same as the moon
a friend who dropped a priceless ceramic vase down the stairs immediately
a monster that only activates when someone says "is it safe"
and a cart we lost in the first two minutes and never spoke of again.
The objective is simple. Carry valuable items carefully to extraction. Do not break them. We have never once done this.
The physics system is a masterpiece of human suffering:
"Okay, careful, careful—"
The vase flies sideways into a wall.
It was worth $4,000.
We needed $4,000.
Dave is no longer allowed to touch things.
The proximity voice chat adds incredible immersion. You can hear your friends whispering in real time as you creep through a dark mansion. You can also hear the exact moment someone starts screaming because a monster that looks like a rejected Pixar villain walked through a door at 2% speed and somehow this is the most terrifying experience of our collective lives.
The monster AI is elegantly simple. It hears noise. We make noise. This is our entire relationship with death.
The upgrade shop offers meaningful progression. We bought better lungs. We needed better lungs because we keep screaming. We are still screaming. We just have more health while we do it.
10/10.
We never hit quota.
We're going back in.
Someone find the cart.

I do like REPO, but there are so many frustrating things in this game that have ruined me enjoying this game as much as I'd want to enjoy it. There IS a good game here, but I don't see many people pointing out the MANY negative or frustrating aspects of this game, so I wanted to point some of these out and I hope the developers see this so the game can be the best that it can be.

Firstly let me talk about maybe my biggest issue, the Huntsman AI. I don't know why many people think he is ok, but I've heard it's worse on the host's end and I host often so maybe that's why I have such an Issue with it and others don't. I have had MANY times where he shoots me either across an entire room where I could not hope to react and get one tapped, or he shoots me when ANOTHER sound source made him trigger and he shoots me anyway. I've had times where I tap a object on the ground to bait his shot, and instead of shooting the object far away from me he shoots me instead. This makes absolutely no sense and I personally think he should not work this way, he should always shoot the sound source not the nearest player like how he does at times. I suggest taking inspiration from the Eyeless Dogs from Lethal Company, just make him not instantly shoot at the first sound he hears and make him have an audio cue where players can be aware they are making too much noise like a sound of him grumbling or him going "huh", and if they continue he then shoots them. I also think his idle sounds should play more often and have more range so you can hear him before you get in his range so less unfair deaths where you didn't even know he was there at the edge of your vision occur. I think in general the monsters in this game are too quiet/don't have idle sounds that are good sound cues. Besides some exceptions like the Loom/Robe, them being quiet is part of their design.

This is a little less important but, WHY DO SOME COSMETICS GIVE YOU ONE OUT OF A PAIR?! I genuinely don't understand why they did this, because I've had so many times where I get a cosmetic that looks like I'd use it... IF ONLY I GOT THE PAIR AND NOT ONE FOR A SINGLE SHOE/LEG. Some cosmetics do look cool asymmetrically, but (in my opinion) most don't and this just makes cosmetics grindy for the ones you want because the chances of getting the other one in the pair are low. I just don't see any positives to it being this way because if you wanted to just use one, you could just equip one out of the two if you got the cosmetic in a pair anyway. But I do like everything else about the cosmetic system they did a good job on how they work in every other way. I just really disagree with it working like this with pairs of cosmetics.

Back to another big issue (again in my opinion). The physics system is WAY to janky in some areas, mostly with big items. Big items take damage for absolutely no reason very often. Sometimes you'll just touch it to check the value... BOOM -$2,377 for no reason. Sometimes you'll slide it across the ground gently... BOOM -$5,078 for no reason. Sometimes you rotate it slightly... BOOM -$3,980 for no reason. The big items really need to be less janky because a lot of the time they quickly become not worth it because it randomly took damage from sliding on a carpeted floor. When moving big items is a main game mechanic, it should be polished.
 Also I'm not sure whether to call this a physics issue or not but, you also should not be able to hit yourself with a melee weapon so easily. Many times I've needed a melee weapon quickly and hit myself while pulling it out, getting me killed. 

I also have an issue with the early levels having a tier 3 enemy like a Trudge or Head, which would not be an issue if the levels had more paths. What often happens (especially when I played solo with the Trudge) is the monster blocks and camps where you need to go and they waste your time, and because its early game (especially bad on the first level) you can't deal with these guys and just have to wait 4-8min just for them to leave. I think this is worse with the Trudge because he is so slow and takes forever to leave and it's difficult to run past him without getting one shot. In the later levels this is not as bad because you and the monsters have more paths to go, but in early game it really can be frustrating and slow down the game more than it should. I would just make the early levels have more pathways to counter this issue. Also because I'm talking about the Trudge, I think he should not be able to kill you through walls and suck you in through walls either. But this could be a bug and not an intentional gameplay mechanic. I've also been killed by the Cleanup Crew (UH OH guy) when I was enclosed in a safe through the wall from the head explosion.

Another thing is the weapon spread from some ranged weapons (mostly the handgun and similar weapons). Some weapons have insane bullet spread (even after they buffed it). They already have limited ammo, why do I have to be in hugging distance to make sure I don't miss? It makes the weapons feel a tad underpowered.
 
The stamina system early game is a pain to deal with as well, mostly the recovery rate. The amount of stamina you get early game is barely enough to survive and if you aren't in a good spot, don't have full stamina or don't hide perfectly; you just die to some enemies without a chance(head, duck, Robe etc.). You can upgrade both of these aspects, but in a game where you're playing with multiple people you don't get enough till very late because everyone needs it. I think they should AT LEAST make stamina recovery faster with no upgrades.

But that's all. I do enjoy this game, but I don't see people talking about these issues, (even though some can be seen as nitpicks) some can kill the fun of the game (Huntsman). It's ok to criticize a game, it can still be good. It helps the game be the best it can be. For anyone who read this, thank you for reading my rant/critique. I hope the developers take this feedback because there is a really good game here held back by some frustration, and unfair AI, mechanics or bugs.
Thank you for reading!

The last few patches have all increased the difficulty and according to the community it's now either a tryhard or cheating kind of game, no longer a fun co-op experience.  For the first part, people in discussions obviously say "git gud lol" and the latter, they tell you which mods you can download to make the game playable, because it's still perfectly balanced, just... you need to mod it so you're not playing with the intended balance.  Examples were "difficulty smoothing" and "shared upgrades"

You need to actually fully beat a level beyond the first to *potentially* unlock any cosmetics, they're also gated behind being a large bulky object to drag to an extraction - they can also take damage - and it's no easy feat to get past even the first stage anymore in a state where you'll be able to get past the second.  I've not done one with fewer than 2 monsters that appear fairly early, and generally there are 3 or more, which is just not good.  Several times, they've been in the very first room we walk into.

There are already built in mechanics that make the game considerably harder the longer you play so I don't see the need to make the early game as difficult as it is, with the increased amount of enemies and their level of aggression.  Multiple times enemies have behaved in ways that they didn't in previous versions.

Oh, and the shop items have been nerfed.

I have mixed feelings on the shop items, weapons especially. It really feels like the game is designed for you to fight instead of stealth at a point, now more than ever.  But that turns the game into a completely different experience for better or worse.  If you can make it to that point and manage to actually see them for sale, that is.

I will be checking back and may update my review because I have fond memories but I think the game is going in the wrong direction and has been for the last few patches.  Add a challenge mode, hard difficulty, whatever for people who want to play the game that way but leave the base experience as an accessible and casual party game where you can mess around with friends and still do something.  If you make a mistake and lose a bunch of stuff due to your own messups and gradually feel the attrition as you go through levels, that's perfectly fine.  But bad RNG where you get little loot in a level full of enemies that destroy it.. is not fun.  And this seems to be happening more and more this version.

I love this game. It’s goofy and entertaining. It’s strategic but doesn’t take itself too seriously. My whole family enjoys both playing and watching people play it (especially when Grian, Geminitay, ImpulseSV, and Skizz play it). It has elements which are similar to Lethal Company, but I appreciate the differences between the games more than anything. I can’t play Lethal because it’s just a bit too creepy/spooky for me with clunky characters. Another big difference is the way you can “go out”. It’s very amusing to knock the other players off a platform to become “king of the losers”. I’ve seen people get out their frustration with this fun mechanic. 

And here’s where my suggestion comes into play: If there is a loser island face off, surely there should be a form of “winner face-off”. After who-knows-how-many levels (perhaps there could be a set goal in the settings?), the players could emerge onto that platform, not as losers, but as winners. They can sip tea (like eating a consumable), then take out their angst for certain monsters by placing toy-sized versions of them on an extraction table that smashes them. All of the while, credits can roll. And here’s another fun aspect to the idea. If they want, they can allow their teamwork to shine and all become REPO winners, or they can take each other out as with the other ending and become “king of the winners”. It could be an even better tag than losing because they reached a goal. So satisfying if you ask me. 

Reasons I think many people would enjoy an ending similar to this outline:
I love setting a goal and reaching it. I love smashing and venting frustration on the actual monsters (hence the smashing of those silly things in an after-party). I like the idea of a celebration tea, whether it be a teacup, cake, oil can, whatever the lore would suggest. And then the opportunity to all be winners because it’s truly teamwork that gets many people through this game, is fun to me; of course, if they would rather be king of the hill, that’s their prerogative. If they can manage to get through the ongoing credits without taking out their friends, then they really deserve that cute crown. 

Yep. Those are my two cents. Love the game, but I’d prefer reaching a point where I can log off for the night feeling like I accomplished something, rather than have a bit of irk that we “lost” again. 
Edit: I do love some of the new features to become King of the losers.

P.S. Once at the winner island there could even be a red button called Hard Mode which uses a trap door to send players back to the truck with their [previously purchased] stuff, because people who reach the winner island are likely to be more confident and eager for another challenge.

DayZ is, at its core, a genuinely brilliant game. The survival atmosphere, the constant tension, those moments when you're starving and finally find a can of sardines, and the way every encounter with a stranger turns into real drama - there’s simply nothing else quite like it. In concept and in those rare moments when everything works, it's one of the best games in the genre

[b]BUT[/b]

Right now, DayZ has one massive, ugly, impossible-to-ignore problem — cheaters. And this isn’t just "you run into one sometimes." It’s an epidemic that turns the entire experience into paranoia and frustration

I’m writing this in mid-2026, and the situation is worse than ever. Cheaters don’t hide - they act like they own the servers. You get killed through walls while crouching in a locked shed in the middle of nowhere. Entire squads get wiped by a single player flying across the map at impossible speeds, headshotting everyone through trees and buildings

The worst part? It completely destroys the core loop that makes DayZ special. You can spend hours - sometimes days - carefully looting, surviving, gearing up, building a base. All of it gone in a single, meaningless second because some cheater decided to ruin your day from three kilometers away with zero counterplay. It’s not "part of the survival experience." It’s a broken game being exploited to death, and the developers simply aren’t doing enough to stop it

Official servers are basically unplayable in their current state. Community servers help a little with active admins, but even that is a band-aid on a bullet wound. The cheating problem is so deeply rooted that it poisons every aspect of the game

I absolutely love what DayZ could be, and what it occasionally is during those rare clean sessions. But I cannot in good conscience recommend anyone buy this game until the cheating situation is genuinely fixed - and honestly, after all these years, I’m not sure it ever will be. Save your money, save your sanity, and stay away until there’s real, meaningful change

After playing for well over 2000 hours between Xbox and PC I have come to the realization that Bohemia interactive have no idea how to run their own game. Official PC servers have been plagued by cheaters for several years (and the recent cross play fiasco has brought this problem to Xbox as well), the same game breaking bugs and glitches persist for what feels like an eternity, even after the developers promise they're fixed, hotfixes get outright cancelled for the promise that the issues will be fixed in the next update instead, and content updates get delayed for 6+ months just to release and cause more problems than they resolve.

The only saving grace is community servers, but there's still some general problems there as well. Some are fantastic and some are very much not. Finding a community sever without power hungry admins or some pay-to-win shop integration can be difficult. It can also hard to find severs that aren't so heavily modded that they resemble a different game altogether, so if you enjoy the vanilla experience you may be out of luck.

All in all, I can't in good faith recommend anyone support this studio.

I want to love this game more than any other game I have played. 

The atmosphere is amazing and the survival gameplay feels great. 

Player "interaction", however, is entirely kill-on-sight. 

This means, most of the time, DayZ runs end thusly: You step into the open, hear a gunshot, get a black screen reading "You are dead." and every minute you've spent stabilizing yourself since the last time you were murdered without warning, is completely wasted.  

Talking (despite the loading tooltip which says "your voice is your strongest weapon") achieves nothing. At best, someone will pretend to be friendly just long enough to shoot you in the back. 

The tension of playing on a server with strangers, where running into someone else could turn into a friendship, or a fight, is necessary to make this game feel like it is worth playing at all. Unfortunately, after a while that tension disappears, because you realize there was never a real chance that seeing another player would result in anything other than a fight, or, more likely, an instant death and the loss of everything you had. And when that tension is gone, so is the reason to play this game.

After a journey of over 1,000 hours across official and vanilla servers, I think I can sum up DayZ in just three sentences. DayZ is the slow paced game that can give you the greatest joy and the deepest disappointment in a single life run. You learn to value, respect, and appreciate every little thing it gives you, while the frustration becomes very real after countless hours of walking, searching, trying to survive, and end up dying with no goals. Still, every minute I spent in this unforgiving world was worth it, and I strongly recommend experiencing it with your best gaming buddies.

Over and out.
Leader.

DayZ is one of the few games that can turn a simple trip to find food into a story you'll remember for weeks. No other survival game creates the same tension when you hear footsteps in a nearby building or spot another player on the horizon.

The game doesn't hold your hand. You'll die from hunger, sickness, zombies, bad decisions, and other players. A lot. But every death teaches you something, and every successful run feels earned.

The best moments aren't scripted—they happen naturally. Meeting a stranger who becomes a teammate, surviving a firefight with barely any ammo left, or spending hours gearing up only to lose everything in seconds. The risk makes every victory meaningful.

It's not perfect. Bugs still happen, and the learning curve can be brutal for new players. But if you're looking for a survival game with unmatched atmosphere, player interactions, and unforgettable stories, DayZ is absolutely worth it.

"No mission markers. No respawns. Just survival."

👍 Recommended.

I started playing July 31 2020. I officially beat the game on easy mode (random nodes and easy multipliers) March 2026. I've played over 2000 hours. It fulfills my need for problem solving in a practical setting. It's not everyone's favorite game, but it most certainly is mine.

I need a better computer to fully enjoy this game. My 2012 toaster has been playing it all this time. Gets kinda laggy/crashy when my factory gets bigger. I was drawn in with the 600 Meter personal cannon video by by Let's Game It Out. I watched and rewatched that video, and I finally caved. I'm glad I did.

No other game has made me pull out a calculator (before I knew there was one in-game) and make a spreadsheet to organize and math-out plans.
Minecraft was the only comparison of note with freedom to build and crafting to produce in-game products. But it's not block based like MInecrafe. It's entirely beautified and has creative freedom. The only limits are your patience and inventiveness, and computer processing power, and the edges of the map.

I'm posting this review NOW because 1.2 has officially released, and I feel the game is finally at a point of completeness that would have the broadest appeal to gamers. I don't know what else the devs have up their sleeves, but I'm happy where it is right now. The devs are likely among the best community managing teams for any game that has been in development up to this point. Not sure if anyone can top it, but Grounded might be a close second and Factorio could be 3rd.

It's not a perfect game. There's glitches. There's holes. There's incomplete gaps tucked away on the map that you can only fall in by being there in that spot. The game goal feels a bit hollow, and the time it takes to accomplish things can make it really draggy (late game in particular).

But it really IS satisfying to play. To work out the details. To plan, to organize, to collect, to build. Adding friends can take it up a notch. I like that it tests my patience, I'm quite proud of my level of patience.
At some point with a much better computer, I will try hard mode (really grindy).

I liked this game so much, I bought 7 more copies for friends. They didn't all have the same love for it that I did, but it's no matter, because I love it just the same.
I may never find another game that pulls me in like this one, but that's okay, because this one is enough for me.

Oh, and sci-fi. Love sci-fi themed games/movies/books. I'm "FarAndAwayFutures" for a reason. This game hits that sweet spot for me.

Okay, well, I'm gonna get back to my factory now.
Try it out, you might like it too.

After spending over 120 hours in Satisfactory, I can confidently say this game is an absolute masterpiece for factory and automation enthusiasts. The depth of its crafting systems, combined with the open-world exploration, makes every session feel rewarding. I love how it balances complexity and creativity—you can start with a simple conveyor belt and eventually build sprawling, intricate factories that feel alive.

The visuals and soundtrack are surprisingly immersive, and the thrill of optimizing production lines keeps you engaged for hours on end. Multiplayer adds another layer of fun, letting friends collaborate and compete on massive projects. Whether you’re a seasoned builder or new to automation games, Satisfactory offers a perfect mix of strategy, problem-solving, and satisfaction.

120 hours in, and I’m still discovering new ways to streamline, explore, and innovate. Highly recommended! ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐

Bought the game when 1.0 launched and it ran surprisingly well on my system. High settings, smooth gameplay, no major issues. I shelved it for later, expecting to come back to an even better game.

[b] Then v1.2 happened. [/b]

Came back after the 1.2 update to actually start playing it properly, and the performance is noticeably worse. Frequent stuttering, had to lower settings, and the game doesn't look nearly as good anymore. The old DLSS bug where Alt+Tab causes the game to render at a lower resolution is still there too, making everything blurry until you restart.

What finally made me quit wasn't even the stuttering. It was the [b]temperatures.[/b] This game absolutely cooks my system now. I genuinely wasn't comfortable running it any longer because of how hot everything was getting.
[i] Another minute of gameplay and my PC would've qualified as a Tier 1 Smelter. [/i]

Maybe I'll revisit it later if performance improves. For now, I'm moving on and will probably check out [b] StarRupture [/b] instead. (looks more fun than this slate game) Might update this review after comparing the two.
